concise:
Ayah 1–6 · linked video: 01. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 1-6)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 1-6

Introduction to Surah Aal-E-Imran

By the grace of Allah ﷻ, we begin our study of Surah Aal-E-Imran, a companion surah to Al-Baqarah. The parallels between these two surahs are remarkable and will become increasingly evident as we progress through this commentary.

Both surahs are Madani, revealed after the migration to Madinah, and both begin with the mysterious letters Alif Lam Mim. Like Al-Baqarah, this surah is divided into two distinct halves, with the first half containing three subdivisions. Where the first half of Al-Baqarah dedicated substantial space to discussing the Children of Israel and the Jews, the first half of Aal-E-Imran focuses primarily on Christians.

A significant portion of this surah was revealed in the ninth year after Hijrah, when a delegation of Christian monks and ministers from Najran, a town near Yemen, came to visit the Prophet ﷺ. They had heard of his claim to prophethood and stayed in Madinah for some time to engage in discourse with the Muslims. The courtesy extended to them remains instructive for believers today. Despite some having no intention of accepting Islam and coming primarily to debate, they were housed in Masjid An-Nabawi itself. This hospitality exemplifies the Islamic approach to interfaith dialogue.

Both surahs contain battle commentary. Al-Baqarah provided pre-battle guidance before Badr, while Aal-E-Imran offers post-battle reflection after Uhud. These ayahs address matters of discipline within the Muslim ranks and extend those lessons to the entire community.

Several themes introduced at the end of Al-Baqarah are explored more deeply in Aal-E-Imran. The concept of Ulul Albab (people of understanding) mentioned briefly before is examined comprehensively here. The beautiful supplication at the end of Al-Baqarah (“Our Lord, do not punish us if we forget or make a mistake…”) finds its parallel in another powerful dua near the end of this surah. Just as Al-Baqarah concluded with “and give us victory over the disbelieving people,” this surah ends with guidance on steadfastness and resistance.

Where Al-Baqarah dealt extensively with matters of the heart—taqwa (consciousness of Allah ﷻ), iman (faith), the hardening and softening of hearts—Aal-E-Imran emphasizes Islam as the outward manifestation of that inner faith. While iman represents the internal state, Islam represents its external expression, and this term appears repeatedly throughout this surah.

Al-Baqarah mentioned previous revelations only in passing, but Aal-E-Imran names them explicitly, setting the stage for the detailed discourse with the People of the Book that follows.

Ayah 1

الٓمٓ

Alif Lam Mim. This surah begins with the same mysterious letters as Al-Baqarah, immediately establishing a connection between these two comprehensive Madani revelations.

Ayah 2

ٱللَّهُ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْحَىُّ ٱلْقَيُّومُ

Allah ﷻ—there is no deity worthy of worship, obedience or love in any way, shape or form, except Him—Al-Hayy (the Ever-Living), Al-Qayyum (the Sustainer of all existence).

This declaration of pure monotheism serves as the foundation for everything that follows. Al-Hayy means the Ultimate Source of Life, the Absolutely Living from whom all life derives. Al-Qayyum describes the One who maintains, sustains and takes care of all existence. These two magnificent names together capture Allah’s ﷻ transcendent life and His intimate involvement with His creation.

Ayah 3

نَزَّلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ بِٱلْحَقِّ مُصَدِّقًا لِّمَا بَيْنَ يَدَيْهِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةَ وَٱلْإِنجِيلَ

He sent down upon you the Book with truth, confirming what came before it. And He sent down the Torah and the Gospel.

The choice of words here is profound. Nazzala (sent down over stages) differs from anzala (sent down at once). The intensive form nazzala emphasizes that the Quran was revealed gradually, stage by stage, over twenty-three years. This differs from the Torah and Gospel, which were revealed in their entirety.

The use of ’alayka (upon you) rather than ilayka (to you) is equally significant. Ila suggests privilege—it came to you as a gift. ’Ala, however, implies responsibility—this revelation was placed upon you as a burden and trust. The Quran came to the Messenger ﷺ as both honor and obligation.

Bil-haqq (with truth) has multiple dimensions. The Book came through a truthful messenger, it came with truth in its content, and it came with purpose. This revelation is not merely information to be absorbed; it demands submission and action.

Musaddiqan lima bayna yadayhi (confirming what is before it) indicates that the Quran validates the previous revelations. It stands as a witness to the original truth contained in the Torah and the Gospel, before they were altered.

The mention of both Torah and Gospel is deliberate. While Jews reject the Gospel and do not accept Isa (AS) as a messenger, Christians accept both revelations. This subtle reference prepares us for the extended dialogue with Christians that will follow in this surah.

Ayah 4

مِن قَبْلُ هُدًى لِّلنَّاسِ وَأَنزَلَ ٱلْفُرْقَانَ ۗ إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ شَدِيدٌ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَزِيزٌ ذُو ٱنتِقَامٍ

Before this, as guidance for the people. And He sent down the Criterion. Indeed, those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah ﷻ will have a severe punishment, and Allah ﷻ is Exalted in Might, the Owner of Retribution.

Min qablu (much before) connects to the previous ayah, emphasizing that the Torah and Gospel were sent down long ago—the Gospel approximately six centuries before the Quran, and the Torah over a millennium before that. Both came as hudan lin-nas (guidance for humanity).

Al-Furqan (the Criterion) is one of the most powerful names for the Quran. While all previous revelations provided guidance, the Quran is the ultimate criterion that distinguishes truth from falsehood, right from wrong, guidance from misguidance, with unparalleled clarity. Notice that Allah ﷻ uses anzala (sent down at once) for Al-Furqan. Though the Quran was revealed gradually over time (nazzala), its function as a criterion was complete from the very first revelation. From the first day, anyone could distinguish between the one following truth and the one who wasn’t, without needing the entire Quran to have been revealed.

The warning that follows is severe but specific: Inna alladhina kafaru bi-ayatillah (Indeed, those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah ﷻ). The term kafir in the Quran carries a precise meaning that we must understand carefully. It describes those who were exposed to the message of Islam in the clearest possible form—through the best of all messengers, the Prophet ﷺ himself, in language they perfectly understood, in the best possible environment and circumstances—and despite all this, deliberately rejected and actively opposed it.

The Quran does not use kafir indiscriminately for anyone who isn’t Muslim. It specifically refers to those who encountered the truth in its purest form and consciously chose to hide it, reject it and fight against it. This distinction is crucial. A person who has never been properly exposed to Islam, or who lives in an environment where Islam has been misrepresented, cannot be labeled with this term. Muslims must be careful not to use this Quranic terminology loosely for neighbors, colleagues or others who simply aren’t Muslim. Such people may be non-Muslims, but they are not necessarily the kafirun described in these severe warnings.

Lahum ’adhabun shadid (for them is a severe punishment)—this punishment is exclusively for those who knowingly rejected Allah’s ﷻ clear signs after full exposure to the truth.

WAllahu ’Azizun dhun-tiqam (And Allah ﷻ is Exalted in Might, Owner of Retribution). Al-’Aziz means the One with ultimate authority and power. Dhun-tiqam means the Possessor of the ability to take retribution. These people didn’t merely disbelieve; they insulted Allah’s ﷻ messenger, attacked him, tried to kill him, and subjected him to a decade of abuse and humiliation. This is the same messenger whom Allah ﷻ honors so greatly that He becomes displeased when Muslims call him by his first name without adding his title. Allah ﷻ witnessed ten years of insults directed at His beloved ﷺ, and He will exact retribution for every offense against him.

Ayah 5

إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يَخْفَىٰ عَلَيْهِ شَىْءٌ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ

Indeed, Allah ﷻ—nothing is hidden from Him in the earth nor in the heaven.

The grammatical structure emphasizes that nothing whatsoever escapes Allah’s ﷻ knowledge. The use of ’alayhi (upon Him) rather than minhu (from Him) intensifies the meaning—it’s not that things are hidden somewhere else; they cannot be hidden from Him at all. Every deed, every word, every intention, whether on earth or in the heavens, is completely exposed before His knowledge. No secret plots, no hidden conspiracies, no concealed hatred or love exists outside His awareness.

Ayah 6

هُوَ ٱلَّذِى يُصَوِّرُكُمْ فِى ٱلْأَرْحَامِ كَيْفَ يَشَآءُ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

He is the One who fashions you in the wombs however He wills. There is no deity except Him, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.

One manifestation of Allah’s ﷻ perfect knowledge is His fashioning of human beings. Yusawwirukum means He molds you, shapes you, designs you with precise detail. Fil-arham (in the wombs)—in that hidden place where no eye can see, He crafts each person with infinite care. Kayfa yasha’ (however He wills)—tall or short, light or dark, blue eyes or brown, with certain features and characteristics. Every aspect of your physical form was determined by His will before you drew your first breath.

This ayah addresses a fundamental human tendency: dissatisfaction with one’s appearance and qualities. Why am I so short? Why am I not more attractive? Why don’t I have the features I desire? When we complain about how we look, we are complaining about what Allah ﷻ has made. When we spend our lives wishing we were different, comparing ourselves to others, envying their appearance or abilities, we reject Allah’s ﷻ choice in how He fashioned us.

The next surah will expand on this theme, commanding us not to wish for what others have been given. Don’t live your life according to the standards of commercials and social comparison. Don’t measure yourself constantly against others. Instead, be grateful for how Allah ﷻ has molded you.

La ilaha illa huwa (there is no deity except Him)—this declaration of pure monotheism gains deeper meaning in context. The One who formed you has absolute authority over His creation. You cannot question His choices.

Al-’Aziz (the Exalted in Might)—He has complete authority to fashion you however He wills, and you possess no power to challenge that decision.

Al-Hakim (the Wise)—whatever He made you, however He shaped you, it was done with perfect wisdom. When we ask “why?” or “why not?” we question Allah’s ﷻ wisdom in our creation. We have no authority to question it, and we must trust that every aspect of how we were made serves a wise purpose in His grand design.

Ayah 7–13 · linked video: 02. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 7-13)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayah 7-13

Ayah 7

هُوَ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنزَلَ عَلَيْكَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ مِنْهُ ءَايَـٰتٌ مُّحْكَمَـٰتٌ هُنَّ أُمُّ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَأُخَرُ مُتَشَـٰبِهَـٰتٌ ۖ فَأَمَّا ٱلَّذِينَ فِى قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ فَيَتَّبِعُونَ مَا تَشَـٰبَهَ مِنْهُ ٱبْتِغَآءَ ٱلْفِتْنَةِ وَٱبْتِغَآءَ تَأْوِيلِهِۦ ۗ وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَٱلرَّٰسِخُونَ فِى ٱلْعِلْمِ يَقُولُونَ ءَامَنَّا بِهِۦ كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا ۗ وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّآ أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْأَلْبَـٰبِ

This ayah connects to one of the final conversations from Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ states that no person is burdened with something they cannot handle. He knows exactly what we can handle because He is the One who molded us in our mothers’ wombs. Our potentials, strengths and weaknesses were all determined there.

He is the One who sent upon you the Book - Allah ﷻ begins by emphasizing His exclusive authority in revealing this scripture to the Prophet ﷺ.

Among them are ayat that are muhkamat - The word مُحْكَمَات comes from حُكْم, which carries multiple interconnected meanings: firmness, rulings, governance and wisdom. These ayat possess wisdom that becomes obvious upon reflection, and they contain clear rulings and injunctions. Notice that Allah ﷻ uses the pronoun هُنَّ (“they” feminine) rather than the standard pronoun for non-living things. This grammatical choice magnifies the glory of these ayat, especially those containing rulings and injunctions.

They are the mother of the Book - The word أُمّ literally means “mother” but here signifies essence or foundation. These muhkamat ayat represent the essence and crux of the Book, the practical results of revelation. Notice Allah ﷻ uses the word كِتَاب (Book) here rather than Quran. Kitab is particularly associated with law, with the Sharia of Islam. The essence of Islamic law is found in the ayat al-muhkamat.

All the hadith of the Prophet ﷺ that deal with matters of Sharia are supplements to and authentic explanations of the ayat that give injunctions. For example, Allah ﷻ gives us a general injunction: “Do not marry polytheist women until they become believers.” But the detailed specifics of marriage—the Sunnah of marriage, the various rulings—all of that comes from the Sharia of Muhammad ﷺ under that one ayah. When Allah ﷻ says أَقِيمُوا الصَّلَاةَ (establish the prayer), all the chapters of wudu, all the chapters of salah, all the detailed rulings we study in fiqh and Sharia from the practices of the Prophet ﷺ fall under that one ayah. This is what is meant by “the mother of the Book.”

And others that are mutashabihat - These are ayat that are ambiguous or unclear in their precise meaning. The word مُتَشَابِهَات also means they are very similar to each other, which can create confusion. The root ش-ب-ه relates to vagueness and similarity. The Jews said in Surah Al-Baqarah, “The cow is vague to us (تَشَابَهَ عَلَيْنَا)” - meaning it could be any cow, we don’t understand clearly. These ayat may be confusing to the human mind, like the disconnected letters at the beginning of surahs (الم، كهيعص). We may try to figure them out, but ultimately we don’t know their precise meaning.

This is part of Allah’s wisdom—He left ayat inside this revelation that we may not be able to fully understand.

As for those who have deviation in their hearts - Allah ﷻ now describes a particular group: الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ (those in whose hearts is deviation). What is this deviation?

They pursue what is vague in it - They become obsessed with the mutashabihat. They want to know what every ambiguous part means. “What does كهيعص mean? Until I find out what it means, I won’t move forward.” There is so much to learn in the religion, but they want to go after the vague things exclusively.

This is actually a fascinating form of hypocrisy. The demands of Islam require very practical changes in life—serious changes in financial dealings, social interactions and ethical behavior. But there are people who don’t want to make changes in their lives while still wanting to distract from this fact. So they make these ambiguous subjects their pet projects. Every time you try to bring up Islam’s practical requirements—“What about your very clearly haram business practices?”—they respond, “No, no, but the seven heavens—I need to understand that first. What are the layers of Jannah? What does it mean that Allah ﷻ rose to the throne?”

These kinds of philosophical, abstract conversations often happen among friends who are not very religious. When they do talk about Islam, they talk about that kind of material. Why? Because it gives the impression that they think about Islam and keeps everyone wrapped up in these discussions. The practical element of the Book never comes up.

Seeking fitnah - They pursue the mutashabihat ابْتِغَاءَ الْفِتْنَةِ (in pursuit of fitnah). They put themselves and others in trial. “How can this be revelation when these letters don’t even make sense? They don’t have any meaning!” But just because you don’t understand something doesn’t mean it has no meaning. The Quran is described as الْقُرْآنِ الْحَكِيمِ (the Wise Quran), full of wisdom. That attitude itself becomes a source of fitnah for the person and for society.

And seeking its creative interpretation - ابْتِغَاءَ تَأْوِيلِهِ (seeking its ta’wil) - they want to come up with creative interpretations so they can say, “Look, I figured something out that nobody figured out before. I’m a genuine interpreter of the Quran.” In order to be recognized as creative interpreters, they pursue these ambiguous matters.

And no one knows its true interpretation except Allah - وَمَا يَعْلَمُ تَأْوِيلَهُ إِلَّا اللَّهُ. Get it through your heads: no one knows certain things. Their true interpretation belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

And those firmly rooted in knowledge - الرَّاسِخُونَ - وَالرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْم comes from راسخ, which means deeply rooted, absorbed. These are people who don’t just take in knowledge—they’ve absorbed it, internalized it, consumed it. It has become part of them. This absorption comes with a certain attitude.

What is their attitude? They say, آمَنَّا بِهِ (we believe in it). كُلٌّ مِّنْ عِندِ رَبِّنَا (All of it is from our Master). Notice the emphasis: from our رَبّ (Master), especially.

Let’s understand this attitude. How can someone deeply rooted in knowledge not be concerned about figuring everything out? The real question to ask is whether or not this is revelation. The fundamental question at the heart of our religion is: Is this revelation from God or not? When that question is thoroughly answered for you and you’re convinced this is in fact revelation, then you no longer look for nitty-gritty things where if you don’t figure them out, you might abandon the religion.

When that central question hasn’t been answered—that this is the word of Allah ﷻ—then you’ll find yourself saying, “This doesn’t make sense, so I don’t follow. That doesn’t make sense, so I don’t follow.” You’ll find microscopic criticisms or points of interest. These can only be of interest to you if that central question hasn’t been answered.

But when that is cleared, when that’s squared away, then whether you understand or not, you know it’s from your Master. Whether you comprehend everything becomes irrelevant. You ask Allah ﷻ to open the doors of wisdom, that’s it. And you make sure you’re not asking Allah ﷻ to help you understand everything—you’re asking Him not to make you of those who ask these inappropriate questions, which are themselves an indication of what’s going on in their hearts.

Allah ﷻ didn’t just say people ask certain questions. He said الَّذِينَ فِي قُلُوبِهِمْ زَيْغٌ—those who have deviation in their hearts ask these things. Those who are truly mature realize these are irrelevant questions, and therefore they will never ask them. They ask Allah ﷻ to never become like those who ask such questions. That prayer will come in the next ayah.

And none will be reminded except those of understanding - وَمَا يَذَّكَّرُ إِلَّا أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ. Remember this phrase from Surah Al-Baqarah? Allah ﷻ introduced أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ but didn’t give us details. Now He brings them up again.

أُولُو الْأَلْبَابِ are the people of pure minds. لُبّ is a sound mind, purified from vanities. Let’s first discuss عَقْل (intellect), then discuss the highest form of intellect, which is لُبّ.

عَقْل literally means to tie something up. It represents control over your emotions and the ability to think clearly. If you have a bias—of anger, fear, love, lust, greed, any kind of emotional bias that clouds your judgment—then you are not exercising your ’aql. You’re not exercising your intellect. It is only when you put your emotions in check, when you put your biases aside and think about something, that you are engaged in the use of ’aql.

The opposite of ’aql is جَهْل (ignorance). جَهْل is not just ignorance—it is an inability to control and restrain yourself. Someone with jahl, when something comes into their head, they just say it. They feel like eating something, they just eat it. They want to do something, they just do it. This is jahl.

The person of ’aql restrains himself before passing judgment, before opening his mouth, before taking action. The jahil doesn’t. That’s the fundamental difference between ’aql and jahl.

Now that you’ve put your emotions to the side, you’re able to think clearly about things. You have clarity of thought. It’s simple: when you’re too angry, you can’t think clearly. When you’re too sad, you can’t think clearly. When you’re preoccupied, you can’t think clearly. To have a clear mind, you really have to move your emotions aside.

Once you do think clearly, Allah ﷻ talks about the highest form: the people who possess لُبّ .لُبّ literally means the essence of something. It is Allah ﷻ saying that this ability to think, ponder and reflect—this ability to engage in deep thought—is the essence of what makes a human being.

The Prophet ﷺ described deep reflection (تَفَكُّر) as more rewarding than people who pray the entire night. Deep reflection. Thought. Because this is a gift from Allah ﷻ.

None will make an effort to remember except people of pure minds - حَلِيصٌ مِنَ السَّفْسَاهِ (pure from vanities). A mind that is free from vanities. A mind that is not preoccupied with junk, with filth, with unnecessary distractions—endless sports statistics, movie trivia, award ceremonies, character details, video game walkthroughs. You can have some of that, but if your mind is full of that garbage, you don’t have a clean mind. You’re not truly able to remember Allah ﷻ.

You’ll see this practically. When you’re engaged in vanity a lot and then try to remember Allah ﷻ, your heart immediately says, “Go back to the movie. Finish this quickly, then go back to the movie.” Your heart is not truly remembering Allah ﷻ. But people who have sound minds—their hearts incline toward Allah ﷻ. They want to remember Him more.

A Beautiful Secret in This Ayah

Notice that it began with people who have a lot of knowledge: الرَّاسِخُونَ فِي الْعِلْمِ (those deeply rooted in knowledge). Yet Allah ﷻ mentions ذِكْر (remembrance). Remembrance happens in the heart. As Allah ﷻ says elsewhere, “By the remembrance of Allah do hearts find tranquility.”

So Allah ﷻ is talking about people who have deep knowledge, and that knowledge makes them put extra effort into remembering Allah ﷻ, keeping their hearts clean. If you’re worried about your knowledge, you ask Allah ﷻ to increase you in understanding. But once you make the effort, it takes real effort to make your heart clean, to truly remember Allah ﷻ.

This leads directly to the next ayah.

Ayah 8

رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً ۚ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلْوَهَّابُ

Our Master, do not deviate our hearts - رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا. SubhanAllah, look at the connection. The knowledge is there, but when knowledge comes, with it comes a concern for one’s heart. It’s as if the more you know, the more you realize you’re in greater danger.

For many of us, increasing knowledge automatically means increasing legitimacy: “I’ve reached a certain level, I have a certain status, I’m at a certain level of iman.” No. Those who are deeply rooted in knowledge are the most worried about their hearts. لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا - don’t deviate our hearts.

Already mentioned were those who had deviation in their hearts and pursued vain intellectual pursuits that don’t benefit them in religion. These people who have real knowledge know not to go into those tangents. They know not to write books about “how many angels can fit on the tip of a finger” (which has actually been done, by the way, in Islamic literature). They don’t care about that material. They have more important concerns: لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا.

After You have guided us - بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا. The word إِذْ here emphasizes “after the fact that” You have guided us.

Another teaching emerges here: You want to stay clean and sound-minded? Keep your focus on the Quran. It will teach you how to think and not go off into tangents. Allah ﷻ prioritizes for you what you should think about more. You want to follow a text? Here’s the text.

And gift us from especially You a mercy - هِبَة .وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً is a gift, distinct from عَطَاء (giving). آتِنَا means “give us,” but هَبْ لَنَا means “gift us.” Give us a gift especially from You—مِن لَّدُنكَ. What should that gift be? A special mercy that comes directly from You.

Indeed, You are the Giver of gifts - إِنَّكَ أَنتَ الْوَهَّابُ. You are no doubt exclusively, repeatedly the Giver of gifts. You keep on giving gifts. You keep on granting.

Ayah 9

رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ جَامِعُ ٱلنَّاسِ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ ۚ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ ٱلْمِيعَادَ

Our Master, indeed You are the Gatherer of people - رَبَّنَا إِنَّكَ جَامِعُ النَّاسِ. For a day that has no doubt in it whatsoever - لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ.

In the previous surah, Allah ﷻ said the Book has no doubt in it (لَا رَيْبَ فِيهِ). Now the believer, the one with knowledge, says the Day has no doubt in it.

Certainly Allah does not turn back on His promise -مِيعَاد .إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعَادَ carries three meanings: the time of the promise, the place of the promise and the promise itself.

The Day of Judgment will come at a particular time. Allah ﷻ promises that time, and it will not be postponed, brought forward or delayed. People will be gathered to a particular place, and that location will not change—there will be no “gate change” at the last minute. And the promise itself—Allah ﷻ will not violate it in any way. We will learn this concept again at the end of this surah, where the beginning ties to the end.

Ayah 10

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ لَن تُغْنِىَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَٰلُهُمْ وَلَآ أَوْلَـٰدُهُم مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ شَيْـًٔا ۖ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ ٱلنَّارِ

Those who disbelieve—their wealth will not benefit them -لَن .إِنَّ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا لَن تُغْنِيَ عَنْهُمْ أَمْوَالُهُمْ indicates this will never happen. Their monies are not going to be of any benefit to them.

Nor their children - وَلَا أَوْلَادُهُم. Their assets and their children will not benefit them.

Against Allah in any way, shape or form - مِنَ اللَّهِ شَيْئًا. Not even a little bit.

Some scholars say this ayah came when the Muslims were returning from Badr as victors. When they were coming back, the Jews of Medina looked at them and said, “Oh, you just fought some boys from Makkah. When you deal with us, you’ll see what it’s about.” They trash-talked, saying, “You haven’t had real battles yet. The Makkans that were sent to fight you—that’s not a big deal. The next fight, you’ll see.”

Allah ﷻ responds: No. Actually, their monies and their assets will not be able to benefit them at all.

And they are the fuel of the Fire - وَأُولَٰئِكَ هُمْ وَقُودُ النَّارِ.

Ayah 11

كَدَأْبِ ءَالِ فِرْعَوْنَ وَٱلَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ ۚ كَذَّبُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَأَخَذَهُمُ ٱللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ شَدِيدُ ٱلْعِقَابِ

Like the legacy left behind by the family of Pharaoh - كَدَأْبِ آلِ فِرْعَوْنَ. Like the outcome, the pattern of the people of Pharaoh.

And those who came much before them - وَالَّذِينَ مِن قَبْلِهِمْ. The Pharaohs were like a dynasty. Fir’awn was not really a name—it was a title. So there were many Fir’awns in the past, like the many Nimrods. There were many dynasties that came before them as well.

They all did the same crime - كَذَّبُوا بِآيَاتِنَا. They lied against Our miraculous signs, against Our revelation.

So Allah seized them because of their sins - فَأَخَذَهُمُ اللَّهُ بِذُنُوبِهِمْ.

And Allah is intense in taking revenge - وَاللَّهُ شَدِيدُ الْعِقَابِ.

Ayah 12

قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ سَتُغْلَبُونَ وَتُحْشَرُونَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ ۚ وَبِئْسَ ٱلْمِهَادُ

Tell those who disbelieved: You will be dominated - قُل لِّلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا سَتُغْلَبُونَ.

And you will be herded into Hellfire - وَتُحْشَرُونَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ. Notice the word choice. Allah ﷻ said about Himself in the previous ayah: إِنَّكَ جَامِعُ النَّاسِ (You are the Gatherer of people). جَمْع is when you gather people. But here we don’t read “you will be gathered” (تُجْمَعُونَ). We read تُحْشَرُونَ.

حَشْر is when you herd animals. Generally, you gather people with jam’, but gathering animals is hashr. When the Muslims were told to speak to the disbelievers, they were to say: You will be herded like animals on Judgment Day. تُحْشَرُونَ إِلَىٰ جَهَنَّمَ.

And what a horrible cradle that is -مِهَاد .وَبِئْسَ الْمِهَادُ is the cradle of a mother. What a horrible cradle. Just as a mother squeezes the baby in the cradle, Hellfire squeezes its prisoners.

Ayah 13

قَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ ءَايَةٌ فِى فِئَتَيْنِ ٱلْتَقَتَا ۖ فِئَةٌ تُقَـٰتِلُ فِى سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَأُخْرَىٰ كَافِرَةٌ يَرَوْنَهُم مِّثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْىَ ٱلْعَيْنِ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِۦ مَن يَشَآءُ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً لِّأُو۟لِى ٱلْأَبْصَـٰرِ

Already there was a miraculous sign for you - قَدْ كَانَ لَكُمْ آيَةٌ. This is the Battle of Badr. Allah ﷻ is making post-battle commentary. Remember there was pre-battle commentary, and now there’s post-battle commentary.

In the story of two groups that clashed with each other - فِي فِئَتَيْنِ الْتَقَتَا. One group fighting in the path of Allah - فِئَةٌ تُقَاتِلُ فِي سَبِيلِ اللَّهِ. And another that disbelieves - وَأُخْرَىٰ كَافِرَةٌ.

They would see them as double what they actually were with their eyes - يَرَوْنَهُم مِّثْلَيْهِمْ رَأْيَ الْعَيْنِ. This phrase has been interpreted multiple ways.

Some say the Muslims saw the enemy armies and realized they were at least double their number—even though the enemy was actually three times their size, the Muslims saw them as at least double. That’s what the Muslims perceived.

Others say that Allah ﷻ made it seem to the disbelievers that even though they saw the Muslims as only a third of their forces, Allah ﷻ made the Muslims look like they were twice their actual number. Allah ﷻ instilled that fear into the hearts of the enemy when they saw the Muslims on the battlefield.

And Allah aids with His help whoever He wills - وَاللَّهُ يُؤَيِّدُ بِنَصْرِهِ مَن يَشَاءُ. He gives powerful aid to whoever He wants. In other words, victory doesn’t come to anyone except by Allah’s help.

No doubt about it, in all of that there truly is a lesson - إِنَّ فِي ذَٰلِكَ لَعِبْرَةً. A warning.

For people who possess eyes - لِّأُولِي الْأَبْصَارِ. For people who possess sight, meaning they can see reality for what it is. Allah ﷻ will help whoever He wants.

Allah ﷻ is dropping a hint to the Jews who were talking trash: If you really have eyes, Allah ﷻ brings victory no matter what the odds. If the Muslims can take on the Quraysh, they can take on you. You better watch out.

The next ayah begins a universal teaching in the Quran about the desires of people, the desires of humanity. It’s mentioned right after Allah ﷻ talks about fighting in the path of Allah. When someone goes to fight in the path of Allah, they have left everything behind—family, money, future goals. They’re ready to die. Allah ﷻ praises and honors that attitude of those who fought in His path. But even they have desires. It’s not like they don’t want to have a family or don’t want to have money or don’t enjoy looking at a beautiful, well-bred horse. They enjoy it too. Despite these temptations being natural for all of humanity, they still went and fought in the path of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 14–16 · linked video: 03. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 14-16)

PRIMARY_AYAH: 14

Ayah 14

زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ وَٱلْبَنِينَ وَٱلْقَنَـٰطِيرِ ٱلْمُقَنطَرَةِ مِنَ ٱلذَّهَبِ وَٱلْفِضَّةِ وَٱلْخَيْلِ ٱلْمُسَوَّمَةِ وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ وَٱلْحَرْثِ ۗ ذَٰلِكَ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا ۖ وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلْمَـَٔابِ

This ayah speaks directly about desires that capture the hearts of men in particular. Not all men may experience every one of these desires, and certainly there are men whose priorities differ. Yet overwhelmingly, this passage identifies what men get caught up with, what occupies their thoughts and drives their choices. Interestingly, Allah ﷻ does not provide a similar explicit list for women because their desires are far more complex and varied, differing significantly from person to person.

Allah ﷻ begins with زُيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ حُبُّ ٱلشَّهَوَٰتِ - “Beautified for people is the love of desires.” The passive form here raises an important question: who beautified these things? The ayah doesn’t specify, leaving open the possibility that this beautification comes from multiple sources, including our own nature, societal influences and the whispers of Shaytan.

The first item on this list is مِنَ ٱلنِّسَآءِ - desires from women. The Prophet ﷺ said that the worst fitna he feared for the men of his Ummah after his passing was women. This is not a condemnation of women themselves but a recognition of how powerful this attraction can be. In today’s world, this challenge has intensified dramatically. Images and content are everywhere - on mobile devices, laptops, in the workplace, in classrooms, on every screen we encounter. The temptation saturates our environment through language, music, television and social media.

The question becomes: can we maintain the standards Allah ﷻ has given us for guarding our gaze and protecting our modesty? When the environment constantly pushes against these boundaries, standards begin to drop incrementally. We see this manifesting in how our youth dress, what they listen to and watch, and even in their language. As the principle states, every container only pours out what it contains. If inappropriate language flows freely from the tongue, it reveals what’s occupying the mind.

Next comes وَٱلْبَنِينَ - sons. Though the word Banin technically can include both sons and daughters, there’s a particular emphasis here on the pride fathers take in their sons. You might be young now and think you don’t care about having children. But as men age and reach their forties and fifties, they find themselves reflecting on their sons with profound pride. A father may never tell his son directly that he’s proud of him, but he’ll tell everyone else. “My son accomplished this, my son did that.” The son becomes a source of honor and joy, even when the father struggles to express it directly to him.

The ayah continues with وَٱلْقَنَـٰطِيرِ ٱلْمُقَنطَرَةِ مِنَ ٱلذَّهَبِ وَٱلْفِضَّةِ - “heaped-up piles of gold and silver.” Notice the emphasis: not just piles, but piles that have been repeatedly added to. القناطير refers to large quantities, and المقنطرة means piled upon. This describes the human obsession with accumulating wealth. Today we don’t literally pile gold and silver, but we have bank accounts where we make deposits and then check the balance repeatedly. Five minutes later, we check again. The numbers bring a strange tranquility, even as the obsession with savings consumes our thoughts.

For married men with responsibilities, the mind becomes like a running Excel spreadsheet: How much are we saving? Where is this money going? How will we pay off the house? What about college bills, Islamic school tuition, gas, groceries? The calculations never stop. Yet the pile is never enough. You add to it, then add more, yet contentment remains elusive.

Then Allah ﷻ mentions وَٱلْخَيْلِ ٱلْمُسَوَّمَةِ - branded or distinguished horses. Horses represent speed, power and status. It’s remarkable that to this day, when we measure a car’s engine, we speak of “horsepower.” Whether it’s a Ferrari, a Mustang or any luxury vehicle, the horse remains the symbol of masculine pride and achievement. This ancient desire has simply transferred to modern machinery.

وَٱلْأَنْعَـٰمِ - cattle or livestock - represents the means of making money. For farmers, cattle were their productive assets. Today, your “cattle” might be your computer, office space, storefront, warehouse or business infrastructure. These are the assets you invest in that generate income. People take tremendous pride in these: “This is my office, my business. We have this many employees, we handle projects worth millions.” The conversation at business class airports revolves around sales, mergers and acquisitions. Even during downtime, this becomes the primary topic because it has consumed their entire identity.

Yet there’s a poignant reality here. Many high-powered executives reach their fifties and sixties having sacrificed family for career. Some then adopt pets as substitutes for children, or finally decide to adopt a child when they realize what they’ve missed. Others walk the streets of major cities, high-earning professionals picking up after their dogs, having traded human relationships for career achievements.

Finally, وَٱلْحَرْثِ - the harvest or crop - represents the ultimate goal: the return on investment. On a farm, the cattle help you reach the crop, but the crop is what you really love because that’s the tangible result. In modern terms, this is the bonus check, the successful sale, the profit margin. Nobody frowns when making a deposit at the bank. We anxiously wait for checks to clear, then breathe with relief when they do. This is what people orient their entire lives around.

Then comes the devastating truth: ذَٰلِكَ مَتَـٰعُ ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا - “That is the enjoyment of worldly life.” The word متاع refers to something temporary, something you use but that never fully satisfies. By choosing this particular word, Allah ﷻ tells us that no matter how much you pursue these things, they will never bring lasting satisfaction. You’ll get the car you dreamed about, and there will always be a nicer car. You’ll have tremendous savings, yet you’ll always feel it could be more. You’ll have accomplished children, yet you’ll think they could have done better. The nature of these worldly pursuits is that they cannot fulfill the human soul.

The ayah concludes with a powerful contrast: وَٱللَّهُ عِندَهُۥ حُسْنُ ٱلْمَـَٔابِ - “And Allah has with Him the best return.” Everything in this world provides temporary pleasure at best. But with Allah ﷻ alone, exclusively in His possession, is the ultimate return, the best possible destination.

Ayah 15

۞ قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ ۚ لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا وَأَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ

This lesson echoes what we learned in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ taught us that for the disbelievers, worldly life was beautified, and they mock the believers. But those who have taqwa will be elevated on the Day of Judgment. Notice that Allah ﷻ didn’t say “believers” will be on top - He said specifically those with taqwa, those who protected themselves from being hypnotized by worldly temptation.

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ: قُلْ أَؤُنَبِّئُكُم بِخَيْرٍ مِّن ذَٰلِكُمْ - “Say, shall I inform you of something better than all of that?” Better than what? Better than women, sons, wealth, horses, business assets and profitable returns. On one side sits everything the previous ayah listed; on the other side sits something incomparably superior.

لِلَّذِينَ ٱتَّقَوْا۟ عِندَ رَبِّهِمْ جَنَّـٰتٌ - “For those who protected themselves, with their Master are gardens.” Not just one garden, but multiple gardens - جَنَّـٰتٌ is plural. These are تَجْرِى مِن تَحْتِهَا ٱلْأَنْهَـٰرُ - gardens beneath which rivers flow. Imagine visiting the most beautiful place on earth - pristine beaches, majestic mountains, breathtaking landscapes. When you’re there, you desperately want to stay. But you can’t. It’s too expensive, time is limited, responsibilities call you back.

Allah ﷻ says His gardens are different: خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا - “abiding in them forever.” This isn’t a vacation where you spend two days appreciating beauty before returning to ordinary life. You stay. You simply stay in that beauty eternally.

وَأَزْوَٰجٌ مُّطَهَّرَةٌ - “and purified spouses.” But then comes something that transcends all physical pleasures: وَرِضْوَٰنٌ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ - “and pleasure from Allah.” This phrase represents the ultimate reward, something that trumps everything else mentioned. You will finally be at peace. You will not want more.

In this world, we never reach a point where we genuinely want nothing more. There’s always something we lack, something we desire. But Allah ﷻ promises something we’ve never experienced: complete contentment. رِضْوَٰن carries two meanings. First, Allah ﷻ will be pleased with you - He will express His contentment with your life’s work. Second, you will be content, fully satisfied in a way impossible in this world.

Think about how people live to make others proud. A child works to make their parents happy, an employee to please their boss, a student to make their teacher proud. When someone you respect says, “I’m proud of you,” it feels like life’s greatest accomplishment. Now imagine the CEO of a massive company walking directly to you among thousands of employees and saying, “I’m really proud of the work you did last week.” The sense of validation would be overwhelming.

In Jannah, you experience the joy of knowing Allah ﷻ is pleased with you. He might say, “Good work, My slave. I appreciate what you did that summer in 2011 when you were in college. I saw how you lowered your gaze when it was difficult. Good job. I’m proud of you.” Nothing can be higher than this. This is the ultimate reward - knowing we made Allah ﷻ proud.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ - “And Allah is Seeing of His servants.” بَصِير means He has you in full view, watching constantly to see how you perform so that He can be pleased with you. The ayah began by asking, “Should I inform you of something better?” Better than all worldly desires. Then it presents the choice: all those temporary pleasures on one side, Allah’s eternal pleasure on the other. Which do you prefer?

Ayah 16

ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ

Allah ﷻ now describes the characteristics of those slaves He watches constantly, those with whom He is pleased. Who are they?

ٱلَّذِينَ يَقُولُونَ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّنَآ ءَامَنَّا - “Those who say, ‘Our Master, indeed we have believed.’” They affirm without doubt: we truly believe in Your promise, we believe it’s coming, we have accepted everything You’ve revealed as truth.

فَٱغْفِرْ لَنَا ذُنُوبَنَا - “So forgive us our sins.” Because they believe, they ask Allah ﷻ to cover their mistakes, to conceal the things they’re embarrassed about. ذُنُوب comes from dhanab, the tail of an animal - things trailing behind us that we’re not proud of, that we wish to hide. They ask Allah ﷻ to cover these shameful errors.

وَقِنَا عَذَابَ ٱلنَّارِ - “And protect us from the punishment of the Fire.” Even though Allah ﷻ has offered these slaves beautiful gardens and His pleasure, they remain afraid of the Fire. They never feel their iman is sufficient. They never become complacent, always concerned they might be missing something. So they continuously ask Allah ﷻ to protect them from the Fire.

These are the qualities of Allah’s beloved servants in this world - but what specifically characterizes them? The next ayah will detail their attributes: الصَّابِرِينَ وَالصَّادِقِينَ وَالْقَانِتِينَ وَالْمُنفِقِينَ وَالْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحَارِ - “The patient, the truthful, the obedient, those who spend, and those who seek forgiveness before dawn.”

Notice the وَ (“and”) between each quality. This grammatical structure teaches us something profound. It could mean one person possesses all these qualities simultaneously - and Alhamdulillah, that’s possible. But the presence of “and” also indicates that these might be separate groups: some people excel particularly in patience, others in truthfulness, others in obedience, others in charity, others in pre-dawn prayers. Each quality represents a path to Allah’s pleasure, and all who sincerely pursue any of these paths are included in His mercy.

الصَّابِرِينَ - those who show perseverance and patience. والصَّادِقِينَ - those who are truthful. Perhaps someone has less patience but excels in honesty. Another might be working on truthfulness but possesses tremendous sabr. Each person has different strengths.

وَالْقَانِتِينَ - those who are subservient and obedient. قُنُوت differs from simple obedience; it means being ready and willing to obey instantly. Some children require repeated reminders: “Can you get me water? Did you hear me? Get the water!” But another child sees their mother looking tired and brings water before being asked. That’s qunoot - eager readiness to serve. These people don’t just obey Allah ﷻ; they’re positioned at the starting line, ready to spring forward at His slightest indication.

وَالْمُنفِقِينَ - those who spend in Allah’s cause. These aren’t people who give reluctantly, clutching their donation as they place it in the box. They give with smiles, with joy, freely and generously.

وَالْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِالْأَسْحَارِ - “those who seek forgiveness in the early morning hours.” This phrase reveals a powerful secret. Want Allah ﷻ to forgive you? When should you ask? Early morning, in the hours before dawn. This is the optimal time to seek His forgiveness.

But there’s a deeper lesson here about how to embody all these qualities. If your morning is right, your day is right. When your day is right, your life is right. When your morning begins properly - with Fajr prayer on time, with istighfar, with beautiful private supplication between you and Allah ﷻ alone - that entire day enjoys the blessings of that sacred start. Everything about that day becomes blessed.

Conversely, when you wake up late, pray Fajr half-asleep, rush through it mechanically, that impacts your entire day. But when you begin with focused salah and heartfelt istighfar in those precious pre-dawn moments, that day carries barakah. This makes you more patient (sabr), more truthful (sidq), more obedient (qunoot) throughout that day. You develop these qualities progressively because you’re starting each day correctly.

What a beautiful secret Allah ﷻ has shared with us in this remarkable passage. The path to His pleasure isn’t mysterious or impossibly complex. Start your day with Him, in those quiet hours when most of creation sleeps, when you rise to stand before your Master. That’s where transformation begins. That’s how ordinary people become the extraordinary servants whom Allah ﷻ watches with pleasure, saying, “Well done, My slave. Well done.”

Ayah 17–18 · linked video: 04. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 17-18)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 17-18

Ayah 17

ٱلصَّـٰبِرِينَ وَٱلصَّـٰدِقِينَ وَٱلْقَـٰنِتِينَ وَٱلْمُنفِقِينَ وَٱلْمُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِٱلْأَسْحَارِ

The patient, the truthful, the obedient, those who spend, and those who seek forgiveness before dawn.

Grammatical Context and Connection

From a grammatical standpoint, these descriptions continue from the previous ayah. The words appear in the grammatical state of nasb (الصَّـٰبِرِينَ) rather than raf’ (الصَّـٰبِرُونَ) because they describe “the slaves” mentioned in ayah 15: “Allah ﷻ is fully aware of His slaves.” These are the slaves who say “Our Lord, forgive us our sins,” and this ayah continues describing their qualities.

The conversation actually begins several ayahs earlier with the discussion of desires: “Beautified for people is the love of that which they desire - of women and sons, heaped-up sums of gold and silver…” (3:14). Now Allah ﷻ presents the qualities that help a believer control those natural temptations.

Understanding Desires as Natural Forces

The desires Allah ﷻ mentions are not inherently evil. In fact, they’re necessary components of human life on earth. The attraction between men and women ensures the continuation of the human species. The drive to have children connects to this. The motivation to earn wealth and better oneself professionally prevents the collapse of human civilization. These are all essential aspects of life.

The analogy here is like a powerful vehicle with great acceleration but no brakes. The power itself is valuable, even necessary, but without control mechanisms, that same power leads to destruction. The qualities Allah ﷻ lists in this ayah are the brakes that allow us to harness these powerful drives without being consumed by them.

As-Sabireen: The Patient Ones

The first brake is sabr - patience and self-restraint. We often think of patience only in negative situations, like when someone insults us and we hold back our response. But sabr also means restraining yourself from fulfilling a temptation that’s right in front of you, something you have access to but choose not to indulge in. It’s like meat dangling before a lion that still holds back.

This quality represents strength of character. Someone who will not be completely overtaken by the beautification of this world must first possess the ability to hold themselves back, to exercise self-control even when every fiber of their being wants to give in.

As-Sadiqeen: The Truthful Ones

The second quality is being honest - not just with others, but crucially, with yourself. You cannot be delusional about your struggles. You cannot pretend, “I don’t have that problem. I don’t desire wealth. I only think about Jannah. I don’t even notice beautiful houses or well-decorated homes.”

That’s denial, and it’s dangerous. It’s natural for human beings to appreciate beauty. Why else would Allah ﷻ describe Paradise with gardens, trees, and rivers? Because we find those things beautiful. The fact that dunya is described as tempting means these attractions exist within us.

Being truthful means acknowledging: “Yes, these temptations are real. I feel them. I recognize them in myself.” This is like the first step in any form of therapy - you must come to terms with your problem, escape denial, before you can actually address it. Honesty with yourself prepares you to combat the reality of temptation.

Al-Qaniteen: The Obedient and Subservient

If you have sabr and you’re honest with yourself, now it’s time for resolve and commitment. Qunut represents this ready and willing attitude: “Yes, Allah ﷻ, whatever You tell me to do, I will do.” This quality creates the fine balance between competing demands in life.

In Surah Al-Hadid, we learn about certain Christian communities who became so spiritually concerned that they denied the natural desires Allah ﷻ placed in human beings. They viewed these as “desires of the flesh” to be completely rejected in pursuit of a higher calling. But this created a spiritual, personal, and psychological imbalance. Allah ﷻ says about them: “They were not able to give it consideration as it deserved” (57:27). The expectations they placed on themselves were impossible to maintain.

This balance between materialism and spirituality remains a challenge for the Ummah today. On one end of the spectrum, some want to completely shun the world. On the other end, others embrace worldly pursuits without considering the spiritual harm of indulgence. The more we immerse ourselves in dunya - even in what’s halal - the harder spiritual connection becomes. Concentration in prayer weakens. Tears in dua become rare. The Quran becomes harder to listen to. This is simply the reality of how the heart works.

The quality of qunut provides this balance, creating subservience to Allah’s ﷻ commands that keeps both worlds in proper perspective.

Al-Munfiqeen: Those Who Spend

If this world has been beautified, Allah ﷻ has beautified the next world far more. Spending in the path of Allah ﷻ is essentially depositing in your next life - a retirement plan for the Akhirah. The more you invest, the more you look forward to that eventual return.

Consider worldly investments. Some accounts, like retirement funds, cannot be accessed until you reach a certain age. You don’t have access to that money now, but what consoles you is knowing it’s better for you in the long run. However, many people lack the patience for long-term investments. When told the return will come in ten years, they say, “Ten years? No thanks. I’ll keep my money now, even if it doesn’t grow.”

This connects directly back to the first quality. People who engage in long-term investments must first have what? Patience. They need sabr. When someone has that patience, and they have qunut - that subservience to Allah’s ﷻ call - they can spend without hesitation. They can let go of one of life’s primary sources of comfort: money.

Al-Mustaghfireen bil-Ashar: Those Who Seek Forgiveness Before Dawn

The final quality addresses another comfort everyone enjoys: sleep. Even the poorest person, even someone in torment, finds relief in sleep. Problems disappear during those hours of rest.

But those who truly desire the Akhirah are المُسْتَغْفِرِينَ بِٱلْأَسْحَارِ - they seek forgiveness in the early morning hours, before dawn. They sacrifice this daily comfort that everyone else clings to. There’s the special comfort from wealth that they learn to release through spending. And there’s the daily comfort of sleep that they willingly give up to stand before their Lord.

Watch children - they may resist sleep, but once they fall into it, they love it. They don’t want to wake up. The acting performance of pretending to be asleep on a school morning is Oscar-worthy. This is how much human beings naturally cling to this comfort. Yet the true believers sacrifice even this for their connection with Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 18

شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَأُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ قَآئِمًۢا بِٱلْقِسْطِ ۚ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا هُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ

Allah bears witness that there is no deity except Him, and [so do] the angels and those of knowledge - [that He is] maintaining [creation] in justice. There is no deity except Him, the Exalted in Might, the Wise.

The Testimony and Its Witnesses

This ayah transitions from personal struggle to collective justice. The imbalance created by uncontrolled desires doesn’t just harm individuals - it creates injustice in society. From the smallest business disputes to the largest conflicts between nations, it all stems from greed, from people wanting more than their fair share of the resources that have been beautified for humanity.

Allah ﷻ Himself bears witness - شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ. The word shahada (testimony) immediately evokes the imagery of a courtroom, a formal institution whose entire purpose is to exercise justice. From this word alone, the theme of justice permeates the ayah.

Three groups testify to La ilaha illa Hu - that He alone deserves worship and obedience: Allah ﷻ Himself, the angels, and those who possess true knowledge (أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ). But their testimony isn’t merely verbal - it manifests in their actions.

Standing by Justice

The phrase قَآئِمًۢا بِٱلْقِسْطِ - “standing by justice” or “maintaining justice” - is crucial. Allah ﷻ literally states that He testifies to His exclusive right to be worshiped, and one of His justifications is that He stands by justice. The angels worship Allah ﷻ because He is just, because He Himself stands by justice and expects justice from everyone else.

Therefore, whoever testifies to La ilaha illa Allah has also testified and committed themselves to justice itself. The word qaim means not just standing by something, but committing to execute it. In classical Arabic, “rise to the occasion” is expressed as qama bi - to rise up and fulfill something. Allah ﷻ is committed to justice. He actually executes it.

Justice as the First Practical Call of Islam

This leads to an important reflection about Muslim societies. We love applying legal labels to countries and communities: Is this Islamic? Is that Islamic? Does this have Shariah? Does that have Shariah? But one thing is often missing from this discourse.

Consider the first call of Islam during the Makkan period - roughly two-thirds of the Quran. This call focuses on the unseen: belief in Allah ﷻ, belief in the Messenger ﷺ and his message, and belief in the Akhirah. But what practical changes were being demanded on the ground?

Much of Islamic law hadn’t been revealed yet in the Makkan period. There were no extensive regulations on inheritance, divorce, or many other legal matters. So what practical change was being asked for, besides belief itself?

If you examine the Makkan Quran carefully, you can identify it in one word: justice.

أَرَءَيْتَ ٱلَّذِى يُكَذِّبُ بِٱلدِّينِ - “Have you seen the one who denies the Recompense? For that is the one who drives away the orphan” (107:1-2). This is about justice.

وَيْلٌ لِّلْمُطَفِّفِينَ - “Woe to those who give less [than due]” (83:1) - those who cheat in business, giving customers a little less than they deserve. This is about justice.

The baby girls buried alive for no crime except being female - وَإِذَا ٱلْمَوْءُۥدَةُ سُئِلَتْ بِأَىِّ ذَنۢبٍۢ قُتِلَتْ “For what sin was she killed?” (81:8-9). This is about justice.

All of these issues transcend religious identity. Justice is not an “Islam thing” - it’s a universal human concern. But what we learn from this ayah is that for a Muslim, the first practical implication of La ilaha illa Allah is justice. That’s the first practical, collective concern.

A Living Example

Years ago, I met a young brother at Masjid At-Taqwa in Brooklyn. He was there about an hour before Jumu’ah, looking somewhat out of place. He had taken his shahadah maybe an hour earlier.

His story: At 16 or 17, he began exploring religions. He became a devout Christian, studying the Bible intensely, but grew dissatisfied with theological contradictions. He thought perhaps those who held to the Old Testament preserved more truth, so he studied Torah and converted to Judaism - no easy process. He even traveled to Israel, studied Hebrew, returned to New York, and joined a Yeshiva institute.

Despite all this dedication, he remained dissatisfied. Why? Because he recognized that those born into the religion treated him as inferior, as an outsider who didn’t really belong.

Then he came to the masjid just to observe. He walked into a run-down neighborhood in Bed-Stuy, Brooklyn, expecting disappointment. Instead, he found Hispanics, African Americans, Malaysians, Turks, Yemenis, Moroccans, Pakistanis, and Bangladeshis - all together in one congregation, indistinguishable from one another in their rows.

He told me: “This can’t be anything but the truth. This is it.” Just seeing Muslims together in that way was enough for him. He hadn’t even begun studying Islam, yet he decided right then to become Muslim.

This is شَهِدَ ٱللَّهُ - this is أُو۟لُوا۟ ٱلْعِلْمِ recognizing truth. He saw justice embodied, and that testimony led him to Islam.

The Names of Allah: Al-Aziz Al-Hakeem

The ayah concludes by reaffirming La ilaha illa Hu, as if to say: now that you understand it’s supposed to be implemented with justice, refresh this understanding, renew it.

Then come two divine names: ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ - the Exalted in Might, the Wise.

Al-Aziz relates to authority, honor, and nobility. It describes the respect someone commands because of their authority. When someone has legitimate authority, they command respect - that’s izza. Allah ﷻ commands all honor and respect because of His ultimate authority.

There’s an axiom: “Absolute power corrupts absolutely.” The more authority humans possess, the more likely they become corrupt. When a dictator or king has absolute power, they often make unwise, impulsive decisions because they face no consequences. They don’t have to think twice. Extremely wealthy people exhibit this too - spending on ridiculous things as a manifestation of power disconnected from wisdom.

But Allah ﷻ mentions Al-Aziz immediately tied to Al-Hakeem. Both go hand in hand. He has absolute authority, yet His every decision overflows with wisdom. There is no recklessness, no corruption, no impulsiveness in His power.

This also answers a fundamental question: Who has the authority to define what justice actually is? Allah ﷻ Himself. And whatever He defines as justice is full of wisdom - Al-Hakeem.

This is the foundation upon which all Islamic law, all Islamic ethics, and all Islamic social order must be built: justice rooted in divine wisdom, executed by the One who has both absolute authority and perfect wisdom.

Ayah 19–21 · linked video: 05. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 19-21)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 19-21

Ayah 19

إِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمُ ۗ وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ ۗ وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ

This surah introduces a theme that runs throughout its entirety: Islam itself. Just as Surah Al-Baqarah continually discussed iman and the state of the heart, this surah keeps returning to the practical manifestations of submission to Allah ﷻ.

The ayah begins with a powerful declaration: إِنَّ ٱلدِّينَ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمُ (Indeed, the deen acceptable to Allah is Islam). The word deen carries deeper meanings than simply “religion.” In Arabic, deen originally referred to dealings between people, particularly loans and transactions. It encompasses every relationship and interaction in life: husband and wife, parent and child, community members, governments and citizens. Each relationship has particular expectations, specific parameters of what to give and what to expect in return.

The famous Arabic expression captures this: كَمَا تَدِينُ تُدَانُ (As you deal with others, you will be dealt with). Therefore, when we translate deen as “way of life,” it means that every interaction, every transaction, every dealing you engage in must be based on submission to Allah ﷻ. This is Islam. For any interaction to be acceptable to Allah ﷻ, it must be rooted in submission to His ﷻ will.

The ayah continues: وَمَا ٱخْتَلَفَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ (And those who were given the Book did not differ). Notice the passive construction: those who were given the Book. The Book was revealed by Allah ﷻ Himself, filled with justice and wisdom. When the ultimate authority speaks, there should be no room for disagreement. Yet disagreement arose, and the ayah explains when and why: إِلَّا مِنۢ بَعْدِ مَا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ (except after knowledge had come to them).

This is a sobering reality that we must confront even today. Young Muslims often ask: “Why do knowledgeable people disagree with each other? Why do scholars sometimes speak against one another?” The answer is provided here: بَغْيًۢا بَيْنَهُمْ (out of transgression among themselves). The word بَغْيًا indicates an urge to dominate, to show superiority over others, to transgress against someone else’s position. It was a contest of egos.

This reveals something profound about the nature of knowledge and submission. The very beginning of this ayah emphasized Islam—submission. Submission itself is an act of humility, a giving up of one’s ego. When someone acquires knowledge of this deen yet falls into disagreement, it indicates they have lost sight of its very essence: submission.

This is a warning for all students of knowledge. The more we learn, the more we might find ourselves getting into debates and arguments about methodology, interpretation, or practice. One must constantly check their intention: Did I learn this knowledge to engage in arguments, or to increase my submission to Allah ﷻ? Knowledge should increase us in humility, not inflate our egos. Otherwise, we can become extremely knowledgeable people who have lost the very essence of what we started with.

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: وَمَن يَكْفُرْ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (And whoever disbelieves in the signs of Allah, then indeed, Allah is swift in account). This conditional statement addresses anyone who would disbelieve in Allah’s ﷻ ayat in the manner described—disagreeing even after receiving knowledge, projecting their ego forward.

The phrase سَرِيعُ ٱلْحِسَابِ (swift in account) carries subtle eloquence. By definition, audits take time, so saying Allah ﷻ is “swift” in His reckoning seems paradoxical. The beauty is this: when people of knowledge argue, every time you pass a verdict against them, they have an intellectual response. It takes time to reach a final judgment through debate. But with Allah ﷻ, there will be no lengthy debate. The judgment will be immediate and final.

Ayah 20

فَإِنْ حَآجُّوكَ فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّهِ وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِ ۗ وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ ءَأَسْلَمْتُمْ ۚ فَإِنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ ۖ وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ

The ayah now addresses the Prophet ﷺ directly about debates with the People of the Book. The word حَآجُّوكَ comes from the root meaning to present an argument so convincing that your opponent is completely stumped. It implies they are trying to find something to get him with, attempting to corner him in debate.

Allah’s ﷻ response is instructive: فَقُلْ أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّهِ (Then say, “I have submitted my face to Allah”). Don’t get caught up in endless debates. Just declare your position with clarity. Notice the Prophet Ibrahim (AS) similarly declared, أَسْلَمْتُ لِرَبِّ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ (I have submitted to the Lord of the worlds). But here, the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ is instructed to add “my face” to the declaration of submission.

Why specify the face? Submitting yourself is already an act of humility, but adding “face” to submission represents further humility. This directly contrasts with those who disagreed in the Book out of بَغْيًا—arrogance. Their transgression was manifest, while the humility of Allah’s ﷻ Messenger ﷺ is manifest in the phrase أَسْلَمْتُ وَجْهِىَ لِلَّهِ.

Then comes وَمَنِ ٱتَّبَعَنِ (and whoever follows me). Notice the beautiful expression of humility: he doesn’t even complete the pronoun. It’s not a full statement about his followers, just a brief mention. This continuation expresses further humility because he speaks minimally of himself.

The command then extends: وَقُل لِّلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْأُمِّيِّـۧنَ ءَأَسْلَمْتُمْ (And say to those who were given the Scripture and to the unlettered, “Have you submitted yourselves?”). The Messenger ﷺ is commanded to give this declaration both to the People of the Book and to the Arabs who were unlettered. The previous ayah asked about belief: أَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِمَآ أَنزَلَ (Will you believe in what I have sent down?). Now the question is about Islam itself: أَأَسْلَمْتُمْ (Have you submitted?).

The response is conditional: فَإِنْ أَسْلَمُوا۟ فَقَدِ ٱهْتَدَوا۟ (So if they submit, they have been guided). But then comes the possibility of rejection: وَّإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّمَا عَلَيْكَ ٱلْبَلَـٰغُ (But if they turn away, then upon you is only the notification).

This is crucial instruction. In the previous passage, we read that if they turn away, they are stuck in their disbelief. When Allah ﷻ has already indicated someone won’t listen, wouldn’t you be motivated to stop talking to them? Why waste your breath? But Allah ﷻ instructs His ﷺ Messenger to continue: your only responsibility is to deliver the message. Whether they accept it, whether they think about it internally, whether they are impacted—none of that is your concern. Your concern is simply to continue delivering the message.

This is vital for Muslims engaged in dawah, whether within our families or communities. The effort continues regardless of how well it’s received, regardless of whether we think someone is paying attention or being impacted. We can become frustrated when giving advice that seems to be brushed off. But our job is nothing more than to deliver a sincere reminder. If we start thinking, “I gave such an excellent speech, yet this person hasn’t changed,” then we’re beginning to think we control hearts. We don’t. We can say a thousand words, and Allah ﷻ can place His blessing in half a word, and that single word can transform someone’s life—not because it came from our mouth, but because Allah ﷻ empowered it.

The ayah concludes: وَٱللَّهُ بَصِيرٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ (And Allah is Seeing of His servants). This is the second time this phrase appears in this passage. Earlier, when describing the people of taqwa, it said Allah ﷻ is Seeing of His servants. Taqwa is an internal state—only Allah ﷻ knows who truly possesses it. Now it’s repeated when someone turns away from the Messenger’s ﷺ words. Turning away is an outward, visible action. Why mention Allah’s ﷻ sight for something apparent?

The reminder is this: even in the visible act of turning away, there’s an unseen dimension. Only Allah ﷻ knows what’s truly in the hearts. So don’t pass judgment. The repetition connects to what came before: I have full view because I know what’s inside the hearts.

Ayah 21

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ

This ayah opens with إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ يَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ (Indeed, those who disbelieve in the signs of Allah). The present tense verb يَكْفُرُونَ encompasses both the present and the future. Compare this to an earlier passage in Surah Al-Baqarah: ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ كَانُوا۟ يَكْفُرُونَ (That is because they used to disbelieve). That was commentary about the history of the Israelites. Here, Allah ﷻ speaks about the present and extends into the future. The scope is broader, the application more general.

The ayah continues: وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ (and kill the prophets without right). In Surah Al-Baqarah, the phrase was بِغَيْرِ ٱلْحَقِّ (without the right), referring to a specific, known justification. The Israelites had particular legal rulings: eye for an eye, and the prohibition لَا تَسْفِكُونَ دِمَآءَكُمْ (you shall not shed your own blood)—meaning you cannot kill your own people. These were known parameters.

Here, the phrase is بِغَيْرِ حَقٍّ without the definite article. This opens the meaning: without any justification whatsoever. The conversation is more general than just the historical Jews—it extends to any who would follow their pattern. Moreover, since this addresses the present and future, those future justifications are unknown, uncertain. So the phrasing captures this: without any imaginable justification, any thinkable cause.

Then comes a profound addition: وَيَقْتُلُونَ ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ مِنَ ٱلنَّاسِ (and kill those who order justice from among the people). The Israelites killed prophets. Muslims did not kill prophets, because we had no prophets after the Messenger ﷺ. But we had scholars.

The Prophet ﷺ said, “The scholars of my ummah are like the prophets of the Children of Israel.” This can be understood in an honorable sense: they are honored as prophets were honored; they shepherd the people as prophets shepherded their people. But it can also be understood tragically: what happened to the prophets of the Israelites—execution at the hands of their own people—also befell the scholars of this ummah. Across Islamic history, some of our greatest scholars were killed by Muslims themselves. This pattern continues even today.

Notice Allah ﷻ didn’t simply say “they kill the scholars” or “they kill the believers.” He specified: ٱلَّذِينَ يَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْقِسْطِ (those who order justice). When people have a particular agenda rooted in injustice, they can tolerate anyone—until someone starts connecting Islam to justice itself. So long as you keep Islam and justice divorced from each other, you’re safe. But the moment you call for justice, you become a threat. You rattle the status quo. You earn the same fate as those who were sent as prophets, because you called for justice.

This applies not only at the governmental or societal level. Consider your own family dynamics: injustices within extended families, in-laws treating each other unjustly, grandparents being unjust to grandchildren. All kinds of injustices occur within our families. Who speaks out against them? And when someone does, what happens? You get socially murdered. If you try to raise these issues, you might hear, “I’ll disown you.” Speaking out about justice, especially regarding those closest to you—which is precisely what we’re commanded to do—is incredibly difficult. You make enemies. You get labeled: “Who do you think you are, so self-righteous?” These are the same accusations thrown at the prophets.

The ayah concludes with stark sarcasm: فَبَشِّرْهُم بِعَذَابٍ أَلِيمٍ (So give them tidings of a painful punishment). The word بَشِّرْ typically means to give good news, to congratulate. Here it’s used ironically: “Congratulate them that they’re going to burn, and it’s going to hurt.” This is divine sarcasm—a devastating indictment of those who silence the voices calling for justice.

Ayah 22–25 · linked video: 06. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 22-25)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 22-25

Ayah 22

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ ٱلَّذِينَ حَبِطَتْ أَعْمَـٰلُهُمْ فِى ٱلدُّنْيَا وَٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ

These are the people whose deeds have been completely nullified, both in this world and in the Hereafter. The word حَبِطَتْ implies that their actions are seized, rendered void of any blessing or benefit. This is a profound reality—they will experience no blessings from what they considered good deeds, neither here nor in the Akhirah.

When we perform righteous actions sincerely for Allah’s ﷻ sake, we typically witness their fruits in both realms. A sincere, focused salah filled with khushu brings peace to the heart—this is a gift we experience immediately in the dunya, in addition to its reward in the Akhirah. Similarly, when we give sadaqah purely for Allah’s ﷻ pleasure, we see an increase in our rizq (provision) in this world as well as reward stored for us in the Hereafter. The Prophet ﷺ taught us that “giving charity does not decrease wealth at all.” The consequences of our good deeds manifest in both worlds.

Consider the example of the Quraysh before Islam. They were custodians of the Kaaba, providing water to pilgrims and maintaining the sacred house. They considered these acts righteous deeds. Yet Allah ﷻ declares that such people will see none of the goodness of their supposed good works in this world, nor in the next. Why? Because their deeds lacked the essential foundation of tawhid and sincere devotion to Allah ﷻ alone.

The ayah concludes with a stark warning: وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ—they will have no helpers, no aids, no one to intercede for them or rescue them from their fate in the Akhirah.

Ayah 23

أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ نَصِيبًا مِّنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ وَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ

Allah ﷻ asks: “Haven’t you seen those who were given a portion from the Book?” The word نَصِيبًا means a portion or share—something beneficial that comes your way. Imagine a delicious cake being sliced at a gathering, with everyone eagerly awaiting their portion. This is the imagery behind nasib—something desirable, something you’re fortunate to receive. Allah ﷻ is describing His revelation as a treasure, a gift, something precious that was entrusted to the People of the Book. This echoes what we learned in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ mentioned that when a book came to them confirming what they already had, they should have recognized its value.

يُدْعَوْنَ إِلَىٰ كِتَـٰبِ ٱللَّهِ—they are being invited to the Book of Allah. Notice the beautiful connection here: they were given a book, and through that very book, they are being called to act according to its guidance. The purpose? لِيَحْكُمَ بَيْنَهُمْ—so that it may judge between them, so that decisions can be made with fairness and justice. Allah ﷻ is connecting not just Islam to justice, but the very essence of revealed scripture to justice. The fundamental purpose of the Book was to establish fair judgments between people, to create a society built on equity and truth.

Yet what happens? ثُمَّ يَتَوَلَّىٰ فَرِيقٌ مِّنْهُمْ—then a group from among them turns away. The word فَرِيقٌ comes from the root that means to separate or cut off—a faction that deliberately distinguishes itself from the whole. Despite having the Book, despite knowing its commandments, they deliberately turn away. وَهُم مُّعْرِضُونَ—while they are deliberately ignoring. They weren’t unaware; they consciously overlooked what they knew to be true.

This ayah carries powerful lessons for every generation. You can have people who claim allegiance to the Book of Allah ﷻ—perhaps they recite it beautifully, study its meanings diligently, memorize its verses, or celebrate its revelation. But when the Book begins to make demands on their lives—when it comes to business decisions, family matters, inheritance disputes, financial dealings—they conveniently turn away. They say, “Yes, of course we believe in the Quran,” but when you bring up what Allah ﷻ actually says about a specific matter, suddenly there’s resistance, avoidance, or rationalization. This is the very behavior Allah ﷻ condemns: selective adherence to divine guidance, accepting the Book in theory while rejecting it in practice.

Ayah 24

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟ لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ ۖ وَغَرَّهُمْ فِى دِينِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ

ذَٰلِكَ بِأَنَّهُمْ قَالُوا۟—that behavior, that turning away, stems from what they said: لَن تَمَسَّنَا ٱلنَّارُ إِلَّآ أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ—“The Fire will not touch us except for a few numbered days.” The word تَمَسَّنَا suggests a light touch, something minimal. In their minds, even if there were consequences, they wouldn’t be severe. This false belief was mentioned previously in Surah Al-Baqarah, where they claimed the Fire would touch them for only أَيَّامًا مَّعْدُودَاتٍ —countable days.

Here in Surah Aal-E-Imran, the wording shifts slightly to مَّعْدُودَٰتٍ , which in Arabic grammar indicates even fewer days than before. Interestingly, by this point in the surah, Allah ﷻ has mentioned fewer sins committed by them compared to what was detailed in Al-Baqarah. It’s as if their internal rationalization has become even more minimizing: “It’s really not that bad. Maybe just a long weekend in the Fire.” The human capacity for self-deception knows no bounds.

Then comes the devastating diagnosis: وَغَرَّهُمْ فِى دِينِهِم مَّا كَانُوا۟ يَفْتَرُونَ—“And what they used to fabricate deluded them in their religion.” The word يَفْتَرُونَ means to make something up, to fabricate a lie. Here’s what’s remarkable: they originally invented this belief to comfort themselves, to rationalize their disobedience. But they repeated the lie so many times, said it so often, that they eventually started believing it themselves. They lived the lie so long that the line between truth and fabrication disappeared in their own minds.

This is the terrifying power of propaganda and self-deception. When you hear something wrong often enough, you begin to believe it. When you say something false repeatedly enough, it becomes your reality. What started as a deliberate fabrication to ease their conscience became the delusion that destroyed them. They were deceived by their own deception.

Ayah 25
