فَكَيْفَ إِذَا جَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ

فَكَيْفَ—“So how will it be?” This is a rhetorical question of epic proportions. The word كَيْفَ by itself creates a scene of overwhelming magnitude. It’s not asking for information; it’s declaring the unimaginable weight of what’s coming.

إِذَا جَمَعْنَـٰهُمْ—“When We gather them.” Remember, these people were فَرِيقٌ—a faction that cut themselves off, that went their separate way. Now Allah ﷻ will gather them back. There’s profound irony here: you scattered yourselves, separated yourselves from the truth, but on that Day, there will be no escape, no separation, no turning away.

لِيَوْمٍ لَّا رَيْبَ فِيهِ—“For a Day in which there is no doubt whatsoever.” These people spent their lives rationalizing, minimizing, and deluding themselves about punishment. But before they worry about how many days they’ll spend in the Fire, they need to confront the reality of this Day itself—a Day that is coming with absolute certainty. Notice this phrase echoes the opening of the surah: إِنَّ اللَّهَ لَا يُخْلِفُ الْمِيعَادَ (2:9)—“Indeed, Allah ﷻ never fails His promise.” They forgot to internalize that fundamental truth.

وَوُفِّيَتْ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا كَسَبَتْ—“And every soul will be given in full what it earned.” The verb وُفِّيَتْ comes from the root و-ف-ي, meaning to fulfill completely, to pay in full. It’s the word you would write at the bottom of an invoice: “Paid in Full.” No outstanding balance. No partial payment. Every single person will receive the complete recompense for everything they did.

There’s a remarkable linguistic precision here that reveals the miracle of the Quran. This verb is in the passive past tense. Throughout the Quran, when Allah ﷻ is the central subject doing the recompensing, the past tense is used—even though the Day of Judgment is in the future. This is because Allah ﷻ is the Owner of time itself. For Him, future events are as certain as past ones. The past tense expresses His absolute guarantee and dominion over what’s to come.

However, when the human being is the central subject of the ayah, the present-future tense is used instead, because we are bound by time. We must wait for events to unfold. For example, in another ayah, Allah ﷻ says: “It is not befitting for a prophet to have certain aspirations, and whoever does will come with whatever they harbored on the Day of Judgment. Then every person will be given what they earned in full” (3:161). There, the verb يُوَفَّىٰ appears in the present-future tense because the focus is on the human being and their accountability within time.

This consistent pattern throughout the Quran—past tense when Allah ﷻ is the subject, present-future tense when humans are the subject—demonstrates the deliberate, miraculous precision of divine speech.

وَهُمْ لَا يُظْلَمُونَ—“And they will not be wronged in the least.” Despite the severity of the reckoning, despite receiving full recompense for their deeds, no one will be treated unjustly. This isn’t oppression; this is perfect justice. The root ظ-ل-م appears here in the negative, emphasizing that Allah’s ﷻ accounting is flawlessly fair. Those who fabricated beliefs to excuse their disobedience, who turned away from divine guidance while claiming to follow it, who deluded themselves into thinking consequences would be minimal—they will face exactly what they earned, no more and no less. And that precise, uncompromising justice should give us all pause to reflect on how seriously we take Allah’s ﷻ commands in our own lives.

Ayah 26–27 · linked video: 07. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 26-27)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayah 26-27 Commentary

Ayah 26

قُلِ ٱللَّهُمَّ مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ تُؤْتِى ٱلْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتَنزِعُ ٱلْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَآءُ وَتُعِزُّ مَن تَشَآءُ وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَآءُ ۖ بِيَدِكَ ٱلْخَيْرُ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

Say: “O Allah, Owner of all sovereignty, You give sovereignty to whom You will and You take sovereignty away from whom You will. You honor whom You will and You humble whom You will. In Your hand is all good. Indeed, You are over all things competent.”

The Divine Command to Make Dua

This ayah represents one of the most significant supplications in the Quran. Allah ﷻ begins with the command قُل (Say), which elevates this dua beyond a mere reported prayer. When Allah ﷻ uses “Qul” in the Quran, He is directly commanding His Messenger ﷺ—and by extension, all believers—to recite these words. This is not simply a dua that we learn by observing others; it is a supplication that Allah ﷻ Himself has honored us with and commands us to memorize and internalize.

The context of this ayah is significant. The previous ayahs discussed the People of the Book, particularly those who received Scripture yet turned away from it knowingly. They had been given tremendous authority and kingdom—Allah ﷻ mentions in Surah Al-Baqarah how Prophet Dawud (AS) was granted kingdom and wisdom, and how the rule of Prophet Sulayman (AS) spread across nearly the entire known world, creating an empire unprecedented in human history. Yet despite these gifts, some among them became delusional, imagining they would not be held accountable on the Day of Judgment.

Allah ﷻ now teaches us this supplication to understand a fundamental truth: if you think you have no control on Judgment Day, you should realize you have no real control even now. All authority, all dominion, all power belongs to Allah ﷻ alone.

Understanding “Allahumma”

The opening word اللَّهُمَّ deserves special attention. Some people mistakenly think the “humma” at the end is related to the plural pronoun “hum”, leading to confusion. This is incorrect for several reasons. First, “hum” is a third-person pronoun, while addressing Allah ﷻ requires the second person. Second, the letter ه already exists in the word “Allah,” so adding another would be redundant. Third, this construction was never understood this way in classical Arabic scholarship.

“Allahumma” is actually a unique vocative particle used specifically for calling upon Allah ﷻ. The extra meem with a shadda at the end serves as a device for invocation. In classical Arabic, this pattern appears in other calling expressions—for example, هَلُمَّ means “come here.” The “umma” suffix functions as a method of calling or summoning. Therefore, “Allahumma” is the most emphatic way of saying “O Allah.”

What makes this particularly special is that while most Quranic supplications use رَبَّنَا (Our Lord), this dua uses “Allahumma.” Throughout the Quran, you find “Rabbana atina,” “Rabbana la tuzigh,” “Rabbana innana amanna”—but “Allahumma” is rare and distinctive in the Quran, appearing most frequently in the prophetic traditions outside the Quran.

Owner of All Sovereignty

The phrase مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ combines two profound concepts. The word مَالِك means owner, while مُلْك means kingdom or sovereignty. If Allah ﷻ had said “Malika al-Mulk” (Owner of Ownership), only the concept of possession would be captured. If He had said “Malika al-Malik” (King of Kings), only the concept of kingship would be emphasized. But by combining both—Malik and Mulk—Allah ﷻ declares that He is both the Owner and the Sovereign of all dominion.

This combination is profound because ownership and kingship operate on different scales. When you own something small—a pen, a car—you have complete, microscopic control over it. You know it inside and out; it’s entirely in your grasp. When you have dominion over something large—a kingdom, a territory—you have macro-level authority but cannot control every minute detail within your realm. No earthly king can control every leaf falling from every tree in his kingdom.

But Allah ﷻ is مَـٰلِكَ ٱلْمُلْكِ—the Owner of all Sovereignty. He possesses both the expansive authority of a king over the entire universe and the intimate, complete control of an owner over every microscopic detail within it. Every speck of dust, every falling leaf, every breath taken—all are under His absolute authority and intimate knowledge. This is a declaration of Allah’s complete and total dominion over all creation.

The Giving and Taking of Authority

تُؤْتِى ٱلْمُلْكَ مَن تَشَآءُ (You give sovereignty to whom You will) begins the description of how Allah ﷻ exercises His authority. Notice that Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “You give ownership” but “You give mulk”—You give sovereignty, kingdom, dominion. This is the greater gift. When someone gives you ownership of something, that’s valuable. But when someone gives you dominion over something, authority over a realm, that is immense.

The significance of this phrase becomes clear when we make dua. Even when we’re asking Allah ﷻ for something small, we’re addressing the One who has given entire kingdoms to whomever He wills. Our small request pales in comparison to what He has already granted others. This puts our supplications in perspective—we’re asking from the One whose generosity knows no bounds.

وَتَنزِعُ ٱلْمُلْكَ مِمَّن تَشَآءُ (And You take sovereignty away from whom You will) uses the powerful word تَنزِعُ, which comes from نَزْع. This word describes yanking something out from where it was firmly rooted and established. Imagine pulling a plant from the ground, complete with its deep roots—this requires force and determination. Or imagine extracting a tooth that’s deeply embedded—this is also naz’.

The implication is striking: sometimes kingdoms become so established, so stable, so entrenched that no one can imagine their downfall. Dynasties rule for generations, and their authority seems permanent and unshakeable. Yet Allah ﷻ says He can yank away this authority, no matter how deeply rooted it appears. If we look at contemporary examples—the Arab Spring—no one imagined those regimes would fall. Even two weeks before the uprisings, people said it was impossible. Yet when Allah ﷻ wills, He removes authority from whoever He wills, regardless of how permanent their rule appears.

Honor and Humiliation

وَتُعِزُّ مَن تَشَآءُ (And You honor whom You will) describes the granting of عِزَّة —honor that comes specifically from authority and power. This isn’t just respect or dignity in general; it’s the honor and prestige that accompany position and authority.

وَتُذِلُّ مَن تَشَآءُ (And You humble whom You will) uses the word تُذِلُّ , which doesn’t merely mean to humiliate. The root ذُلّ originally means to weaken, to make powerless. Humiliation is the result of this weakness. When someone is rendered incapable of responding or defending themselves, that state of powerlessness leads to humiliation. So Allah ﷻ describes not just bringing disgrace, but removing power and strength, which naturally results in a humiliated state.

Understanding these paired concepts helps us recognize that honor and disgrace in this world are entirely in Allah’s hands. He elevates and He humbles. He empowers and He weakens. No amount of human scheming can permanently secure honor, and no amount of plotting can permanently bring someone low. Everything returns to His will.

All Good Is in His Hand Alone

بِيَدِكَ ٱلْخَيْرُ (In Your hand is all good) is a profound declaration that deserves deep reflection. Allah ﷻ doesn’t say “good and evil are in Your hand” or “everything is in Your hand.” He specifically says all الْخَيْرُ (good) is in His hand alone.

What does this mean? It means that whatever Allah ﷻ does—whether He gives sovereignty or takes it away, whether He honors someone or humbles them—it can only be good. We may not understand the wisdom. We may perceive something as harmful to us. But the reality is that only good emanates from Allah ﷻ.

This echoes the powerful teaching from Surah Al-Baqarah: وَعَسَىٰ أَن تَكْرَهُوا شَيْئًا وَهُوَ خَيْرٌ لَّكُمْ (Perhaps you hate something while it is good for you). You might despise something that Allah ﷻ has decreed, but it contains good for you that you cannot perceive. And you might love something intensely that actually contains harm for you. But Allah ﷻ knows, and you do not know.

The statement “In Your hand is all good” is us acknowledging this lesson. Whatever Allah ﷻ executes, whatever He brings into being, contains ultimate good. All goodness is His property alone, and He distributes it according to His infinite wisdom.

Perfect Power Over All Things

إِنَّكَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (Indeed, You are over all things competent) concludes the ayah by affirming Allah’s unlimited capability. The word قَدِيرٌ emphasizes complete power and ability. Allah ﷻ is not only capable of doing the things mentioned in this ayah—giving sovereignty, removing it, honoring, humbling—but He is capable of accomplishing any good He wills. His power extends over all things without exception or limitation.

This ending perfectly complements the beginning. We started by acknowledging He is the Owner of all Sovereignty, and we end by affirming He has complete power over everything. Between these two declarations, we’ve described the various ways He exercises this ownership and power in the world.

Ayah 27

تُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَتُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ ۖ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ ۖ وَتَرْزُقُ مَن تَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

You cause the night to enter into the day, and You cause the day to enter into the night; and You bring the living out of the dead, and You bring the dead out of the living. And You give provision to whom You will without account.

The Merging of Night and Day

This ayah continues expanding on Allah’s absolute control, now moving from political sovereignty to cosmic sovereignty. The verb تُولِجُ comes from وُلُوج, which means to force something through a narrow opening, to stuff something into a space where it doesn’t easily fit. This is more intensive than simply إِدْخَال (putting in).

To illustrate: if I place a napkin in a box, that’s idkhal—simply putting something in. But if I take a large t-shirt and try to cram it into that same small box, pushing and stuffing it through a narrow opening, that’s wuluj. The word implies effort, force, penetration through resistance.

تُولِجُ ٱلَّيْلَ فِى ٱلنَّهَارِ وَتُولِجُ ٱلنَّهَارَ فِى ٱلَّيْلِ (You cause the night to enter into the day, and You cause the day to enter into the night) uses this powerful verb to describe the transition between night and day. It’s not a passive, gentle transition. There’s a cosmic struggle, a forcing of one into the other. Allah ﷻ stuffs the night into the day, making it gradually overcome the daylight. Then He stuffs the day into the night, forcing the morning to break through the darkness.

This imagery appears elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah Ta-Ha, Allah ﷻ describes knowing مَا يَلِجُ فِي الْأَرْضِ (what penetrates into the earth)—things that must be dug in, forced beneath the surface. The earth resists, but they’re pushed through and buried. Similarly, night and day engage in a constant cosmic battle, each being forced into the other by Allah’s power.

This description makes us reflect on something we witness every single day but take for granted. The coming of night and day isn’t a mechanical, lifeless process. It’s a demonstration of Allah’s active, ongoing control over the cosmos. He doesn’t set the world in motion and step back; He is continuously exercising His authority over creation.

Life From Death and Death From Life

وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْحَىَّ مِنَ ٱلْمَيِّتِ وَتُخْرِجُ ٱلْمَيِّتَ مِنَ ٱلْحَىِّ (And You bring the living out of the dead, and You bring the dead out of the living) presents pairs of opposites under Allah’s control, just like night and day.

On one level, this refers to biological life. The sperm and egg, which are not alive in the full sense, combine to produce a living human being. A seed, which appears lifeless, produces a living plant. A chicken egg, seemingly inert, hatches into a living bird. Scholars have provided these various physical interpretations.

Some have noted the tragic reality that sometimes a mother gives birth to a child and dies in the process—the living coming from the living, but at the cost of life. Other times a mother delivers a stillborn child—death emerging from life. These are painful reminders of Allah’s absolute control over life and death.

However, the most profound interpretation comes from scholars like Fakhruddin ar-Razi (may Allah have mercy on him), who understood this spiritually. Sometimes Allah ﷻ brings believers from disbelieving families—living hearts from spiritually dead environments. A person may be born to parents who rejected faith, raised in an atmosphere of disbelief, yet Allah ﷻ guides their heart to Islam. This is truly bringing the living from the dead.

Conversely, sometimes children of righteous, believing parents turn away from faith and become disbelievers. This is bringing the dead from the living. In this reading, the truly alive are the believers, and disbelievers are as good as dead. This interpretation finds support elsewhere in the Quran. In Surah Yasin, Allah ﷻ says لِّيُنذِرَ مَن كَانَ حَيًّا (to warn whoever is truly alive)—meaning those whose hearts are alive with faith can receive warning, while those whose hearts are spiritually dead cannot benefit from it.

This understanding adds a deeper dimension. Allah ﷻ controls not just biological life but spiritual life. He gives life to dead hearts and allows living hearts to die. He guides whom He wills and allows whom He wills to stray. Even our faith, our most precious possession, is entirely in His hands.

Provision Without Measure

وَتَرْزُقُ مَن تَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (And You give provision to whom You will without account) concludes this passage by addressing one of humanity’s most pressing concerns: sustenance and provision.

The word رِزْق encompasses all forms of provision—food, wealth, health, knowledge, opportunities, and blessings of every kind. Allah ﷻ provides to whomever He wills بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ (without account, without measure, without limitation). When Allah ﷻ gives, He doesn’t count or calculate. He doesn’t measure out provision grudgingly. His generosity is limitless and His treasuries are inexhaustible.

This phrase should instill tremendous hope in believers. When we ask Allah ﷻ for provision, we’re not asking from someone with limited resources who must carefully ration what they give. We’re asking from the One who provides without measure, whose giving has no bounds.

A Prophetic Supplication for Those in Debt

The Prophet ﷺ specifically taught a supplication based on these ayahs for those overwhelmed by debt. A man came to him ﷺ complaining about crushing debt he couldn’t escape. The Prophet ﷺ instructed him to recite these ayahs—Ayah 26 and 27—and then add this beautiful supplication:

يَا رَحْمَنَ الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ وَرَحِيمَهُمَا، تُعْطِي مِنْهُمَا مَنْ تَشَاءُ، وَتَمْنَعُ مَنْ تَشَاءُ، ارْحَمْنِي رَحْمَةً تُغْنِينِي بِهَا عَنْ رَحْمَةِ مَنْ سِوَاكَ

“O Most Merciful of this world and the Hereafter, and the One who is constantly merciful to both of them. You give from them to whomever You will, and You withhold from whomever You will. Show me mercy—a special mercy that makes me free of needing the mercy of anyone other than You.”

This supplication is profound. The person in debt is saying: “O Allah, right now I depend on the mercy of my creditors. I need them to be patient with me, to extend my deadline, to forgive some of my debt. But grant me a mercy so complete, so comprehensive, that I no longer need their mercy. Make me independent of having to beg anyone’s mercy except Yours.”

The dua continues: اللَّهُمَّ أَغْنِنِي مِنَ الْفَقْرِ وَاقْضِ عَنِّي الدَّيْنَ (O Allah, make me independent of poverty and pay off my debt for me). The Prophet ﷺ said that if your debt were the size of the mountain of Uhud, this supplication would be sufficient to have it relieved if recited with sincerity.

Every student buried under student loans, every person struggling with debt, every family burdened by financial obligations should memorize these ayahs and this prophetic supplication. Recite them regularly with full presence of heart, knowing you’re calling upon the Owner of all Sovereignty, the One in whose hand is all good, the One who provides without measure.

These ayahs ultimately teach us that whether we’re concerned about worldly authority, spiritual guidance, or material provision, everything rests entirely in Allah’s hands. Recognizing this truth with full conviction is the foundation of proper tawakkul—reliance upon Allah ﷻ—and the key to inner peace regardless of our circumstances.

Ayah 28–30 · linked video: 08. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 28-30)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 28-30

Ayah 28

لَّا يَتَّخِذِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ ۖ وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ فِى شَىْءٍ إِلَّآ أَن تَتَّقُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ تُقَىٰةً ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ

After Allah ﷻ has expressed His power, His dominion, His ownership and our complete dependence on Him, the subject takes an unexpected turn. The ayah begins: لَّا يَتَّخِذِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ أَوْلِيَآءَ مِن دُونِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ - “Believers should not take disbelievers as awliya (protective friends) instead of the believers.”

The word أَوْلِيَآءَ is the plural of وَلِيّ, which means a protective friend - someone you can rely on in times of trouble. This ayah addresses a very real situation the early Muslims faced. Many of them had long-standing relationships with those who became disbelievers. This was their uncle, their cousin, their childhood friend. These were the people they grew up with, shared experiences with and trusted deeply. When someone moved to Madinah, they had lifelong relationships with Christians and Jews who had now become enemies of the Prophet ﷺ.

These bonds don’t disappear overnight. Friends share things they can’t discuss with parents or employers. When you’ve been friends for life, you open up to them, you expect their support. The Muslims are being told that while they can maintain courtesy and even friendliness with these individuals, they cannot rely on them for protection. They cannot look to them as their source of security.

The context is important to understand. The Muslims were still a growing entity in Madinah. Other groups around them were far more powerful. This ayah was revealed in the third year after hijrah, when Muslims were not yet fully established. Perhaps it might seem strategically advantageous to have some allies among these other groups. But Allah ﷻ is telling them: you need to understand that you shouldn’t look for any awliya other than Me.

وَمَن يَفْعَلْ ذَٰلِكَ فَلَيْسَ مِنَ ٱللَّهِ فِى شَىْءٍ - “And whoever does that, then he will find nothing from Allah.” Notice the remarkable style here. The ayah doesn’t say “he will get nothing from Allah” - it simply says “nothing from Allah” without mentioning the person. It’s as though Allah ﷻ is so displeased with this action that He doesn’t even dignify the person with mention in His words.

Then comes the exception: إِلَّآ أَن تَتَّقُوا۟ مِنْهُمْ تُقَىٰةً - “Except if you are trying to protect yourself from them out of taqiyah (protection).” The word تُقَىٰةً comes from the root وِقَايَة, which means protection. If you fear harm from them and need to maintain some relationship for your protection, this is permissible.

This is crucial to understand. The ayah negates taking them as awliya, but the exception indicates you can have some relationship with them if you fear they might harm you. This predominantly addresses what we might call international relations - peace treaties and diplomatic arrangements. This isn’t about your relationship with your non-Muslim neighbor or coworker.

The Sahaba (RA) maintained very cordial relationships with Christians and Jews throughout their lives. But this ayah addresses the situation where disbelievers are in a state of war with the Muslims. When believers are addressed collectively against disbelievers in this context, it’s speaking about Muslims versus their enemies in battle. In that state, personal relationships become complicated because individuals may have allegiances to their armies and could use those personal connections to spy on the Muslims.

If you are forced to have some relationship, it should be based on legitimate protection - treaties and formal agreements. This doesn’t mean befriending them to attack them later or using deception, because our religion is built on clarity and honesty. Some groups have principles that permit lying or deceiving non-believers for their cause, but Muslims don’t have that. This very surah will later address the claim that there’s no harm in committing crimes against “gentiles” or non-believers. How would we be any different if we adopted the same attitude?

وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ - “And Allah warns you of Himself.” If you’re so afraid of these disbelievers, perhaps you’re forgetting something. Allah ﷻ is putting things in perspective. The word يُحَذِّرُكُمُ comes from حَذَر, which specifically means fear of something impending - an immediate threat. It’s the kind of fear that makes you jump back when lightning strikes or when you hear a loud noise. Allah ﷻ is warning you of the immediate threat, which is His displeasure if you disobey Him.

وَإِلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْمَصِيرُ - “And to Allah alone is the final return.” The word ٱلْمَصِيرُ indicates an eventual return, like completing a long journey and finally arriving where you were meant to be all along. You can travel the world, do your thing, but at the end you’re going to come back to where you belong - back to Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 29

قُلْ إِن تُخْفُوا۟ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ أَوْ تُبْدُوهُ يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ ۗ وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ

قُلْ - “Say” - the Prophet ﷺ is commanded to tell them: إِن تُخْفُوا۟ مَا فِى صُدُورِكُمْ أَوْ تُبْدُوهُ - “If you hide whatever is in your chests or you expose it.” Notice the ayah says صُدُورِكُمْ (your chests) rather than قُلُوبِكُمْ (your hearts). The heart is called قَلْب, the thing itself. The chest is صَدْر, the place where the heart resides. The implication of using “chest” is that there’s a lot inside you that you’re hiding. The chest is bigger than the heart, suggesting you’ve got many things concealed within.

يَعْلَمْهُ ٱللَّهُ - “Allah already knows it.” Whether you hide it or expose it, Allah ﷻ will know it. This is a powerful reminder to the person who was told they could, for protection purposes, maintain some cordial relationship with disbelievers. If that relationship makes you feel so safe that you start fearing them instead of Allah ﷻ, or if you actually start taking them as awliya, then you’re not taking Allah ﷻ as the real authority and source of protection.

وَيَعْلَمُ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ - “And He knows whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth.” Whether you have that fear of Allah ﷻ in your heart or not, Allah ﷻ will know, because nothing gets hidden from Him.

وَٱللَّهُ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ - “And Allah has complete control over all things.” He has the power to bring about consequences for what He knows.

Ayah 30

يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوٓءٍ تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُۥٓ أَمَدًۢا بَعِيدًا ۗ وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ

يَوْمَ تَجِدُ كُلُّ نَفْسٍ - “The day on which every single person is going to find” مَّا عَمِلَتْ مِنْ خَيْرٍ مُّحْضَرًا - “whatever they did of any good, brought forth and presented.” This is such a beautiful phrase. The word مَا here is particularly significant. In Arabic, when you use الَّذِي it refers to something known and specific, but مَا refers to something unknown or general. The implication is profound: nobody remembers some of these good deeds - you don’t even remember them yourself, but Allah ﷻ remembers.

مِنْ خَيْرٍ - “of any kind of good deed.” You didn’t even realize it was a good deed. Someone walked into the masjid and you just smiled at them - that simple act will show up on Judgment Day. When you smiled at a believer, you fulfilled the Sunnah of the Prophet ﷺ. You walked into the masjid out of habit with your right foot, and that shows up too. Even things done accidentally, without conscious intention, if they were good, they’re recorded.

مُّحْضَرًا - “It will be brought forth, presented.” The word أَحْضَرَ means to make something present, to bring it forward. In modern Arabic, it means simply to bring, but the classical meaning is deeper - it means to make something stand, to present it formally. Your deeds are gathered and made to stand in front of you: this is all your stuff. The implication is powerful - you might think some of your deeds escaped notice, but no, it’s all been captured and brought before you.

وَمَا عَمِلَتْ مِن سُوٓءٍ - “And whatever they had done of any nasty deed.” The word سُوٓءٍ comes from a root that means a corpse. Evil deeds are called سُوٓءٍ because a corpse is nasty, ugly, hideous - something you don’t want to look at, something you want to cover up. These are ugly, nasty deeds, large and small. The stealing of the eyes - a glance where it shouldn’t be. A little snicker at someone. Something muttered under your breath. Words said with a seemingly innocent qualifier: “I’m not backbiting or anything, but that brother is a real problem.” Just because you said “I’m not backbiting” doesn’t erase the fact that you just backbit. The angels don’t write “he’s just saying” - they write what was actually said. All of it will come out. All of it will be presented.

تَوَدُّ لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُۥٓ أَمَدًۢا بَعِيدًا - “He will wish that between him and it there was an endless distance.” The word تَوَدُّ is important here. It doesn’t just mean “to wish” or “to want” - it means to love something intensely, to want it more than anything else. The word comes from وُدّ, which is a kind of love where all you think about is this one thing. It’s not a casual “out of sight, out of mind” kind of love, but an intense, consuming desire.

So at this moment, when the person is standing on Judgment Day and all their good deeds are brought forward, and all their bad deeds are brought forward - everything they remember and everything they don’t - there’s no escape. In modern courts, executives caught in scandals say “I don’t recall” as a defense. On Judgment Day, whether you recall or not is irrelevant. It’s on record. Here, let me remind you - it’s right here, all of it.

At that point, when he sees there’s no escape, he has one overwhelming wish: لَوْ أَنَّ بَيْنَهَا وَبَيْنَهُۥٓ أَمَدًۢا بَعِيدًا - “If only there was between him and it an endless distance.” Some scholars say the pronoun refers to all the sins, others say it refers to the person himself and the sins. Either way, the wish is the same.

Let’s examine the word أَمَدًا. This word is remarkable. In Arabic, you have units of time: minute, hour, day, year, decade, century, eon. The longest possible unit of time in Arabic is أَمَد - an endless, unimaginable expanse of time. When Arabs wanted to express “forever” in the most literal sense - not “it’ll take me forever to get to the masjid” (a 10-minute walk), but real, actual forever - they used أَمَد.

The person isn’t just wishing his sins were far away. He’s wishing there was an أَمَد - an endless distance that would take an endless amount of time to cross - just to reach them. His time is about to run out, and he’s desperately hoping that somehow the proceedings on Judgment Day will be delayed: “Wait, we need to get his file - it’s very far away, it’ll take a long time.” He wants to avoid dealing with the consequences of what he did.

بَعِيدًا - “Far away.” As if أَمَد itself isn’t far enough, Allah ﷻ adds بَعِيدًا. It’s like saying أَمَد isn’t sufficient - he wants that to be buried even further away.

وَيُحَذِّرُكُمُ ٱللَّهُ نَفْسَهُۥ - “And Allah warns you of Himself.” This is the second time this phrase appears in these ayahs. The previous ayah said don’t take disbelievers as guardians - you better watch out for Allah ﷻ. Now Allah ﷻ is explaining why you should watch out: this is what awaits on Judgment Day. This is the consequence of forgetting Who you should truly fear and rely upon.

After this terrifying image, after warning us and making our hearts tremble, Allah ﷻ mentions something extraordinary: وَٱللَّهُ رَءُوفٌۢ بِٱلْعِبَادِ - “And Allah is Ra’oof (Most Compassionate) with His slaves.”

This is one of the most beautiful Names of Allah ﷻ. Consider what رَءُوف means. When you see someone suffering - perhaps late at night you watch those appeals about starving children or people without clean water or those who are sick - your heart wrenches. You feel their pain and want to help them. That feeling, that urge to help when you see suffering, is رَأْفَة. Allah ﷻ is Ra’oof - He is incredibly compassionate and feels for His subjects. He knows what they’re going through.

Allah ﷻ is telling us: I am Ra’oof. I know what you’re going through. I feel for you. Think about your closest friends - the ones who can truly relate to you, who understand what you’re going through, who you can talk to without inhibitions. That lack of barrier makes you love them even more. When marriages struggle, it’s often because spouses can’t understand each other’s feelings anymore. “I talk to you, but I don’t think you understand what I’m saying. You don’t feel anything.” That disconnect creates distance.

But when you have someone who truly feels what you’re going through, who can help you through it, that in itself makes you love them. And when you’re going through difficult times, you naturally want to turn to them. Allah ﷻ is telling us through this Name that He knows what we’re going through. When someone realizes this about Allah ﷻ - that He knows their struggles, that He’s there for them - love is generated between them and their Lord.

Some of Allah’s Names make you fear Him. Some make you respect His power. Some make you appreciate His blessings. And some - like Ra’oof - simply make you want to love Him. This is one of those Names that draws your heart to Allah ﷻ, because He understands what you’re going through in a way that no one else can.

And it’s this realization - this knowledge that Allah ﷻ is both the One to be feared on Judgment Day and the One who feels compassion for our struggles - that should shape how we live our lives and where we place our trust.

Ayah 31 · linked video: 09. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 31)

Surah Aal-E-Imran, Ayah 31

قُلْ إِن كُنتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ ٱللَّهَ فَٱتَّبِعُونِى يُحْبِبْكُمُ ٱللَّهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

Say, “If you should love Allah, then follow me, [so] Allah will love you and forgive you your sins. And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful.”

This ayah establishes a profound principle: the inseparable connection between loving Allah ﷻ and following His Messenger ﷺ. Allah ﷻ instructs the Prophet ﷺ to tell the believers that if they truly love Allah ﷻ, they must demonstrate that love through following the Messenger ﷺ. This isn’t merely about obedience; it’s about love manifesting itself in action.

Two Levels of Following: Ita’ah and Ittiba

The Quran uses two distinct Arabic words for following: ita’ah and ittiba. While both appear throughout the Quran, this ayah specifically uses ittiba (“then follow me”). When two similar words appear together in the Quran, it highlights the difference between them. The next ayah will use ita’ah, bringing both concepts into focus.

Ita’ah represents the bare minimum expected of a Muslim. It means obeying the commands that come to you. When Allah ﷻ and His Messenger ﷺ tell you to do something, you do it. This is non-negotiable; failing to meet this standard constitutes disbelief, as the next ayah clarifies: “And obey Allah and the Messenger. But if they turn away, then indeed, Allah does not love the disbelievers.”

Ittiba, however, is a higher calling altogether. This word comes from the imagery of someone walking on the beach and leaving footprints behind. Someone following with ittiba tries to step in every single footstep left behind. You’re not waiting for instructions to come to you; you’re actively seeking them out. You’re going to the Messenger’s example and asking: How should I do this? What’s the best way? How can I do it in a way that pleases Allah ﷻ?

This is the difference: someone practicing ita’ah might eventually reach the same destination as the leader, even if they took different turns along the way. But someone practicing ittiba is meticulously following every step, every turn, every detail of the path. They ask: How should I get married? Where should I spend my money? What’s the best thing to do with my free time? How should I wake up? How should I go to sleep? How should I be when I’m angry? When I’m sad? In the morning? At night?

When it comes to love, Allah ﷻ doesn’t merely ask us to obey the Messenger ﷺ. He asks us to have ittiba of the Messenger ﷺ—to follow him precisely, lovingly, passionately.

The Promise: Allah’s Love in Return

The ayah then presents the magnificent promise: “Allah will love you.” Consider this carefully. You’re seeking to love Allah ﷻ, and in response, you get something far better—Allah ﷻ will love you. But notice what this reveals first and foremost: how much Allah ﷻ loves His Messenger ﷺ. He loves him so much that anyone even trying to be a little bit like him receives Allah’s love. It’s as if Allah ﷻ is saying, “I love this one because they’re trying to be like My beloved Messenger.”

People express their love for the Messenger ﷺ through poetry and devotional songs. But I tell you, nobody praises the Messenger of Allah ﷻ like Allah ﷻ Himself. If you truly want to learn how to praise the Messenger ﷺ, study the Quran. Who can do hamd (praise) like the One whose own name is Al-Hamid? It’s incredible.

Consider, for example, Surah Al-Ahzab where Allah ﷻ says: “Indeed, Allah and His angels send blessings upon the Prophet” (33:56). Stop right there. What’s left? Where do you go from there? Salutations are coming upon the Messenger ﷺ from Allah ﷻ and His angels. Not just “the angels” (al-mala’ikah), but “His angels” (mala’ikatahu)—the angels that He owns. Does that leave any angel out? Not a single angel in existence fails to send prayers upon the Messenger ﷺ. And then Allah ﷻ adds inna at the beginning—“Indeed, no doubt about it”—emphasizing the certainty of this reality.

Covering Your Sins

The ayah continues: “And He will forgive you your sins.” Out of that love for you, because you tried to follow the Prophet ﷺ, Allah ﷻ will do something more—He will cover up your sins.

Understand that we’ll never follow the Prophet ﷺ to perfection. We’ll fall way short. But just because you tried out of love, Allah ﷻ will take all those shortcomings and cover them up.

The word dhunub (sins) encompasses two kinds of imperfections. First, there are the flaws in our good deeds themselves. You’re standing in salah thinking about lunch. You’re memorizing Quran, but the congregation started before you finished your recitation, and you continued praying while unsure if you completed the required portion. You’re praying Asr, but you’re thinking about something else. You don’t even know if you made wudu properly. You forgot whether you prayed three or four rakats and gave yourself the benefit of the doubt.

Before we even start talking about actual bad deeds, our good deeds are full of flaws. These are also dhunub. Who’s going to pay for those? Who’s going to cover those imperfections?

Imagine on the Day of Judgment, you’re asked about a specific prayer—maybe Asr on a particular date at a particular masjid—and you think, “Oh my God, here we go. I remember now. I was thinking about something else. I’m not even sure if I prayed correctly.” Allah ﷻ will cover it up. In other words: “Okay, you got credit for that. Move on.”

You got credit? Why? Because Allah ﷻ appreciates that you tried to follow the Prophet ﷺ. So you got credit. It’s okay—full of flaws, but you’re covered.

Allah’s Constant Forgiveness and Mercy

The ayah concludes: “And Allah is Forgiving and Merciful” (Ghafur Rahim). This ending tells us that if you want Allah’s extreme forgiveness (al-Ghafur) and constant mercy (ar-Rahim), learn to be a follower of the Messenger of Allah ﷺ. Learn to be a lover of him.

The Balance Between Love and Obedience

The very next ayah states: “Say, ‘Obey Allah and the Messenger.’ But if they turn away, then indeed, Allah does not love the disbelievers.”

Notice the progression. The first ayah presented a high aspiration—ittiba, passionate following born from love. Now Allah ﷻ is saying: “Look, that’s a high aspiration, but let me tell you, there are no exceptions to the bare minimum. You better at least do ita’ah.”

This also addresses a dangerous phenomenon that will exist in the Ummah: people who claim to show love to the Prophet ﷺ while not obeying him. There will be people so moved by love for the Messenger ﷺ that they cry when they hear his name, yet you won’t find an ounce of obedience to him or implementation of Islam in their personal lives. True love of the Messenger ﷺ must manifest in following his example.

The Forgotten Sunnah: Character and Interactions

Among the forgotten aspects of following the Prophet ﷺ, perhaps the most neglected is his character in dealing with people. How did the Prophet ﷺ interact with his wife? With his parents? With his friends? With his enemies? With obnoxious people? With hypocrites? With troublemakers? With questioners? With ignorant people? With arrogant people? He dealt with people in such an incredible way that this alone is a study in and of itself.

Consider the story of a young Christian boy who came to the Prophet ﷺ after the Battle of Ta’if. The Prophet ﷺ had been beaten so severely that his sandals were stuck to his feet from dried blood. Picture yourself in that condition—exhausted, in pain. Most of us get irritated when interrupted during minor inconveniences. If you’re moving furniture and your child asks a question, you might say, “Can’t talk to you later? I’m breaking my back here. Hold on.”

But this child approached the injured Prophet ﷺ, helped him, and the Messenger ﷺ sat down and made small talk with him: “So where are you from? Oh, you’re from that tribe. Do you know my brother Yunus (AS)?” He engaged in simple, kind conversation. He didn’t say, “Leave me alone, child. Can you not bother me right now? I’m hurt.”

This is incredible—to not let your mood affect how you deal with people. You can be in a bad mood; that’s human. But to prevent that from affecting how you talk to people—that’s a sunnah, and it’s a forgotten one.

The Quran commands: “Speak to people nicely” (2:83). These aspects are often overlooked when we discuss following the Sunnah. People immediately jump to certain things—the beard, the miswak, modes of dress—all of which are beautiful and important. But before all of those external matters, there’s something else: character (akhlaq).

Character is fundamentally about your interactions with other people, your dealings with Allah’s creation. This is at the essence of what should be revived in us.

The Warning Against Turning Away

The ayah that follows warns: “But if they turn away, then indeed, Allah does not love the disbelievers (al-kafireen).”

The word kafireen means both “those who disbelieve” and “those who are ungrateful.” They’re ungrateful to Allah ﷻ and ungrateful that they received this amazing Messenger ﷺ—an opportunity to follow him that they didn’t seize. The statement “la yuhibb al-kafireen” (He does not love the disbelievers) implies someone else’s existence by negation. If Allah ﷻ doesn’t love them, then who does? Shaytaan does.

This ayah marks the conclusion of the first third of the first half of Surah Aal-E-Imran. May Allah ﷻ grant us the ability to love Him by following His Messenger ﷺ with sincerity, and may He cover our shortcomings with His infinite mercy.

Ayah 32–37 · linked video: 10. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 32-37)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 32-37

Ayah 32

قُلْ أَطِيعُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ وَٱلرَّسُولَ ۖ فَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُحِبُّ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ

Say: “Obey Allah ﷻ and the Messenger.” But if they turn away, then Allah ﷻ does not love the disbelievers.

The root letters of the word أَطِيعُوا (obey) are ط-و-ع, which literally mean to volunteer. This root carries within it the meaning of willing obedience. We see this same root in Surah Al-Baqarah when Allah ﷻ mentions voluntary fasting: “And whoever volunteers good, then it is better for him” (2:184). The very structure of this command implies that true obedience must come from a willing heart.

This understanding addresses an important question about faith and action. When someone fails to pray or misses their zakat, does this automatically make them a disbeliever? The scholars have debated this issue extensively, and we must understand the distinction carefully.

There is a fundamental difference between struggling with obedience and outright rejecting it. When someone says, “I know Allah ﷻ commanded prayer, but I have decided I don’t have to pray,” they have crossed a line. This is different from saying, “I know I must pray, but I’m struggling to get myself to wake up for Fajr.” The first person has rejected the authority of Allah’s command altogether, placing their personal whim above divine revelation. This constitutes an act of disbelief because they are unwilling to submit to what has been revealed.

The second person acknowledges the obligation but falls short in practice. The scholars would call such people sinful, weak in faith, or corrupt in their practice, but not necessarily disbelievers. They haven’t rejected Allah’s command; they recognize it but struggle to fulfill it. While these are serious shortcomings that require immediate correction, they don’t represent the complete rejection of willingness to obey that the ayah refers to.

The ayah states that if they turn away, “then Allah ﷻ does not love the disbelievers.” This turning away (تَوَلَّوْا۟) refers to a conscious rejection, not mere human weakness. Some may hide this rejection in their hearts, never openly declaring it, but Allah ﷻ tells us, “Whether you conceal what is in your hearts or reveal it, Allah ﷻ knows it” (3:29). What remains hidden may stay concealed until the Day of Judgment, when all that was internal becomes manifest.

Ayah 33

۞ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ءَادَمَ وَنُوحًا وَءَالَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَءَالَ عِمْرَٰنَ عَلَى ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Indeed, Allah ﷻ chose Adam, Nuh, the family of Ibrahim, and the family of Imran over all the worlds.

This ayah addresses a fundamental theological issue that was particularly relevant to the Christian delegation visiting the Prophet ﷺ. Christians developed a problematic concept around the idea of offspring, particularly regarding Isa (AS), whom they claimed to be the son of God. Allah ﷻ corrects this deviation by establishing that from Adam (AS) all the way down to the family of Imran, these honored individuals were human offspring, one generation coming from another.

The mention of آل (family) is significant. Allah ﷻ chose entire families and lineages: the family of Ibrahim (AS) and the family of Imran. This already implies and includes Isa (AS), who comes from this blessed lineage. He is part of this chain of human prophets, not divine offspring as Christians claim.

Ayah 34

ذُرِّيَّةًۢ بَعْضُهَا مِنۢ بَعْضٍ ۗ وَٱللَّهُ سَمِيعٌ عَلِيمٌ

A progeny, some of them from others. And Allah ﷻ is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

The word ذُرِّيَّةً (progeny/offspring) reinforces the point: these are human descendants, one generation coming from another in the natural way Allah ﷻ created for humanity. There is no divine parentage, no supernatural birth in the sense Christians claim. They are human beings chosen and honored by Allah ﷻ.

Allah ﷻ concludes this ayah by reminding us that He is السَّمِيعُ الْعَلِيمُ (All-Hearing, All-Knowing). This isn’t merely a beautiful name mentioned in passing. In the previous ayah, we were told theoretically that Allah ﷻ hears and knows all things. Now, in the very next ayah, we see a practical case study of what this means. Allah ﷻ heard a private prayer made centuries ago and preserved it in the Quran. He is about to tell us what the wife of Imran said in the privacy of her home, demonstrating that He truly hears everything.

Ayah 35

إِذْ قَالَتِ ٱمْرَأَتُ عِمْرَٰنَ رَبِّ إِنِّى نَذَرْتُ لَكَ مَا فِى بَطْنِى مُحَرَّرًا فَتَقَبَّلْ مِنِّىٓ ۖ إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ

When the wife of Imran said: “My Master, indeed I have vowed to You what is in my womb, dedicated entirely to Your service. So accept this from me. Indeed, You are the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.”

The phrase ٱمْرَأَتُ عِمْرَٰنَ (the woman of Imran) has been understood in different ways. One common interpretation is that this refers to the wife of a man named Imran. However, there’s another valid understanding: Imran was the father of Musa (AS), and this could refer to a woman from that blessed lineage. Just as we say “woman of” to indicate lineage or tribal affiliation in Arabic, this could mean a woman from the family line of Imran, a descendant from that honored prophetic heritage.

This kind of affiliation across generations is common in Hebrew tradition as well. Later in this surah, we’ll see people address Maryam (AS) as “sister of Harun.” She wasn’t literally Harun’s sister, but she belonged to that prophetic family. It’s a way of saying, “You come from that lineage, so don’t forget the standards and honor of that family.”

Whether this refers to the immediate wife of a man named Imran or a woman from the lineage of Imran, Allah ﷻ knows best. Personally, I find the historical lineage interpretation compelling, though both views have merit.

She makes this supplication privately in her home: “My Master” - رَبِّ - she calls upon Allah ﷻ with this intimate, personal term. She’s pregnant, and she makes a نَذْر (vow) to Allah ﷻ. The word نَذَرْتُ comes from a root meaning to dedicate or devote something specifically to Allah ﷻ. It’s when you set something aside exclusively for His sake.

In Islamic law, you can make nadhr of property - dedicating land for a masjid, for example, or setting aside money for charity. Once you’ve made that nadhr, that dedication becomes binding. This sister made nadhr of something unique: the child in her womb. “Whatever is in my belly,” she says, “I dedicate مُحَرَّرًا - freed, liberated entirely for Your service.”

The word مُحَرَّرًا comes from the root ح-ر-ر, related to freedom. She’s saying, “This child will be free from all worldly obligations - free to serve Your house alone.” In Jerusalem, at Masjid Al-Aqsa, there were people called the Zuhar who lived in the masjid, dedicated to its service. They would clean it, maintain it, teach people, and spend their time in worship and study. This was considered one of the greatest honors - to be a servant of Allah’s house. She wanted her child to have this honor, to be like the imam or caretaker of the masjid, freed from farming, business, and all other worldly pursuits.

Notice that she uses the masculine form here (مُحَرَّرًا), clearly expecting a boy. In that time and place, only males could take on this role of living in and serving the masjid due to the requirements of gender separation and the physical demands of the work.

Then she says فَتَقَبَّلْ مِنِّىٓ - “So accept from me.” It’s beautiful that she doesn’t say “accept him from me” or “accept this child from me.” She says “accept from me.” Her focus is on her own intention and sincerity. She hasn’t even given birth yet - she doesn’t know if the baby will be healthy or if there will be complications. But her sincere intention, her heartfelt nadhr, this devotion itself - that’s what she wants Allah ﷻ to accept and reward her for.

She concludes by echoing what Allah ﷻ just told us about Himself: إِنَّكَ أَنتَ ٱلسَّمِيعُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ - “Indeed, You are the All-Hearing, the All-Knowing.” Allah ﷻ told us He’s As-Samee’ Al-’Aleem in the previous ayah, and now we see a case study. She made this prayer privately, quietly, in her home. Yet Allah ﷻ heard it so completely that He recorded it in the Quran for all of humanity until the Day of Judgment. This is what it means that He is All-Hearing, All-Knowing.

Ayah 36

فَلَمَّا وَضَعَتْهَا قَالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّى وَضَعْتُهَآ أُنثَىٰ وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا وَضَعَتْ وَلَيْسَ ٱلذَّكَرُ كَٱلْأُنثَىٰ ۖ وَإِنِّىٓ سَمَّيْتُهَا مَرْيَمَ وَإِنِّىٓ أُعِيذُهَا بِكَ وَذُرِّيَّتَهَا مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ

But when she delivered her, she said: “My Master, I have delivered a female.” And Allah ﷻ knew best what she delivered. “And the male is not like the female. And I have named her Maryam, and I seek Your protection for her and her offspring from Shaytan, the outcast.”

In Arabic, there are two main verbs for childbirth: حَمَلَ and وَضَعَ. The verb وَضَعَ literally means to put something down, to lay down a burden. When a mother gives birth, she’s finally relieved of the weight she’s been carrying - she puts that burden down. This is the verb used here: وَضَعَتْهَا - “when she delivered her.”

When the baby was born, she said رَبِّ إِنِّى وَضَعْتُهَآ أُنثَىٰ - “My Master, I have delivered a female.” The emphasis here is profound. She doesn’t just say “I delivered a girl.” She says إِنِّى - “Indeed, I” - emphasizing herself as the subject, and then وَضَعْتُهَآ أُنثَىٰ - “I have delivered her, a female.” The structure emphasizes both her role and the unexpected outcome.

Then Allah ﷻ interjects with His own commentary: وَٱللَّهُ أَعْلَمُ بِمَا وَضَعَتْ - “And Allah ﷻ knew best what she delivered.” This statement is remarkable. She knew what she had given birth to when the baby came. But Allah ﷻ knew before the child was in her womb. He knew before she made her supplication. This phrase carries multiple meanings: “Allah ﷻ knows better than you the value of what you’ve been given. Allah ﷻ knows better what purpose this child will serve. No boy could have been like this girl you’ve been blessed with.”

Then she says, وَلَيْسَ ٱلذَّكَرُ كَٱلْأُنثَىٰ - “And the male is not like the female.” Some scholars say this is her statement, expressing her concern: “A boy could have served in the masjid, but a girl has limitations due to hijab and the requirements of gender separation. How will I fulfill my vow?”

Other scholars suggest this could be Allah’s statement, meaning: “The male is not like this female - no boy could achieve what this girl will achieve.” Given that Allah ﷻ just interjected with His own commentary, this interpretation holds weight. Either way, the reality is that no male prophet has the unique distinction that Maryam (AS) has. An entire surah of the Quran is named after her - Surah Maryam. Allah ﷻ chose her above all the women of all the worlds. No male would have been like her.

The mother continues: وَإِنِّىٓ سَمَّيْتُهَا مَرْيَمَ - “And I have named her Maryam.” There’s a beautiful lesson here about naming children. You can give your children good names that aren’t necessarily Arabic or directly from the Quran. Maryam is a beautiful name, but it wasn’t an “Islamic” name in the sense we sometimes think about it today. It was simply a good name from her culture and language.

This addresses a misconception some Muslims have developed. When people embrace Islam later in life, they sometimes feel pressured to change their names to Arabic names. If someone’s name is Joseph, they feel they should change it to Yusuf. But look at the Companions (RA) - Salman Al-Farsi (RA) kept his Persian name. Suhayb Ar-Rumi (RA) kept his Roman name. The Prophet ﷺ only changed names that had explicit shirk or negative meanings associated with them.

If your name is Christian or Christopher, perhaps consider changing it due to its direct religious connotation. If your name contains explicit shirk - like some names that mean “servant of” a false deity - then yes, change it. But if you have a neutral name from your culture, there’s no obligation to Arabize it. Islam is universal, and that universality extends to the names we carry.

The mother then makes a powerful supplication: وَإِنِّىٓ أُعِيذُهَا بِكَ وَذُرِّيَّتَهَا - “And I seek Your protection for her and her offspring.” The verb أُعِيذُ is the causative form of seeking refuge. She’s not just seeking refuge herself; she’s actively placing her daughter and her future descendants under Allah’s protection.

Look at her foresight - she’s thinking generations ahead. The baby has just been born, and she’s already praying for children who haven’t even been conceived yet. She doesn’t even specify how many generations - ذُرِّيَّتَهَا could mean her children, grandchildren, and all descendants. This is the mindset of a believer: thinking not just about immediate concerns but about the long-term spiritual protection of one’s lineage.

She seeks protection مِنَ ٱلشَّيْطَـٰنِ ٱلرَّجِيمِ - “from Shaytan, the outcast.” The word ٱلرَّجِيمِ comes from the root meaning to pelt or stone. In Surah Al-Kahf, when the people of the town threaten the people of the cave, they say: “If they discover you, they will stone you” - يَرْجُمُوكُمْ (18:20). Stoning was a form of execution, a way of showing utter condemnation and curse.

So Ash-Shaytaan Ar-Rajeem means “the pelted devil” or “the condemned devil” - the one who is constantly pelted, cursed, and driven away. We fulfill this meaning every time we say أَعُوذُ بِاللَّهِ مِنَ الشَّيْطَانِ الرَّجِيمِ. In the unseen realm, he’s being pelted. And during Hajj, we physically enact this pelting at the Jamarat, symbolically rejecting Shaytan.

The word شَيْطَان itself comes from roots meaning someone overwhelmed with rage and frustration - a being consumed by anger and hatred toward humanity. This mother sought Allah’s protection for her daughter and all her descendants from this enemy, and Allah ﷻ answered her prayer in ways she could never have imagined.

Ayah 37

فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنۢبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا ٱلْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا ۖ قَالَ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

So her Master accepted her with good acceptance and caused her to grow in a good manner, and Zakariyya took charge of her. Every time Zakariyya entered upon her in the prayer chamber, he found with her provision. He said: “O Maryam, from where is this coming to you?” She said: “It is from Allah ﷻ. Indeed, Allah ﷻ provides for whom He wills without account.”

Allah ﷻ responded to the mother’s prayer: فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ - “So her Master accepted her with good acceptance.” Now there’s a question: does “her” refer to the mother or the daughter? The beauty of the Arabic is that it applies to both. Allah ﷻ accepted the mother’s sincere supplication and intention, and He accepted the daughter Maryam (AS) herself. The phrase بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ - “with good acceptance” - emphasizes that this wasn’t just acceptance, but the best, most beautiful form of acceptance possible.

Then Allah ﷻ says: وَأَنۢبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا - “And caused her to grow in a good manner.” The verb أَنۢبَتَ means to cause something to sprout or grow, like a farmer nurturing a plant. This is a powerful metaphor. When a farmer plants a seed and tends to it - providing water, sunlight, good soil, removing weeds - he’s doing everything right. But does that guarantee the plant will grow properly? No. At the end of the day, the plant itself must respond to that care. The seed must be receptive. The plant must take in the water and nutrients.

Think of a teacher-student relationship. A teacher can do everything possible - prepare excellent lessons, give individual attention, go out of his way to help. But if the student refuses to learn, refuses to apply himself, the teacher’s efforts won’t bear fruit. The same applies to parenting. Ya’qub (AS) was an amazing father, a prophet of Allah ﷻ. He did his part beautifully. Yet when his sons initially went astray, who is to blame? Not the parent who did everything right, but the children who didn’t take advantage of that upbringing (though they later repented and reformed).

If Allah ﷻ only wanted to describe His role, He would have said أَنۢبَتَهَا إِنۢبَاتًا حَسَنًا - using the verbal noun form. But He says نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا instead - using the noun for the plant itself, the growth itself. This brilliantly captures both dimensions: Allah ﷻ provided the perfect conditions for her growth, AND she herself responded beautifully to that nurturing. She took full advantage of every opportunity. The eloquence captures both Allah’s favor and her own excellent character and effort.

Then: وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا - “And Zakariyya took charge of her.” There’s a beautiful protection of honor in how this is phrased. In Arabic, there are different forms for taking charge. There’s كَفَلَ (he took charge) and there’s كَفَّلَ (he was made to take charge, or he undertook extensive care).

If the ayah said كَفَلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا, it would simply mean Zakariyya took charge of her on his own initiative. But كَفَّلَهَا can mean two things: either Zakariyya went far above and beyond in caring for her continuously and extensively, OR Allah ﷻ Himself placed her in Zakariyya’s care. Both meanings are valid, and both are true.

Why does this matter? In some Orientalist and even some Christian traditions, there have been terrible accusations made against prophets, including inappropriate suggestions about Zakariyya (AS) and Maryam (AS). The way Allah ﷻ phrases this protects Zakariyya’s integrity completely. The subject of the verb can be understood as Allah ﷻ - meaning Allah ﷻ Himself placed Maryam in Zakariyya’s guardianship. Just as Allah ﷻ was the subject of أَنۢبَتَهَا (He caused her to grow), He’s also the one who made Zakariyya her guardian.

This is similar to another protective phrase in the Quran. When defending Isa (AS) against accusations, the Quran has him declare: وَبَرًّۢا بِوَٰلِدَتِى - “And dutiful to my mother” (19:32). This directly refutes traditions that suggested Isa (AS) was harsh or disrespectful to his mother. No - he declares himself that Allah ﷻ made him good to his mother.

Zakariyya (AS) was Maryam’s maternal uncle (her khaal in Arabic). He took complete responsibility for her upbringing and care, ensuring she had everything she needed for her spiritual development.

The ayah continues: كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا ٱلْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا - “Every time Zakariyya entered upon her in the prayer chamber, he found with her provision.” The word مِحْرَاب refers to a prayer chamber or sanctuary - a private place dedicated to worship. Maryam (AS) devoted herself to worship in this special place, and every time her uncle came to check on her, he found rizq (provision) with her - food that he hadn’t brought, that no one had delivered through normal means.

He asked: يَـٰمَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَـٰذَا - “O Maryam, from where is this coming to you?” The word أَنَّىٰ means “from where” or “how.” He’s asking about the source of this provision.

Her response is simple and profound: هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ - “It is from Allah ﷻ.” The word عِندِ means “with” or “from the presence of” - it indicates that this provision comes directly from Allah’s presence, not through normal worldly means.

Then she adds a principle that would echo through the ages: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ - “Indeed, Allah ﷻ provides for whom He wills without account.” The phrase بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ means “without calculation, without measure, without limit.” When Allah ﷻ provides, He doesn’t calculate like we do. He doesn’t count and ration. His provision is limitless, beyond our comprehension.

This wasn’t just a statement about food appearing miraculously. It was a declaration of faith, a principle of tawakkul (reliance on Allah ﷻ). This young girl, dedicated to Allah’s worship, understood something deep: when you devote yourself to Allah ﷻ, He takes care of your needs in ways you cannot imagine. Her mother had made nadhr, dedicating her to Allah’s service, worried about how a girl could fulfill this role. But Allah ﷻ accepted that dedication completely, nurtured Maryam (AS) perfectly, placed her in the care of a prophet, and provided for her directly from His presence.

This is the fruit of sincere devotion. This is what happens when someone is truly dedicated to Allah ﷻ - He takes care of them in ways that transcend normal worldly means. And this miracle that Zakariyya (AS) witnessed would inspire his own supplication, which we’ll see in the verses that follow.

Ayah 37–44 · linked video: 11. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 37-44)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 37-44

Ayah 37

فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا بِقَبُولٍ حَسَنٍ وَأَنۢبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا وَكَفَّلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا ۖ كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا ٱلْمِحْرَابَ وَجَدَ عِندَهَا رِزْقًا ۖ قَالَ يَـٰمَرْيَمُ أَنَّىٰ لَكِ هَـٰذَا ۖ قَالَتْ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ

Her Lord accepted her with gracious acceptance and caused her to grow in a good manner and put her in the care of Zakariya. Every time Zakariya entered upon her in the prayer chamber, he found with her provision. He said, “O Maryam, from where is this [coming] to you?” She said, “It is from Allah. Indeed, Allah provides for whom He wills without account.”

Allah’s Perfect Nurturing of Maryam

When we examine the phrase نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا (grew in a good manner), we discover a beautiful implication. Allah ﷻ made sure that Maryam grew up enthusiastic and obedient to worship. She was very proactive and constantly engaged in worship, even as a young child. This wasn’t merely about physical growth, but about spiritual cultivation. She did what she was supposed to do all the time, remaining constantly engaged in acts of devotion.

The Miraculous Provision

Every time Prophet Zakariya (AS) would enter upon her in the mihrab (prayer chamber), he would find rizq (provision) with her. Allah ﷻ doesn’t specify exactly what this provision was, but some scholars of Tafsir tell us these were fruits from out of season. This wasn’t like having access to modern refrigeration or international shipping. This was truly miraculous - fresh fruits without refrigeration, artificial hormones, or preservatives. Fruits have a very small lifespan, and in that time and place, obtaining out-of-season produce would require months of travel, making preservation nearly impossible.

When Prophet Zakariya (AS) sees this repeatedly, he asks, “Ya Maryam, anna laki hadha?” The word أَنَّىٰ here is significant - it means “how come you have this?” or “where did you get this from?” It’s a question born of genuine amazement at something that defies natural explanation.

The Eloquence of Maryam’s Response

Notice Maryam’s beautiful response. She doesn’t simply say “min Allah” (from Allah). Instead, she says هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ - “It is especially from Allah.” The word عِندِ shows respect and indicates special proximity to Allah ﷻ. This word choice elevates the response from merely identifying the source to acknowledging the special nature of this divine provision.

Even more remarkable is her use of the word هُوَ (it). In English, there’s a difference between saying “this is a gift” and “it is a gift.” When you say “this is a gift,” you’ve pointed at something limited and specific. But when you say “it is a gift,” you’ve made it far more comprehensive than what meets the eye. It’s as if she’s saying: this fruit you see now, and the provision from the time before, and the time before that, and whatever other blessings you observe - the roof over my head, the health I have, the clothes on my back - all of this is especially from Allah ﷻ.

Then she adds a principle that would inspire Prophet Zakariya (AS) to make his own dua: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَآءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ - “Indeed, Allah provides for whom He wills without account.” Without any audit, without any restriction or limitation. This young girl is reminding a prophet, a scholar, the imam of the masjid, the one who regularly reminds others, about Allah’s limitless provision.

The Greatness of Prophets in Receiving Reminder

This demonstrates something beautiful about the character of prophets - they’re not always in the position of giving reminder. They are always ready to take reminder as well. The moment Prophet Zakariya (AS) takes this reminder, that Allah ﷻ provides without limitation, he thinks of making dua for himself. He’s an old man at this point, without children. But if Allah ﷻ can bring fruits out of season, why can’t He grant a child in old age?

This is why the very next word in the Quran is هُنَالِكَ - “right then and there.” There’s no gap, no passage of time. The reminder goes straight to his heart, and immediately inspires him to turn to Allah ﷻ in supplication.

Ayah 38

هُنَالِكَ دَعَا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُۥ ۖ قَالَ رَبِّ هَبْ لِى مِن لَّدُنكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً ۖ إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ

At that, Zakariya called upon his Lord, saying, “My Lord, grant me from Yourself a good offspring. Indeed, You are the Hearer of supplication.”

The Immediate Impact of Maryam’s Words

The word هُنَالِكَ at the beginning of this ayah is significant - it means “right then and there.” Allah ﷻ uses this word to show us that the moment Prophet Zakariya (AS) heard that reminder from Maryam about Allah’s limitless provision, he was immediately inspired to make dua. He thought to himself: she can get rizq like that, then I should make dua to my Master.

The Beauty of His Supplication

Look at how he makes his request: رَبِّ هَبْ لِى - “My Lord, give me a gift.” The word هِبَة in Arabic means a gift. He’s not asking for something he’s earned or deserves - he’s asking for a pure gift from Allah ﷻ. Then he adds مِن لَّدُنكَ - “from especially You,” using a word that indicates special divine proximity, just as Maryam had used ’indi Allah.

He asks for ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً - “a good offspring.” The word dhurriyyah implies not only that the child himself should be good, but that his lineage, if there is one, should also be good and pure. This is a comprehensive request for goodness across generations.

The Foundation of His Confidence

Prophet Zakariya (AS) ends his dua by saying إِنَّكَ سَمِيعُ ٱلدُّعَآءِ - “Indeed, You are the Hearer of supplication.” This entire conversation is essentially a practical thesis on the divine attribute mentioned earlier: إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَمِيعٌ بَصِيرٌ - “Indeed, Allah is Hearing and Seeing.” Allah ﷻ sees everything and knows everything. He knew what was in the womb when Maryam’s mother made her vow. He heard that dua. He knows where this miraculous provision came from. He knows exactly what Maryam felt in her heart when she gave her answer. And now He hears the dua of Zakariya (AS). This divine attribute of hearing permeates the entire narrative.

Ayah 39

فَنَادَتْهُ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةُ وَهُوَ قَآئِمٌ يُصَلِّى فِى ٱلْمِحْرَابِ أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ مُصَدِّقًۢا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ وَسَيِّدًا وَحَصُورًا وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ

So the angels called him while he was standing in prayer in the chamber, [saying], “Indeed, Allah gives you good tidings of Yahya, confirming a word from Allah and [who will be] honorable, abstaining [from women], and a prophet from among the righteous.”

The Immediate Response

The فَ at the beginning - “then” - creates a sense of immediacy. It’s as though no time passed at all. The answer came swiftly. One moment he’s making dua, and the next moment angels are calling him while he’s standing in prayer in the mihrab.

The Significance of His State

Allah ﷻ tells us specifically what Prophet Zakariya (AS) was doing when the angels came: وَهُوَ قَآئِمٌ يُصَلِّى فِى ٱلْمِحْرَابِ (while he was standing in prayer in the chamber). Why mention this detail? It wasn’t necessary to tell us what he was doing when the angels arrived. But Allah ﷻ highlights it to teach us something important: when you make dua, what should you do afterwards? Engage in Salah. Continue standing before Allah ﷻ.

The door was answered because Prophet Zakariya (AS) made his request and then remained in the best position to receive a gift. The best position to receive a gift from the King is to be standing humbly in front of the King. That’s what Salah is - standing before Allah ﷻ with complete humility and devotion.

The Good News of Yahya

The angels announce: أَنَّ ٱللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ - “Indeed, Allah gives you good tidings of Yahya.” The name يَحْيَىٰ literally comes from the verb حَيَّ, which means “to live” or “he will be given life.” This is deeply significant. Prophet Zakariya (AS) had just described himself as close to death - his hair had turned white like ash, burned away by age. And now Allah ﷻ gives him a child whose very name signifies life.

In Surah Maryam, we learn that Allah ﷻ says: لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا - “We have not assigned to anyone before this name” (19:7). No one had been named Yahya before. This was a completely new name, just as this was a completely miraculous birth.

The Mission of Yahya

The angels describe several qualities of this blessed child:

مُصَدِّقًۢا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ ٱللَّهِ - “Confirming a word from Allah.” His primary mission will be to confirm and strengthen “a word from Allah.” This word refers to Prophet Isa (AS), who we’ll later learn is called كَلِمَة (the Word of Allah). The job of Prophet Yahya (AS) will be to assist the mission of Prophet Isa (AS). So the good news isn’t just that Prophet Zakariya (AS) will have a righteous child - this child will have a tremendous mission in life.

وَسَيِّدًا - “And honorable/a leader.” The word سَيِّد comes from a root meaning to become commonplace or well-known. He will have a charismatic personality that draws people to him. When everybody knows you and respects you, you naturally become a leader.

وَحَصُورًا - “And abstaining [from women].” The word حَصُور comes from حَصْر, which means exclusivity or restriction. It implies someone who will be reclusive, who will stay to himself. Prophet Yahya (AS) will have no interest in marriage or women. He will remain completely focused on his mission, never even looking at women with desire.

This level of self-control is extraordinary. Human nature is such that even when two men are having a serious conversation about religion, if a woman walks by, there’s an almost involuntary moment where the eyes are drawn. It’s pre-programmed into human nature. But Prophet Yahya (AS) will have such complete mastery over this natural inclination that he won’t even experience that initial moment. This is the meaning of hasur.

وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ - “And a prophet from among the righteous.” He will be counted among the great prophets, numbered among the most righteous of people. The word نَبِيّ (prophet) is different from رَسُول (messenger), a distinction we’ll explore more later in the Quran.

Ayah 40

قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ وَٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرٌ ۖ قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ ٱللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ

He said, “My Lord, how will I have a boy when I have reached old age and my wife is barren?” He said, “Such is Allah; He does what He wills.”

The Question of Amazement

Prophet Zakariya (AS) responds: رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِى غُلَـٰمٌ - “My Lord, how will I have a boy?” Notice he uses the word غُلَـٰم rather than وَلَد. Why this specific choice? A ghulam is not just any boy or child - it’s a boy old enough to be active, to play, to help out. It’s a boy who has reached a certain level of maturity and capability.

This is actually quite eloquent, because the angels had just told him that his son would grow up to become a prophet who assists another prophet. For that mission, the child would need to reach at least young adulthood. Prophet Zakariya (AS) is essentially acknowledging: “I understand he’ll grow to that age and fulfill this mission, but how will this even begin when I’m already so old?”

The Two Obstacles

He mentions two barriers to this miracle: وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِىَ ٱلْكِبَرُ - “And I have reached old age.” The word بَلَغَ means to reach or arrive at something. He’s saying, “I’ve already arrived at advanced age, at the stage of maturity that leads to decline.”

Then he adds: وَٱمْرَأَتِى عَاقِرٌ - “And my wife is barren.” The word عَاقِر has a darker, more emphatic tone than other words for barrenness. She couldn’t bear children even when she was young. Now she has two reasons not to conceive: her original infertility, and her advanced age.

Interestingly, in Surah Maryam, where this story is also told, Prophet Zakariya (AS) mentions three obstacles instead of two, but here he focuses on just these two central impossibilities.

Allah’s Simple Response

The response is beautifully concise: كَذَٰلِكَ ٱللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَآءُ - “Such is Allah; He does what He wills.” The verb used here is يَفْعَلُ (He does/He acts). Keep this verb in mind, as we’ll see a slight but significant variation in the next ayah.

This response echoes Maryam’s words from before: Allah ﷻ provides for whom He wills without limitation. Now we see the broader principle: Allah ﷻ does whatever He wills. Age, biological barriers, natural law - none of these constrain the will of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 41

قَالَ رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً ۖ قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا ۗ وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا وَسَبِّحْ بِٱلْعَشِىِّ وَٱلْإِبْكَـٰرِ

He said, “My Lord, make for me a sign.” He Said, “Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three days except by gesture. And remember your Lord much and exalt [Him] in the evening and the morning.”

The Request for a Sign

Prophet Zakariya (AS) asks: رَبِّ ٱجْعَل لِّىٓ ءَايَةً - “My Lord, make for me a sign.” Notice something important here: the angels had delivered the news to him, but he doesn’t respond to the angels. He doesn’t turn around to them and continue the conversation with them. They came to speak to him on behalf of Allah ﷻ, but Prophet Zakariya (AS) knows he can speak to Allah ﷻ directly. He was already talking to Allah ﷻ when the angels arrived, and there’s no reason to stop now. So he continues his direct conversation with his Lord.

He’s asking for an ayah - a sign that will confirm this miracle is truly beginning. He wants something tangible to mark the start of this impossible blessing.

The Sign: Silence

Allah ﷻ responds directly to him (not through the angels this time): قَالَ ءَايَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ ٱلنَّاسَ ثَلَـٰثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا - “Your sign is that you will not speak to the people for three days except by gesture.”

The word used here is تُكَلِّمَ, which is more specific than just “speak.” كَلَّمَ means to address somebody in a way where you take a dominant or significant role in the conversation. It’s directed speech where you have an active part. Allah ﷻ will temporarily take away his ability to have these directed conversations with people. He’ll only be able to make رَمْز - gestures, signs, indications.

The Command to Remember

Then comes the beautiful instruction: وَٱذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا - “And remember your Lord much.” Make abundant dhikr of Allah ﷻ.
