Historical examples of such tampering include the alteration of “Bakkah” (Makkah) mentioned in the Torah to “Paran” or another desert location, obscuring the clear reference to Makkah. In modern times, this manipulation continues through translation. Consider the prophecy: “I will raise up for them a prophet from among their brethren.” The word “brethren” referred to both the descendants of Ishaq (AS) and Ismail (AS), yet many contemporary translations insert “from among the Israelites,” an addition not present in the original text. When you examine multiple Bible translations, you’ll find that the majority have inserted this phrase to narrow the prophecy’s scope.

لِتَحْسَبُوهُ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ —so that you might think it’s from the Book. وَمَا هُوَ مِنَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ —but it is not from the Book at all. The repetition emphasizes the deception. They present their alterations with confidence, claiming وَيَقُولُونَ هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ —“this is from Allah.” But the ayah firmly refutes this: وَمَا هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ ٱللَّهِ —it is absolutely not from Allah.

The ayah concludes with a particularly damning observation: وَيَقُولُونَ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ وَهُمْ يَعْلَمُونَ —they attribute lies to Allah while they are fully aware. This isn’t ignorance or mistake. They know exactly what they’re doing. Their deception is conscious, calculated and deliberate.

Ayah 79

مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا۟ عِبَادًا لِّى مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ وَلَـٰكِن كُونُوا۟ رَبَّـٰنِيِّـۧنَ بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ

This ayah addresses a fundamental principle about prophets and revelation. مَا كَانَ لِبَشَرٍ —it is not befitting for any mortal being. The word بَشَر literally comes from بَشَرَة , meaning skin. Human beings are called bashar because their skin is visible, unlike animals covered in fur. In English, we might say “flesh and blood” to capture this sense of mortal humanity.

أَن يُؤْتِيَهُ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَٱلْحُكْمَ وَٱلنُّبُوَّةَ —that Allah would give him the Book, wisdom and prophethood. The word الْحُكْمَ encompasses both wisdom and firmness of judgment—the ability to discern truth and act upon it with conviction. When someone receives these tremendous gifts from Allah ﷻ, what would their message be?

ثُمَّ يَقُولَ لِلنَّاسِ كُونُوا۟ عِبَادًا لِّى مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ —then he would say to people, “Be worshipers to me instead of Allah”? This is unthinkable. No genuine prophet would ever make such a claim.

وَلَـٰكِن كُونُوا۟ رَبَّـٰنِيِّـۧنَ —rather, he would say, “Be godly people.” The term رَبَّانِيِّين derives from رَبّ —Lord. It describes those who are devoted to their Lord, who embody divine qualities of mercy, justice and wisdom in their character. These are people deeply conscious of their servitude to Allah ﷻ alone.

بِمَا كُنتُمْ تُعَلِّمُونَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ وَبِمَا كُنتُمْ تَدْرُسُونَ —because you teach the Book and because you study it. Here lies a crucial warning: those who teach and study sacred scripture should be the most godly of all people. Their proximity to divine revelation should elevate them spiritually.

However, this ayah also contains an implicit caution. There exists a real danger that those who teach and learn the Book might fail to embody its message. They may become experts in vocabulary, grammar and rhetoric. They might memorize passages, deliver eloquent lectures and impress audiences with their knowledge. Yet their own lives might remain hollow, disconnected from the very truths they articulate. This is the spiritual pitfall that the Quran identifies: يَأْمُرُونَ ٱلنَّاسَ بِٱلْبِرِّ وَتَنسَوْنَ أَنفُسَكُمْ (they command people to righteousness while forgetting themselves).

The one who teaches and studies the Book should be closest to Allah ﷻ precisely because they engage most deeply with His Word. This engagement should transform them into rabbaniyyeen—people of profound godliness who live what they teach.

Ayah 80

وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ أَرْبَابًا ۗ أَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ

This ayah continues the theme established in the previous passage about the impossibility of a true prophet calling to shirk. وَلَا يَأْمُرَكُمْ أَن تَتَّخِذُوا۟ ٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةَ وَٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ أَرْبَابًا —nor would he command you to take angels and prophets as lords. The word أَرْبَابًا is the plural of رَبّ, emphasizing the worship of multiple entities instead of the One True Lord.

The rhetorical question that follows is devastating in its clarity: أَيَأْمُرُكُم بِٱلْكُفْرِ بَعْدَ إِذْ أَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ —would he command you to disbelief after you have become Muslims?

This question carries profound implications. The Banu Israel at the time of Musa (AS) were Muslims—they had submitted to Allah ﷻ. The notion that a prophet would come and teach them shirk after they had already embraced tawheed is absurd. Yet this is precisely what some claimed about Isa (AS)—that he called people to worship him alongside or instead of Allah ﷻ. This ayah categorically refutes such claims. No messenger would ever undo submission to Allah ﷻ by calling to its opposite.

Ayah 81

وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ لَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَـٰبٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِۦ وَلَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ ۚ قَالَ ءَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِى ۖ قَالُوٓا۟ أَقْرَرْنَا ۚ قَالَ فَٱشْهَدُوا۟ وَأَنَا۠ مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ

This ayah reveals a covenant made with all the prophets—a binding commitment that transcends individual prophethood. وَإِذْ أَخَذَ ٱللَّهُ مِيثَـٰقَ ٱلنَّبِيِّـۧنَ —and remember when Allah took the covenant of the prophets. The word مِيثَـٰق refers to a comprehensive agreement where both parties fully understand the terms—there is no fine print, no hidden conditions.

The covenant’s content is remarkable: لَمَآ ءَاتَيْتُكُم مِّن كِتَـٰبٍ وَحِكْمَةٍ ثُمَّ جَآءَكُمْ رَسُولٌ مُّصَدِّقٌ لِّمَا مَعَكُمْ —“Whatever Book and wisdom I give you, then there comes to you a messenger confirming what is with you…”

The commitment demanded is absolute: لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ بِهِۦ وَلَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ. The structure of these words is significant. The normal command form for “believe” would be آمِنُوا. The future tense would be تُؤْمِنُونَ. But here we find لَتُؤْمِنُنَّ—with both the emphatic lam at the beginning and the emphatic noon at the end. This construction leaves no room for choice or hesitation: “You absolutely will believe in him, without question.”

Similarly, لَتَنصُرُنَّهُۥ —“You absolutely will support him.” Not only must they believe, but they must also become his helpers, his aides. Even as prophets themselves, they would take a secondary role to support this coming messenger.

This covenant has profound implications for the rabbis of Banu Israel who rejected the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. Some scholars, like al-Zamakhshari, understood this covenant to apply specifically to the Final Messenger. Even if earlier prophets didn’t know exactly when the last messenger would come, they understood that should he arrive in their lifetime, they must believe in and support him. This was a commitment made by all prophets.

The weight of this covenant is emphasized: قَالَ ءَأَقْرَرْتُمْ وَأَخَذْتُمْ عَلَىٰ ذَٰلِكُمْ إِصْرِى —He said, “Do you affirm this? And do you take up My covenant—My heavy burden—in this regard?” The word إِصْر refers to a serious, weighty obligation.

قَالُوٓا۟ أَقْرَرْنَا —They said, “We affirm.” This was the collective response of all prophets.

قَالَ فَٱشْهَدُوا۟ وَأَنَا۠ مَعَكُم مِّنَ ٱلشَّـٰهِدِينَ —He said, “Then bear witness, and I am with you among the witnesses.” Allah ﷻ Himself becomes a witness to this covenant alongside the prophets.

Now the argument against the rabbis becomes undeniable: If even prophets committed to believing in the messenger who would come after them, especially the Final Messenger, then who are you—mere scholars and religious leaders—to reject him? The prophets you claim to follow made this covenant. Your refusal stands in direct contradiction to their commitment.

Ayah 82

فَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ

Having established the covenant made with all prophets, Allah ﷻ now addresses those who turn away from it. فَمَن تَوَلَّىٰ بَعْدَ ذَٰلِكَ —whoever turns away after that. The word تَوَلَّىٰ means to turn one’s back, to reject, to refuse.

The “after that” refers specifically to understanding this covenant—after learning that even the prophets committed to believing in and supporting the messenger who would come after them. The reference here returns to the rabbis of Banu Israel who, despite knowing their own scriptures contained prophecies about the Final Prophet ﷺ, chose to reject him.

فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْفَـٰسِقُونَ —then those are the truly corrupt ones. The word فَاسِق describes someone who has rebelled against divine command, who has broken covenant with Allah ﷻ. This isn’t mere error or confusion—it’s willful disobedience despite clear knowledge.

This brief but powerful ayah serves as both warning and condemnation. It establishes that rejection of the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ by those who possessed prior scripture wasn’t a matter of legitimate theological disagreement. It was fasq—corruption and rebellion against a covenant their own prophets had honored.

Ayah 83

أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ ٱللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ وَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلَمَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ

This ayah poses a rhetorical question that exposes the absurdity of seeking any path other than Allah’s. أَفَغَيْرَ دِينِ ٱللَّهِ يَبْغُونَ —is it other than the religion of Allah that they seek? The word يَبْغُونَ means to pursue, to seek after, to desire.

The word دِين here encompasses more than just “religion” in the narrow sense. It refers to the entire way of life, the system of judgment, the path of submission that Allah ﷻ has ordained. The question implies: given everything that follows, what else could possibly make sense?

وَلَهُۥٓ أَسْلَمَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ —when to Him alone has submitted everything and everyone in the heavens and the earth. Islam—the theme that runs throughout these passages—is here presented in its universal dimension. The word أَسْلَمَ means to submit, to surrender completely.

طَوْعًا وَكَرْهًا —willingly and unwillingly. This phrase captures a profound reality. Some submit to Allah ﷻ consciously and joyfully—the believers who embrace His commands. But even those who reject faith in their minds cannot escape submission to Allah’s cosmic order.

The disbeliever may deny Allah ﷻ with his tongue, but his heart beats by Allah’s command. His lungs inhale and exhale according to divine decree. Every cell in his body, every organ, every system operates under complete divine control. He cannot make his heart beat faster or slower by sheer will. He cannot command his liver to stop processing or his kidneys to cease filtering. These functions occur in absolute submission to their Creator.

When sleep overtakes him, he submits. When illness strikes, he submits. When death arrives, his submission becomes total—not a single thing remains under his control. The rebellious human will that denied Allah ﷻ in life is completely subdued in death.

وَإِلَيْهِ يُرْجَعُونَ —and to Him they will be returned. Ultimately, every path leads back to Allah ﷻ. There is no escaping this return. Those who willingly submitted will return in honor; those who unwillingly submitted will return in humiliation. But all will return.

Ayah 84

قُلْ ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ

The surah concludes this section with a declaration of comprehensive faith. قُلْ —Say. This command is addressed to the Prophet ﷺ, instructing him to make this declaration. The shift from the plural قُولُوا (“all of you say”) found in Surah al-Baqarah to the singular قُلْ (“you say”) here is significant.

In Surah al-Baqarah, the entire Muslim nation was being established, transitioning from the privileged status of Banu Israel. There, the collective voice mattered—قُولُوا. But here, the conversation is specifically with Christians who don’t claim national or ethnic exclusivity. They view Isa (AS) as the culmination of revelation, rendering previous and subsequent prophets less relevant. So now it’s the Messenger ﷺ himself who must speak: قُلْ.

ءَامَنَّا بِٱللَّهِ وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَيْنَا —We believe in Allah and what was sent down upon us. Here we find عَلَيْنَا (upon us) rather than إِلَيْنَا (to us). The choice of عَلَيْنَا emphasizes responsibility and obligation. This revelation isn’t merely a gift we received; it’s a trust we must carry, a mandate we must live by.

This distinction addresses a core difference between the Jewish and Christian attitudes toward scripture. The Jewish problem, as identified throughout the Quran, was viewing themselves as exclusively privileged—chosen people to whom scripture was given as a special status. The Christian problem was believing that faith in Isa (AS) absolved them from the obligations of divine law—the halal and haram, the commandments and prohibitions. By using عَلَيْنَا, the ayah stresses: what was sent to us came as a responsibility we must uphold.

The declaration continues: وَمَآ أُنزِلَ عَلَىٰٓ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ وَإِسْمَـٰعِيلَ وَإِسْحَـٰقَ وَيَعْقُوبَ وَٱلْأَسْبَاطِ —and what was sent down upon Ibrahim, Ismail, Ishaq, Yaqub and the tribes.

Then comes a subtle but crucial difference in structure: وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ مِن رَّبِّهِمْ —and what was given to Musa, Isa and the prophets from their Lord.

Notice the word ما (“what”) appears twice in this ayah. The first ما covers Musa and Isa together, then a second ما separately covers “the prophets.” This creates a grouping: Musa and Isa are mentioned as a connected pair, then other prophets are mentioned separately afterward.

Compare this to the similar declaration in Surah al-Baqarah (2:136), where the structure is different. There you find: وَمَآ أُوتِىَ مُوسَىٰ وَعِيسَىٰ وَمَا أُوتِىَ ٱلنَّبِيُّونَ —with a ما specifically for Musa and Isa, then another ما for the other prophets. That passage addressed Banu Israel directly, emphasizing: “The prophets sent to you specifically started with Musa and ended with Isa, and separately there were other prophets too.”

Here in Aal-E-Imran, addressing Christians, the structure places Musa and Isa within the same ما as one continuous unit, showing that Isa (AS) is part of a larger prophetic continuum. He isn’t the end of revelation but rather a link in the chain. After mentioning them together, the ayah adds وَٱلنَّبِيُّونَ —“and the prophets”—reminding the Christians that the legacy didn’t end with Isa (AS). He was part of an ongoing prophetic tradition that includes the Final Messenger ﷺ.

لَا نُفَرِّقُ بَيْنَ أَحَدٍ مِّنْهُمْ —we make no distinction between any one of them. This is the Islamic position: all prophets deserve our belief, respect and honor. We don’t elevate one at the expense of another. We don’t accept some while rejecting others.

وَنَحْنُ لَهُۥ مُسْلِمُونَ —and we are, exclusively, in submission to Him. The word order emphasizes exclusivity—لَهُۥ (to Him) comes before مُسْلِمُونَ (those who submit). Our submission is to Allah ﷻ alone, not to any prophet, angel or created being. This is the essence of Islamic monotheism, the culmination of the prophetic message that began with Adam (AS) and was completed with Muhammad ﷺ.

Ayah 85–91 · linked video: 17. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 85-91)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 85-91

Ayah 85

وَمَن يَبْتَغِ غَيْرَ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ دِينًا فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ

Allah ﷻ reiterates what He established earlier in the Surah: “Indeed, the religion in the sight of Allah is Islam” (3:19). Now He states emphatically: whoever pursues other than Islam as a way of life, it will not be accepted from him, and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter.

The word يَبْتَغِ carries the meaning of serious pursuit and effort. This isn’t merely passive preference but active seeking. The person is making a deliberate effort to pursue غَيْرَ ٱلْإِسْلَـٰمِ - something other than Islam - as their دِينًا, their complete way of life and judgment.

The message is crystal clear: فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْهُ (it will not be accepted from him). This is a fundamental truth of our faith, and we must be sincere about it. In the modern world, we can and should cooperate with people of other faiths on humanitarian causes - helping the homeless, working for social justice, addressing community problems. There’s no issue with that cooperation. But when it comes to matters of belief and the Hereafter, we must be honest about what we believe.

This doesn’t mean being confrontational or disrespectful. Rather, it means we don’t hide our conviction that Islam is the truth. If we genuinely care about humanity, we must find ways to share this message. We can’t claim to be friends with members of other faith communities yet never discuss the most important thing we believe - that would be insincere friendship.

The invitation to Islam doesn’t have to be a formal debate. It can happen over coffee, in casual conversation, in the natural flow of friendship. We’ve become so uncomfortable around non-Muslims that we either avoid religious discussion entirely or think every conversation must be a formal theological debate. Neither extreme is correct. We can have healthy, friendly conversations where we share what the Quran teaches without being aggressive or apologetic.

وَهُوَ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ مِنَ ٱلْخَـٰسِرِينَ (and he will be among the losers in the Hereafter) - this is the ultimate consequence. Whatever worldly success someone might achieve, if they reject Islam after it has been made clear to them, they will be among the losers when it truly matters.

Ayah 86

كَيْفَ يَهْدِى ٱللَّهُ قَوْمًا كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ وَشَهِدُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ ۚ وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ

Allah ﷻ poses a rhetorical question: How can He guide a people who chose disbelief after they had believed? The ayah lists three compounding factors that make their rejection particularly severe.

First, كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ (they disbelieved after their faith) - they had tasted Iman, experienced its sweetness, understood what it meant, and then walked away from it. This isn’t someone who never knew; this is someone who knew and rejected.

Second, وَشَهِدُوٓا۟ أَنَّ ٱلرَّسُولَ حَقٌّ (they testified that the Messenger is true) - they had personally witnessed and acknowledged the truth of the Prophet ﷺ. They knew in their hearts he was genuine.

Third, وَجَآءَهُمُ ٱلْبَيِّنَـٰتُ (the clear proofs came to them) - they received undeniable evidence, clear signs that left no room for reasonable doubt.

The word بَعْدَ (after) in this context doesn’t only refer to a chronological sequence. It can also indicate a qualitative departure - someone who truly understood the religion for what it is and then consciously chose to ignore it. We sometimes call this “burnout” from the religion. Someone says, “Yeah, I used to be practicing. I’m having a slump right now, so I’ve stopped.” But this isn’t a phase or a fashion statement. This is your commitment with Allah ﷻ.

Others might dismiss your practice as a phase. Your uncle might say, “She started wearing hijab - she’s going through a phase, she’ll get over it.” Even the Prophet ﷺ was dismissed by the disbelievers who said a jinn had possessed him and when the jinn leaves, he’ll be fine. So people will say things - that’s their problem. But you cannot treat your own relationship with Allah ﷻ as temporary or experimental.

Islam isn’t something you try out like joining a club or experimenting with a lifestyle. It’s not comparable to other phases young people go through. When someone truly understands what Islam is - complete submission to the Creator - and then decides to abandon it, that’s the serious matter Allah ﷻ is addressing here.

وَٱللَّهُ لَا يَهْدِى ٱلْقَوْمَ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (And Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people) - and who are the wrongdoers in this context? Those who had all the ingredients for guidance yet chose to wrong themselves by rejecting it.

Ayah 87

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ جَزَآؤُهُمْ أَنَّ عَلَيْهِمْ لَعْنَةَ ٱللَّهِ وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ

The consequence for such people is severe: their compensation is that upon them will be the curse of Allah ﷻ, the angels, and all of humanity together.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ (those people) - this demonstrative pronoun points to the specific category described in the previous ayah. جَزَآؤُهُمْ (their compensation) - what they get in return for squandering the greatest gift possible: guidance itself.

They will receive لَعْنَةَ ٱللَّهِ (the curse of Allah) - to be distanced from His mercy. Then وَٱلْمَلَـٰٓئِكَةِ (and the angels) will curse them. And وَٱلنَّاسِ أَجْمَعِينَ (and all people together) - even humanity on the Day of Judgment will curse them.

Why such universal condemnation? Because they had the greatest gift possible in this world - guidance - in their hands, and they squandered it. They chose something as destructive as kufr instead. Even other people, who may not have had the same level of clarity and evidence, will recognize the magnitude of this rejection on Judgment Day.

Ayah 88

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ

خَـٰلِدِينَ فِيهَا (remaining in it eternally) - they will stay permanently in this state of curse and its consequences. The punishment has two characteristics that emphasize its severity.

First, لَا يُخَفَّفُ عَنْهُمُ ٱلْعَذَابُ (the punishment will not be lightened from them). The verb يُخَفَّفُ is in the passive form, emphasizing that there will be no source of relief whatsoever. Not even a little bit. The word comes from khiffa, meaning lightness or ease.

Second, وَلَا هُمْ يُنظَرُونَ (nor will they be looked at/waited for). The verb yunzaroon has two meanings here: they won’t be given respite, and they won’t even be regarded or paid attention to when they seek relief. There will be no pause in their punishment, no break to catch their breath.

This paints a horrific picture. In this world, when prisoners are tortured, there’s a limit - they might pass out or their body might give out. The torturer has to stop because otherwise the person will die, and they want to continue. So they let them rest briefly, then start again. But in the punishment of the Hereafter, there’s no such limitation. The person can’t die to escape it, but you also can’t call what they’re experiencing “life.” The torment continues without pause, without relief, without end.

Ayah 89

إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ

But then comes an opening, a powerful exception: إِلَّا ٱلَّذِينَ تَابُوا۟ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ (except those who repent after that). Even after all that has been described - the apostasy, the rejection, the curse - there remains a door.

This echoes a similar concept in Surah Al-Baqarah, where those who hide the knowledge of Islam are given the opportunity for repentance. Here, those who tasted faith and let it go are also given hope.

The repentance requires two elements. First, تَابُوا۟ (they repented) - they made sincere Tawbah, returning to Allah ﷻ. Second, وَأَصْلَحُوا۟ (and they reconciled/reformed) - they fixed their behavior, they became better. They had Iman, they wandered away, and they came back to Iman.

We cannot judge someone who goes through this cycle. “Oh, this person flip-flops with their religion.” No. If they made sincere Tawbah and genuinely reformed themselves, hope is still alive for them.

فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَفُورٌ رَّحِيمٌ (then certainly Allah is Ghafoor, Raheem) - extremely forgiving, always merciful. These two names of Allah ﷻ appear together here to emphasize that His capacity to forgive is beyond our imagination and His mercy is constant.

There’s a beautiful story of a Companion whose name was Abu Mihjan ath-Thaqafi. He came to the Messenger of Allah ﷺ and said, “There is not a sin in existence that I haven’t done.” The Prophet ﷺ asked, “Have you accepted Islam?” He said, “Yes.” The Prophet ﷺ told him that Islam wipes away what came before it.

Think about what that statement means: “There is not a sin I have not done.” That’s a comprehensive claim. Yet the mercy of Allah ﷻ is greater. Your sins cannot be bigger than Allah’s mercy, no matter how much you’ve messed up. This is true whether you’re Muslim or non-Muslim. A non-Muslim can convert and start fresh with a clean slate. And a Muslim who has fallen can also make sincere Tawbah and return. It’s not too late. You simply need to have that resolve and never lose hope in the mercy of Allah ﷻ.

Ayah 90

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا لَّن تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلضَّآلُّونَ

Now Allah ﷻ describes a different category of people - those who don’t return. There are two types: those who had faith, lost it for a while, then came back (mentioned in the previous ayah), and those who had faith, lost it, and then went even further away.

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِهِمْ (Indeed, those who disbelieved after their faith) - they started with belief but chose disbelief. But it doesn’t stop there.

ثُمَّ ٱزْدَادُوا۟ كُفْرًا (then they increased in disbelief) - the word ثُمَّ indicates progression and sequence. They didn’t just leave faith; they became more and more entrenched in their rejection. They went from disbelief to active hostility, from passive rejection to aggressive opposition. Their hearts became harder and harder.

لَّن تُقْبَلَ تَوْبَتُهُمْ (their repentance will not be accepted) - this refers to their state on the Day of Judgment. When they finally want to make Tawbah in the Hereafter, it will be too late. Allah ﷻ will say: No, your repentance is no longer acceptable. The door that was open in this life has closed.

وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلضَّآلُّونَ (and those are the ones who are truly lost) - they are the genuinely misguided ones. The word الضَّآلُّونَ indicates complete lostness, wandering without direction, with no path back. They will remain lost because they chose to walk further and further away from the truth.

Ayah 91

إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِم مِّلْءُ ٱلْأَرْضِ ذَهَبًا وَلَوِ ٱفْتَدَىٰ بِهِۦٓ ۗ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ

The final ayah in this passage addresses those who die in a state of disbelief without returning to faith. إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا۟ وَمَاتُوا۟ وَهُمْ كُفَّارٌ (Indeed, those who disbelieved and died while they were disbelievers) - there’s no ambiguity here. They disbelieved and remained in that state until their death.

فَلَن يُقْبَلَ مِنْ أَحَدِهِم مِّلْءُ ٱلْأَرْضِ ذَهَبًا (will not be accepted from any one of them the earth’s weight in gold). The word مِّلْءُ means “the full measure” or “the filling of.” Imagine the entire earth filled with gold - that’s the value being discussed here. If someone had that much gold in their possession on the Day of Judgment, it wouldn’t help them.

وَلَوِ ٱفْتَدَىٰ بِهِۦٓ (even if he tried to ransom himself with it) - even if he actually had it and was willing to give it all up as ransom to escape punishment. In this world, wealthy criminals can sometimes buy their way out of consequences. They pay huge ransoms, settle with massive penalties, use their resources to escape justice. But on that Day, even if someone could offer the weight of the world in gold, it would be worthless.

The irony is striking: Allah ﷻ mentions them offering the world’s weight in gold, but what will they actually have on the Day of Judgment? Nothing. They’ll have nothing to offer at all.

أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ لَهُمْ عَذَابٌ أَلِيمٌ (Those are the ones who will have painful punishment) - the word أَلِيمٌ emphasizes the pain itself, the deep suffering they will experience.

وَمَا لَهُم مِّن نَّـٰصِرِينَ (and they will have no helpers) - no one will be there to aid them, no one to intercede, no one to save them from what they earned for themselves.

These ayahs represent an escalation in the tone of address to the People of the Book. The conversation that began in Surah Al-Baqarah has been progressing, and as it progresses, the warnings become stronger and clearer. Just as Al-Baqarah had two major sections - the first addressing the People of the Book and the second addressing the Muslim Ummah - Surah Aal-E-Imran follows a similar structure. We’re reaching the end of the first major section, which focuses on the People of the Book, and soon the Surah will transition to addressing the Ummah directly.

The parallels between these two Surahs are remarkable: pre-battle commentary about Badr in Al-Baqarah, post-battle commentary about Uhud in this Surah. Discussion of the Jews and Iman in Al-Baqarah, discussion of Christians and Islam in this Surah. This makes sense because the Jewish challenge was primarily about matters of the heart (Iman), while the Christian challenge involved questions about submission and accepting divine law. Each community needed to hear the message most relevant to their particular deviation from truth.

Both Surahs end with powerful supplications. Al-Baqarah concluded with “Grant us victory over the disbelieving people” (2:286), while this Surah will conclude with a message not to worry about the disbelievers - Allah ﷻ will deal with them, and ultimately they will be in the Fire. These beautiful structural and thematic connections demonstrate the Quran’s sophisticated literary unity and its carefully calibrated message to different audiences.

Ayah 92–93 · linked video: 18. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 92-93)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayah 92-93 Commentary

Ayah 92

لَن تَنَالُوا۟ ٱلْبِرَّ حَتَّىٰ تُنفِقُوا۟ مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ ۚ وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ

“You will never achieve righteousness until you spend from what you love. And whatever you spend of anything - indeed, Allah ﷻ is fully Knowing of it.”

This beautiful ayah serves as both a continuation of the conversation with the People of the Book and a profound reminder to the believers. The discussion parallels what we covered in Surah Al-Baqarah regarding الْبِرَّ - righteousness. In that earlier surah, Allah ﷻ clarified that righteousness is not merely turning your faces eastward and westward in prayer. True birr encompasses faith combined with action, and the most emphasized action was spending in charity.

The Universal Address

When Allah ﷻ says لَن تَنَالُوا۟ - “you will never achieve” - He uses the second person plural, which includes both the believers and the People of the Book. This universal address carries profound implications for each group.

For the People of the Book considering Islam, accepting this faith requires tremendous sacrifice. They must give up deeply held beliefs, community ties, and perhaps even family relationships. They must be willing to spend from what they truly love - their heritage, their traditions, their standing in their community.

For the believers, this ayah presents an equally powerful truth: simply accepting Islam does not automatically make you a righteous person. The test of goodness is not mere verbal declaration of faith. The test of goodness is what follows - your willingness to sacrifice what you love for the sake of Allah ﷻ.

Understanding Birr

The word الْبِرَّ means more than just goodness. It carries connotations of safety and stability, derived from its linguistic connection to بَرّ - dry land, as opposed to the unstable ocean. True righteousness provides you with firm ground to stand on, a stable foundation for your life. But this stability cannot be achieved without sacrifice.

The Mercy of “From What You Love”

Notice the profound mercy in Allah’s ﷻ phrasing: مِمَّا تُحِبُّونَ - “from what you love.” Allah ﷻ does not demand that you give away everything you love. He asks for a portion from the things you love. This is a review of the detailed discussion in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ warned us not to give away the filthy, unwanted things while keeping the good for ourselves.

In contemporary terms, this means we shouldn’t approach charity like a garage sale, offloading our old, worn-out possessions while keeping the new and valuable for ourselves. When the community masjid holds a donation drive, we shouldn’t bring the outdated electronics, the clothes we no longer wear, or the toys our children have outgrown. The things we give should be things we genuinely love and value.

This is a crucial lesson to teach our children. If you give your child eight presents, ask them to identify their favorite one, then encourage them to give that away for the sake of Allah ﷻ. Nobody finds this easy - and that’s precisely the point. It’s a test of what you love more: keeping your possessions or pleasing Allah ﷻ.

Time: The Most Precious Asset

While we naturally think of wealth and material possessions when discussing spending, there’s an even more valuable asset we all possess: time. Every moment of our lives represents spending - we’re constantly investing our time whether we’re asleep or awake, whether we’re being productive or wasting hours on meaningless activities.

The best time you have - when your energy is highest and your capabilities are greatest - is your youth. A grandfather and a young person both have the same twenty-four hours in a day, but they don’t have the same capacity to use those hours. Time has diminished the elder’s energy, health, and abilities. Young people often feel they have plenty of time - “I’ll get to it later,” “I’ve got years ahead of me,” “It’s a long weekend, I’ll study eventually.” But this ayah challenges that mindset.

That extra sleep you love is also time you’re spending. That Friday night you consider “your time” is an asset being invested somewhere. Are you spending your most precious hours - when you’re most energetic and capable - on things Allah ﷻ loves? Or are you hoarding them for yourself?

Allah’s Complete Knowledge

The ayah concludes with a powerful reminder: وَمَا تُنفِقُوا۟ مِن شَىْءٍ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ بِهِۦ عَلِيمٌ - “And whatever you spend of anything - indeed, Allah ﷻ is fully Knowing of it.” The structure here differs slightly from similar statements in Surah Al-Baqarah, where Allah ﷻ says “whatever good you spend.” Here, He says “whatever you spend of anything.”

This addition teaches us something profound about quality. When the ayah specifies “good,” it means you don’t need to announce your charity or keep records - Allah ﷻ already knows about your good deeds. But when it says “anything,” it adds another dimension: Allah ﷻ knows not just that you spent, but the quality of what you spent. Was the money itself earned through permissible means? What were your intentions? Where did you spend it?

You might have the tax-exempt receipt from a nonprofit organization proving you donated, but Allah ﷻ knows the full reality behind that transaction. He knows whether the money was earned properly, whether your heart was truly in it, and whether you’re seeking His pleasure or people’s praise.

Ayah 93

۞ كُلُّ ٱلطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا لِّبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ ۗ قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ فَٱتْلُوهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ

“All food was lawful to the Children of Israel except what Israel had made unlawful to himself before the Torah was revealed. Say, ‘Then bring the Torah and recite it, if you are truthful.’”

The Transition: From Essence to Form

After discussing the essence of righteousness - sacrifice and spending from what we love - Allah ﷻ now addresses what can happen when religions lose touch with their essence. This transition is crucial because when we deal with righteousness, we must also confront false perceptions of righteousness.

This is not just about the Children of Israel. Muslims can suffer from the same problem. Over time, what often remains in religious traditions is not the spirit or essence of the religion, but rather its skeletal structure - the external facade. Imagine a massive tree that looks impressive from the outside with its bark, branches, and leaves, but termites have eaten away the interior. The roots are weak and frail, yet from the outside, it appears magnificent. Similarly, a building might look grand externally while its foundations have rotted and it’s on the verge of collapse.

In religion, people often elevate certain observable aspects - clothing, acts of worship, expressions, visible practices - while the essence is lost. I’m not saying these external matters are unimportant, but when they’re all that’s left and the core has disappeared, we have a serious problem.

Understanding “All Food Was Lawful”

Allah ﷻ tells us that كُلُّ ٱلطَّعَامِ كَانَ حِلًّا - all food was lawful for the Children of Israel. We must understand this in its proper linguistic context. When someone says “I gave you everything” or “I’ve tried everything possible to help you,” they don’t mean literally everything in existence. These are expressions that must be understood contextually.

What Allah ﷻ means here is that there were so many permissible things for the Children of Israel - the vast majority of foods were halal for them. This illustrates how few things are actually prohibited in Allah’s ﷻ legislation. It’s as if everything is permissible, and you might find just a speck on the horizon that you shouldn’t touch.

Notice also the beautiful rhetorical choice: حِلًّا instead of حَلَالًا. Hill is the abbreviated, easier-to-pronounce form, which linguistically suggests that permissibility was easy and complete for them. There was no rigorous process required - things were simply, easily permissible.

The Personal Preference of Ya’qub (AS)

The ayah continues: إِلَّا مَا حَرَّمَ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلُ عَلَىٰ نَفْسِهِۦ - “except what Israel had made unlawful to himself.” Remember that Israel is another name for Ya’qub (Jacob) (AS), so the Children of Israel are literally the descendants of Prophet Ya’qub (AS).

What happened was simple: Ya’qub (AS) personally didn’t like certain foods. According to narrations, he didn’t like camel meat. This was his personal preference, not a divine prohibition. Halal and haram are matters of revelation, but personal preferences are something else entirely. Allah ﷻ has made certain things permissible, but they might still not be suitable for you individually. Peanuts are halal, but if you’re allergic to them, they’re effectively prohibited for you - not from a religious standpoint, but from a health perspective.

When Preference Becomes “Religion”

Here’s where the problem developed: over time, out of allegiance and respect for their forefather Ya’qub (AS), the Children of Israel observed that he didn’t eat camel meat. They reasoned, “Our father avoided this, so it must be prohibited by revelation.” What was merely a personal preference became codified in their religious law. Camel meat was declared non-kosher, added to their jurisprudence as religiously impermissible.

This danger manifests even more strongly in our relationship with the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ. If the Messenger of Allah ﷺ personally stops doing something, what might we immediately conclude? That it must be haram. This is why Allah ﷻ addressed this danger directly in the Quran. When the Prophet ﷺ, for personal domestic reasons, stopped taking honey, Allah ﷻ revealed: لِمَ تُحَرِّمُ مَا أَحَلَّ اللَّهُ لَكَ - “Why do you make unlawful what Allah ﷻ has made lawful for you?” (66:1)

This wasn’t just a casual personal matter. It was crucial to address because if the Messenger ﷺ stops consuming honey, companions might understand this as a religious prohibition. He had to be extraordinarily careful in his personal life, because every action - even his private choices - could be misunderstood as religious legislation.

The Sociology of Religious Decline

This represents what happens when a religion starts deteriorating and people lose touch with the original teachings and sources. Instead of returning to the authentic texts, people take their religion from observing religious leaders. Not even from what these leaders say, but from what they do. If a religious leader does something, it must be permissible. If a religious leader doesn’t do something, it must be prohibited.

Even when the leader doesn’t explicitly teach this, it becomes the sociology of that religious community. This problematic sentiment can spread through a society, where external observances and loyalty to personalities replace engagement with the actual revelation.

مِن قَبْلِ أَن تُنَزَّلَ ٱلتَّوْرَىٰةُ - “before the Torah was revealed.” This personal preference of Ya’qub (AS) occurred long before the Torah was even sent down. It had nothing to do with divine legislation.

The Challenge to Bring Proof

Allah ﷻ concludes with a direct challenge: قُلْ فَأْتُوا۟ بِٱلتَّوْرَىٰةِ فَٱتْلُوهَآ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ - “Say, ‘Then bring the Torah and recite it, if you are truthful.’” Don’t tell Me what it says. Don’t report second-hand what you heard from your scholars. Bring the actual text, place it in front of you, and read it directly. If you’re truly being honest, you’ll find that your claims aren’t supported by your own scripture.

This challenges a dangerous tendency in all religious communities: taking religion through the filter of tradition and personalities rather than engaging directly with the revealed text. Allah ﷻ demands that they return to the source, read it themselves, and verify whether their practices align with what was actually revealed.

The lesson for us is profound: Are we building our understanding of Islam on the solid foundation of the Quran and authentic Sunnah? Or are we allowing cultural practices, personal preferences of scholars, and inherited traditions to become confused with the actual religion? True righteousness requires us to constantly return to the source, to verify our beliefs and practices against revelation, and to distinguish between the essence of our faith and the cultural forms it may take.

Ayah 94–100 · linked video: 19. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 94-100)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 94-100

Ayah 94

فَمَنِ ٱفْتَرَىٰ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ٱلْكَذِبَ مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ فَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلظَّـٰلِمُونَ

Then whoever invents a lie against Allah after that - those are truly the wrongdoers.

After establishing the truth about Ibrahim (AS) and his legacy, Allah ﷻ declares a severe warning: whoever fabricates lies about Him after this clarification has been made, they are the ultimate wrongdoers - الظَّـٰلِمُونَ . The word الظَّـٰلِمُونَ signifies those who commit the worst form of injustice, not just ordinary wrongdoing.

This ayah addresses those who might manipulate even clear evidence. The implication is that even if the Torah were brought forward and its testimony examined, some would still fabricate interpretations to suit their agendas. The phrase “after that” (مِنۢ بَعْدِ ذَٰلِكَ) emphasizes that there is no excuse for such fabrication once the truth has been made clear. To lie about Allah ﷻ after receiving clarity is to place oneself firmly in the category of the ultimate oppressors.

Ayah 95

قُلْ صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ ۗ فَٱتَّبِعُوا۟ مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ حَنِيفًا وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ

Say: “Allah has spoken the truth. So follow the religion of Ibrahim, inclining toward truth; and he was not of the polytheists.”

Allah ﷻ commands His Messenger ﷺ to declare unequivocally: صَدَقَ ٱللَّهُ - “Allah has spoken the truth.” This isn’t merely a statement about truthfulness; it’s an affirmation that everything Allah ﷻ has revealed is absolute truth. Had the People of the Book truly adhered to the Torah as it was revealed by Allah ﷻ, they would never have fabricated these lies.

The command then shifts to following مِلَّةَ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ - the way of Ibrahim (AS). The word مِلَّة is distinct from أُمَّة. While ummah refers to a community unified by a common belief system, millah carries a deeper emotional and devotional dimension. It represents a way of life characterized by love, sentiment and heartfelt dedication. Allah ﷻ is calling people not just to intellectually accept Ibrahim’s path, but to affiliate themselves with it in a loving, devoted manner.

Ibrahim (AS) is described as حَنِيفًا - one who was solely and completely dedicated to Allah ﷻ, turning away from all falsehood. The ayah concludes by emphasizing وَمَا كَانَ مِنَ ٱلْمُشْرِكِينَ - “and he was not of the polytheists.” This is a decisive refutation of any claims that Ibrahim (AS) belonged to later religious factions that incorporated elements of shirk.

The progression in these ayahs is significant: we move from one spiritual father (the Prophet Muhammad ﷺ) to another (Ibrahim AS), going back to the grandfather of monotheistic tradition. This genealogy of faith becomes essential for understanding what follows.

Ayah 96

إِنَّ أَوَّلَ بَيْتٍ وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ لَلَّذِى بِبَكَّةَ مُبَارَكًا وَهُدًى لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

Indeed, the first House established for mankind was that at Bakkah - blessed and a guidance for the worlds.

Allah ﷻ declares with certainty: the first house of worship established for humanity was the one at Bakkah. Bakkah is the ancient name for Makkah, and the Quran’s precision in using this specific name is remarkable. Later in Surah Muhammad (48), we find the word “Makkah” mentioned, but here the historical name Bakkah is used deliberately.

This choice of nomenclature connects us to the historical inauguration of this sacred site. Since this ayah discusses the original establishment of the House, the name most tied to that founding moment - Bakkah - is the appropriate one to use. The city came into being because of this House, built as the first structure dedicated to the worship of Allah ﷻ for all of humanity.

The phrase وُضِعَ لِلنَّاسِ - “placed for mankind” - is significant. It states the House was placed “for” people, not necessarily “by” people. This has led scholars to discuss whether Ibrahim (AS) was the original builder or whether he rebuilt a structure that already existed. Some suggest the House may have been there even before Adam (AS). While we may not know the definitive answer in this life, what’s clear is that this House was designated by Allah ﷻ for all of humanity.

The House is described with two essential qualities: مُبَارَكًا - blessed, and هُدًى لِّلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - a source of guidance for all nations. The blessing of this place is tangible even today. Every act of worship performed there is multiplied in reward. Whether it’s the obligatory Hajj, voluntary ’Umrah, prayer, tawaf or supplication - none of it compares to worship performed elsewhere. The blessings are multiplied beyond measure. That is the meaning of مُبَارَكًا.

As a source of guidance for all nations, the Kaaba represents something profound: it is meant to be the spiritual and physical center of Islam for all peoples, not just Muslims. The spirit of Ibrahim (AS) permeates this reality - that the capital of Islam should be a source from which divine light radiates to every corner of the world.

Ayah 97

فِيهِ ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ مَّقَامُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ ۖ وَمَن دَخَلَهُۥ كَانَ ءَامِنًا ۗ وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ حِجُّ ٱلْبَيْتِ مَنِ ٱسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا ۚ وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ

In it are clear signs - the standing place of Ibrahim. And whoever enters it shall be safe. And pilgrimage to the House is a duty upon mankind for Allah, for whoever is able to find a way. And whoever disbelieves - then indeed, Allah is Free of need of the worlds.

Within this sacred precinct are ءَايَـٰتٌۢ بَيِّنَـٰتٌ - clear miraculous signs. The ayah provides an example: مَّقَامُ إِبْرَٰهِيمَ - the Station of Ibrahim. This is where Ibrahim (AS) stood while constructing the Kaaba, and where Allah ﷻ caused the stone to soften beneath his feet, preserving his footprints as an eternal sign. That this rock became soft for Ibrahim’s comfort as he labored to build the House, and that these footprints have been preserved through the ages - this itself is miraculous.

Beyond the Station of Ibrahim, hadith literature points to other miracles associated with this place. The continuous pelting at Mina during Hajj, performed by millions over centuries, should have buried the entire area in pebbles. Yet narrations indicate that the pebbles of those whose Hajj is accepted are lifted by Allah’s command - another manifestation of His miraculous preservation of this sacred site.

وَمَن دَخَلَهُۥ كَانَ ءَامِنًا - “Whoever enters it shall be safe.” This safety operates on multiple levels. Physically, it was and remains a sanctuary where people feel secure. But more profoundly, whoever enters with sincere intention and has their pilgrimage accepted receives safety from the Hellfire. It becomes a cleansing, a fresh start for the Next Life. This is the deeper meaning of كَانَ ءَامِنًا - guaranteed safety in both worlds.

Then comes the momentous declaration: وَلِلَّهِ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ حِجُّ ٱلْبَيْتِ - “And pilgrimage to the House is a duty upon mankind for Allah.” The word حِجُّ itself connects to حُجَّة - an irrefutable proof or argument. On the Day of Judgment, the Quran will be a hujjah either for you or against you. Similarly, a sincerely performed and accepted Hajj becomes an irrefutable case for entering Paradise - evidence of one’s submission and devotion to Allah ﷻ.

Notice the universality: Allah ﷻ mandates Hajj عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ - upon all humanity, not just a specific ethnicity or group. This underscores that Islam is a universal religion. Just as spiritual migration to Allah ﷻ (through faith) is required of all people, so too is physical migration to His House - for those who are able.

The qualification comes: مَنِ ٱسْتَطَاعَ إِلَيْهِ سَبِيلًا - “whoever is able to find a way to it.” Ability here encompasses physical capability, financial means, health and circumstances. The moment a believer becomes capable, they should make Hajj a priority, not something perpetually deferred. It should be a life goal, planned for and pursued at the earliest opportunity.

When you do go for Hajj, understand its true spirit. This is not about luxury packages or status symbols. Hajj is meant to be a complete departure from worldly concerns - a simulation of the Day of Judgment itself. You wear the clothes you’ll be buried in, you stand among millions before your Lord, you leave behind all the trappings of this temporary life. It’s the ultimate spiritual detachment, the supreme act of devotion.

وَمَن كَفَرَ فَإِنَّ ٱللَّهَ غَنِىٌّ عَنِ ٱلْعَـٰلَمِينَ - “And whoever disbelieves - then indeed, Allah is Free of need of the worlds.” Though the ayah discusses Hajj, the response to its rejection is described as disbelief. Why? Because refusing the physical migration reflects a refusal of the spiritual migration of faith. Allah ﷻ needs none of it. He is غَنِىٌّ - completely free of need from all creation. The pilgrimage is for our benefit, our purification, our salvation - not for His.

Ayah 98

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ وَٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ

Say: “O People of the Scripture, why do you disbelieve in the signs of Allah while Allah is Witness over what you do?”

The address shifts directly to the People of the Book. The question is posed: لِمَ تَكْفُرُونَ بِـَٔايَـٰتِ ٱللَّهِ - “Why do you reject the signs of Allah?” These signs include everything previously mentioned - the ayahs of revelation, the House built by their own forefather Ibrahim (AS), the Kaaba that they know about with the same certainty that they know their own children.

Some historical accounts indicate that they deliberately altered the name from Bakkah to something else, claiming they no longer knew its location - a convenient distortion achieved by changing a single letter. This willful ignorance of what they actually knew is the essence of كُفْر - covering up and rejecting the truth.

وَٱللَّهُ شَهِيدٌ عَلَىٰ مَا تَعْمَلُونَ - “And Allah is Witness over what you do.” Every action, every scheme, every distortion - Allah ﷻ witnesses it all. This isn’t just a statement of knowledge; it’s a warning that testimony will be given against them for their deeds.

Ayah 99

قُلْ يَـٰٓأَهْلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ مَنْ ءَامَنَ تَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَآءُ ۗ وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ

Say: “O People of the Scripture, why do you obstruct from the path of Allah those who believe, seeking to make it [seem] deviant, while you are witnesses [to the truth]? And Allah is not unaware of what you do.”

The second question intensifies: لِمَ تَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ - “Why do you obstruct from the path of Allah?” The verb تَصُدُّونَ carries a dual meaning. It describes both stopping oneself from the path and preventing others from it. This obstruction operates on two levels simultaneously: you keep yourselves from accepting the truth, and you actively work to prevent others from embracing it.

An example of this tactic was mentioned earlier in the surah: some would accept Islam publicly during the day, spend time with the Muslims and the Prophet ﷺ, then leave in the evening and announce their apostasy. The purpose? To create doubt - to make those who had recently accepted Islam think perhaps there was nothing worth converting for after all.

تَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا - “Seeking to make it deviant.” You pursue any crookedness, any distortion you can find or fabricate. The word عِوَجًا refers to something that should be straight but becomes bent and deformed, like a pole that deteriorates over time. You actively seek to present the straight path as crooked.

وَأَنتُمْ شُهَدَآءُ - “While you are witnesses.” You yourselves bear witness to the truth. You know it in your hearts, you recognize it in your scriptures, yet you testify against it anyway.

This tactic isn’t merely historical. Today, one of the most effective strategies for calling people away from Islam is not to promote the merits of alternative beliefs, but simply to attack Islam itself. More energy is dedicated to portraying Islam as evil, contradictory and wrong than to presenting the supposed virtues of what’s being offered as an alternative.

وَمَا ٱللَّهُ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ - “And Allah is not unaware of what you do.” Muslims sometimes become distressed by the conspiracies, the attacks, the distortions spread about Islam across media platforms and websites. But understand this: Allah ﷻ is not unaware - not in the slightest. He knows every website, every server holding every file, every video produced, every word spoken. He is fully aware of every single attack, and nothing escapes His knowledge.

Ayah 100

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ إِن تُطِيعُوا۟ فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ

O you who have believed, if you obey a party of those who were given the Scripture, they will turn you back, after your belief, into disbelievers.

Now the warning shifts to the believers directly: يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوٓا۟ - “O you who have believed.” The verb تُطِيعُوا۟ means to obey, but it also carries the meaning of paying attention to, turning toward or giving consideration to something. If you obey or even just pay close attention to any faction from among those given the Scripture - فَرِيقًا مِّنَ ٱلَّذِينَ أُوتُوا۟ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ - then what will happen?

يَرُدُّوكُم بَعْدَ إِيمَـٰنِكُمْ كَـٰفِرِينَ - “They will turn you back, after your belief, into disbelievers.” The verb يَرُدُّوكُم means to return something, like returning a package to sender or merchandise to a store. The imagery here is profound: it’s as if Allah ﷻ accepted you into Islam, but when you began paying attention to and obeying those who reject the truth, you became unacceptable and were returned - rejected.

After your iman had been established, after you had been granted faith, they will turn you back into a state of kufr - disbelief. This is the danger of allowing their arguments, their distortions and their systematic obstruction to influence your understanding. The warning is clear: guard your faith carefully, for those who actively work against the truth seek nothing less than to drag you back into the darkness you’ve escaped.

Ayah 101–102 · linked video: 20. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 101-102)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayah 101-102 Commentary

Ayah 101

وَكَيْفَ تَكْفُرُونَ وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُۥ ۗ وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ فَقَدْ هُدِىَ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ

Allah ﷻ poses a powerful rhetorical question that cuts to the heart of faith: “How could you disbelieve?” This isn’t merely an inquiry—it’s an expression of wonder at the absurdity of turning away from guidance when it’s right in front of you.

The ayah continues: وَأَنتُمْ تُتْلَىٰ عَلَيْكُمْ ءَايَـٰتُ ٱللَّهِ—“And you are the people upon whom the miraculous signs of Allah are being recited.” You’re in the unique position of having revelation coming directly to you. What void could possibly exist in this Quran that isn’t being filled for you? What justification could you possibly have to turn your attention to the People of the Book or anyone else? The fact that these ayat are being recited to you should be sufficient to prevent you from being influenced by others.

Then Allah ﷻ provides a second justification: وَفِيكُمْ رَسُولُهُۥ—“His Messenger is especially in your midst.” Not only is the Quran being recited to you, but the Messenger himself is among you. You are the last people who should have the ability to turn away.

Now, someone might argue: “The ayat of Allah are being recited to us today as well. But the Messenger ﷺ is not with us physically.” While He ﷺ may not be with us in person, His words remain with us. His instructions, His seerah—these are all preserved for us. For those people living centuries ago, the Prophet ﷺ was a person who lived next door. For us, these books and compilations serve the same purpose. Allah ﷻ has ensured these teachings are preserved so the spirit of the Messenger’s ﷺ deen remains with us. His words are meant to be as impactful on us as they were on the people sitting directly in front of Him ﷺ. There’s a reason Allah ﷻ made sure those words, instructions and legacy were preserved. He ﷺ is still in our midst through His ﷺ preserved teachings. This question remains as relevant to us today.

This scenario is happening all around us. Young Muslims who never received a proper Islamic education—often through no fault of their own—end up in college and start listening to perspectives from Christian groups, philosophy clubs or other influences. They might think, “Well, I remember going to Sunday school occasionally, but maybe I should become a Christian or an agnostic.” Allah ﷻ is asking: How can that ever happen when the ayat of Allah are available to them?

The sad reality with many young people leaving Islam is that they were never in a position where the ayat of Allah were truly being recited to them. They were never given direct access to the beautiful teachings of this deen. They were never genuinely introduced to who the Messenger of Allah ﷺ truly is and what He ﷺ taught. What Allah ﷻ is teaching us between the lines is profound: a person who is genuinely introduced to Islam and comes to it with sincere intent will find no justifiable intellectual reason to leave. You could put blocks on their chest, burn them alive or tear them apart—they won’t leave. Once they truly taste what this deen is, it becomes nearly impossible to walk away. Someone who never tasted the deen to begin with might come and go easily, but one who truly experiences it cannot justify leaving it to themselves.

The ayah concludes with a beautiful word: وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ—“Whoever holds firmly to Allah.” Let’s examine this word اعتصم. The root ع-ص-م gives us اعتصام. The basic meaning of عصم is to stop someone or hold them back because if you don’t hold them, they’ll run into danger. Picture a child running toward a burning house to retrieve a toy. You grab the child and hold them back—you’re protecting them through عصم. If they ran forward, they would have rushed into danger.

Now consider when you yourself want to go somewhere dangerous but you hold yourself back, telling yourself, “No, it’s too dangerous. I’ll get myself killed.” This is اعتصام—the intransitive form where the action affects yourself. Whoever protects themselves, seeking protection for themselves from running into a fire toward which they were otherwise headed.

Imagine there’s a wind blowing, pushing you whether you like it or not toward that burning house. You’re not strong enough to fight the wind. What’s the only way you can survive? You have to hold on to something. Allah ﷻ gives us the perfect example Himself. Picture someone who has fallen off a ship during a storm. Ships have anchors with huge chains hanging on the side, with gigantic links. You’re holding onto that anchor chain for dear life. The water, rain and heavy waves are all pulling you to be drowned. The only thing keeping you afloat is your grip on that chain. This is i’tisam—the kind of danger that will lead to destruction unless you hold on for protection.

وَمَن يَعْتَصِم بِٱللَّهِ—“Whoever holds onto Allah for dear life”—فَقَدْ هُدِىَ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطٍ مُّسْتَقِيمٍ—“then he has been guided to a straight path.” Once you hold onto that link, Allah ﷻ will give you the strength to hold onto the next link, then the next. Now you’re heading up a path away from danger. He has guided you to a straight path, one that leads directly upward.

Ayah 102

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِۦ وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ

These next ayat contain a profound philosophy of our religion, capturing a deep teaching in just a few words. Think of earth that has been dead for a long time, with nothing growing on it. If you want it to become a garden again, you must go through certain processes before that dead earth can flourish once more. Similarly, a Muslim nation that has lost its true essence is like that dead earth. If we want it to become a flourishing nation again, to return to its original beauty, there’s a three-step process. The first of these ayat represents the first step, followed by the second and third steps.

The ayah we just covered gets you out of immediate trouble. You were being pulled by the People of the Book down into the storm, but you held onto Allah ﷻ to save yourself. Now you’re heading up a path. But is there still a possibility you could slip and fall again? Absolutely. Every step you take forward must be taken cautiously. You have to protect yourself from every angle because you could slip at any moment. This next ayah ensures you protect yourself at each step.

يَـٰٓأَيُّهَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—“O you who believe, protect yourselves.” Taqwa literally means to protect oneself. وقى means to protect someone else—“I will protect you.” But تقى means “I will try to protect myself.”

Now, protect yourself from whom? Allah ﷻ. The One who just threw you the anchor and helped you survive the storm—Him you should also be terrified of and protect yourself from. Why? Because if you make Allah ﷻ unhappy, you no longer deserve to be pulled out of the storm. What will He do? Let you go. “Don’t hold on. Let go.” You must appreciate this imagery Allah ﷻ is presenting. While you’re holding onto Allah ﷻ, make sure not to make Him unhappy or upset.

ٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ—“Be fearful of displeasing Allah.” This isn’t fear of Allah Himself in the way we fear danger. Taqwa is about protecting yourself from disobeying Allah ﷻ, from disappointing Him, from displeasing Him.

حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِۦ—“As He deserves that protection should be taken of Him, as is worthy of Him.” The Companions (RA) asked: Who can have taqwa of Allah as Allah ﷻ truly deserves? Who prays to Allah ﷻ as He deserves to be prayed to? Who makes dua to Allah ﷻ as He deserves to have dua made to Him? Who remembers Allah ﷻ as He deserves to be remembered? Who thanks Allah ﷻ as He deserves to be thanked? Who’s going to be cautious of Allah ﷻ as is truly worthy of Him? That’s an impossible standard.

Later, Allah ﷻ revealed another ayah in Surah At-Taghabun (64:16): فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ—“Have taqwa of Allah as much as you possibly can.” We understand these two ayat in conjunction. When Allah ﷻ says حَقَّ تُقَاتِهِۦ—“as He deserves”—what that means practically is as much as you possibly can. This becomes a personal judgment call for you and me.

You could claim you’re doing everything you can, but you can’t tell anybody else that. In all good conscience, if you can tell yourself, “I’m doing everything I can,” and you’re comfortable with the idea that one day you’ll be able to tell Allah ﷻ, “I did everything I could”—knowing full well He knows what’s on your tongue, what’s in your heart, and your capabilities better than you know them yourself—then fine. If not, keep trying. Nobody should use the easiest cop-out: “I’m doing everything I can. I’m doing what I can.” That shouldn’t be an excuse to avoid pushing yourself to a higher standard. فَٱتَّقُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ مَا ٱسْتَطَعْتُمْ—as much as you possibly can.

وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ—“Don’t you dare die except that you are in a state of submission.” What gives you a sense of urgency that you must protect yourself from displeasing Allah ﷻ? This life is a struggle, but it’s nothing compared to the struggles that may lie ahead on Judgment Day or for eternity to come. The end of this journey will be the beginning of that one.

لَا تَمُوتُنَّ literally means “don’t you dare die,” but death for the Muslim is the start of the next journey. Death is the first stop. Then there’s life in the grave, then Judgment Day, then the final station—the afterlife, which has no more stops after it. Either Jannah or Jahannam. Death will determine everything else. All future stops are determined by this one thing. Your time is running out. You must protect yourself. Don’t allow death to come to you except that you’re in a state of submission—مُّسْلِمُونَ.

Notice Allah ﷻ highlighted Muslim, not Mu’min. This is important because Islam is an act while Iman is a feeling. Iman is something in your heart; Islam is something in your action. Muslim refers to someone who submits through action. In other words, death will come and you’d better be in obedience when it arrives. You’d better be in submission. Don’t be caught doing something in disobedience when death comes, because death won’t say, “Oh, I’m sorry, you’re watching a movie. I’ll come back at a more convenient time.” It won’t do that. It will catch you whether you’re obeying or disobeying.

The one who truly wants to protect themselves knows death can come at any moment. You’d better not get caught red-handed in the middle of sin. This is called سوء الخاتمة —the worst kind of death, the evil death, meaning you died in the middle of a sin. The state in which you die is a strong indication of what Allah ﷻ has in store for you. There are people who die making sujud—they simply didn’t get up from prostration. There are people praying Fajr at the masjid who made one salam but didn’t get to the second before they were gone.

Then there are the tragic stories—young people headed to parties who never arrived because of car accidents. These are terrifying realities. No person should feel safe. This isn’t just about some historical figure or some evil person who died—this is about you and me. Every one of us has a public face and a private face: how we are at home, among friends, when nobody else is around. The Angel of Death doesn’t announce himself. He doesn’t say, “I’m sorry, you’re in a meeting. You need some private time. You’re on break. I shouldn’t come right now.” It won’t be like that.

وَلَا تَمُوتُنَّ إِلَّا وَأَنتُم مُّسْلِمُونَ—“Don’t you dare die except that you are in a state of submission.” This ayah calls for personal transformation. One must start living a life of caution with Allah ﷻ, and what keeps them constantly cautious is the reminder of death. That reminder will maintain their vigilance.

The most valuable asset we have is time, and the biggest loss we’re all suffering simultaneously is also time. Whether we like it or not, we’re losing it at every moment. All your life amounts to is a series of inhales and exhales. Every breath you take, every time you inhale and exhale, a piece of your life in this world has been taken away that can never return. Every single breath is a step closer to meeting your Master.

إِنَّكَ كَادِحٌ إِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ كَدْحًا—“You are marching forward toward your Lord.” You’ve been put on a conveyor belt heading toward your Master whether you like it or not, whether you accept it or not, whether you’re oblivious to it or not. None of that matters—you’re still heading there. You could stand on a conveyor belt facing backward or forward; you’re still heading forward. Whether you close your eyes or open them, you’re still heading forward.

The one who refuses to see reality and the one who opens their eyes to it—it doesn’t matter in terms of the journey’s direction. They’re both still heading forward. But the one who opens their eyes and looks at the danger around them can protect themselves on this journey. They’ll be ready to be taken in a state where they are Muslim—in submission.

لَا تَمُوتُنَّ uses the forbidding form of the verb. This is said for someone who’s about to die, not someone who’s already dead. It’s the strongest possible warning. Don’t you dare die except while you’re in Islam, in a state of submission.

May Allah ﷻ grant us that state of Islam throughout our lives and at the moment when our souls are taken from us.

Ayah 103–104 · linked video: 22. Ali ’Imran (Ayah 103-104)

Surah Aal-E-Imran: Ayahs 103-104

Ayah 103

وَٱعْتَصِمُوا۟ بِحَبْلِ ٱللَّهِ جَمِيعًا وَلَا تَفَرَّقُوا۟ ۚ وَٱذْكُرُوا۟ نِعْمَتَ ٱللَّهِ عَلَيْكُمْ إِذْ كُنتُمْ أَعْدَآءً فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا وَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ فَأَنقَذَكُم مِّنْهَا ۗ كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ

The Command to Unify Through the Quran

Instead of concerning yourself with judging others and creating division, you should be preoccupied with remembering and reminding yourself and others of the favor of Allah ﷻ upon you. The favor of Allah ﷻ upon you when all of you used to be enemies to one another. The Banu Israil in the previous surah became enemies to one another, and Allah ﷻ told them not to spill each other’s blood, yet they went ahead anyway. The Arab tribes before Islam were the same way, killing each other and at each other’s throats. The Aws and Khazraj were not the best of friends either.

Understanding Ta’lif: The Divine Bonding of Hearts

The word فَأَلَّفَ بَيْنَ قُلُوبِكُمْ carries profound meaning. The root word ألف relates to the number one thousand (alf), which represents infinity in Arabic expression. Ta’lif means to take something broken up into infinite pieces and bring it together as one. Allah ﷻ brought your dispersed hearts together and created this bond between them. Otherwise, they would have remained completely scattered.

The hearts are now united, and the cause of that unity is mentioned at the beginning of this ayah: the rope of Allah ﷻ. It is through the Quran that Allah ﷻ caused this love to happen between your hearts.

The Quran as the Source of Brotherhood

Consider a simple manifestation of this: when you see a young hafiz of the Quran, you naturally have love for them without even realizing it. When you hear beautiful recitation of the Quran, you naturally develop appreciation for the person reciting. This person brought you closer to Allah’s ﷻ Book, and when you hear them recite, you feel drawn to them even if you cannot see them or they are not present.

This demonstrates something essential: Allah ﷻ creates love between our hearts through the Quran. This also means that faith, as was already mentioned in the previous ayah, should make you love others. If your faith makes you judge others, hate others, be condescending toward others, label others, and it doesn’t make you love others, then the only possibility that can explain that is what the first ayah discussed: you don’t yet have what the first ayah required you to have. Perhaps you haven’t tasted the first step, so you cannot reach the second. All you see when you look at others are reasons to be judgmental, reasons to be angry, reasons to be disappointed. “These Muslims are this way. Those Muslims are that way.”

From Enemies to Brothers

This ayah reminds us: this is a favor of Allah ﷻ. You used to be enemies to one another, and He put love between your dispersed hearts. فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا - and you transformed, you became something entirely different by His favor alone. You became إِخْوَٰنًا.

In Arabic, there is a distinction between أَخ and إِخْوَٰن. Akh refers to blood brothers, actual siblings. Ikhwan refers to brothers in cause, like military brothers or members of a group who share a strong bond. Brotherhood is used in this broader sense. Eventually, when you reach the state of Iman - not just Islam but true Iman - the Quran says إِنَّمَا ٱلْمُؤْمِنُونَ إِخْوَةٌ - true believers are like blood brothers at that level. That’s how strong their love becomes. But this ayah describes the starting point: you just became ikhwan, brothers in faith. You didn’t immediately become blood brothers (ikhwa), but you started as ikhwan, and hopefully you will reach that final phase.

The Special Nature of This Gift

This brotherhood manifests in beautiful ways. When you travel to another land and enter a masjid, you immediately feel connection with people you’ve never met. Someone is reciting the same Quran, praying the same prayers. There’s something incredibly beautiful about feeling this sense of brotherhood across the ocean, in another world, yet having something in common that is stronger than anything else you could share.

The ayah emphasizes: فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا - by His blessing alone you became brothers. The word بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ is particularly significant. It means especially by Allah’s ﷻ favor. When supposedly pious, righteous people are at the heart of disagreements, fighting, yelling, screaming, hate, and nastiness in our communities, isn’t this the opposite of what this ayah embodies? Because it is especially by Allah’s ﷻ favor that you became brothers.

When someone does not do everything they can to protect that brotherhood, what are they insulting? The special gift from Allah ﷻ. Allah ﷻ goes out of His way, especially giving you the gift of turning you into brothers, of unifying you, of creating love between you. When someone doesn’t do everything possible to protect that brotherhood, to protect that unity, what are they kicking to the side? The gift of Allah ﷻ.

The Ayah as a Mirror for the Muslim Community

This ayah is not just knowledge to be stored away. It’s meant to transform a person’s attitude, to transform how we deal with masjid politics, how we handle rifts between brothers. Because now there’s a third party involved - it’s not just you and someone you had a disagreement with. Allah ﷻ is involved. This is Allah’s ﷻ favor. You cannot be ungrateful to it. You cannot sacrifice that unity because of your own hurt feelings.

At the Edge of Hellfire

وَكُنتُمْ عَلَىٰ شَفَا حُفْرَةٍ مِّنَ ٱلنَّارِ فَأَنقَذَكُم مِّنْهَا - and you were at the very edge of a ditch, a pit filled with fire, and He rescued you from it.

The word شَفَا means edge or brink. حُفْرَة is a ditch or pit. You were at the very edge of a pit filled with hellfire. In the previous surah, Allah ﷻ told the Banu Israil multiple times how close to hell they were, yet He kept forgiving them. But at one point, when they were violating brotherhood, when they were killing each other and expelling people from their homes, Allah ﷻ said: أَفَتُؤْمِنُونَ بِبَعْضِ ٱلْكِتَـٰبِ وَتَكْفُرُونَ بِبَعْضٍ (do you believe in some parts of the Book and reject other parts?).

What part of the Book did they violate? The part that told them to hold onto brotherhood at all costs. Allah ﷻ told them: فَمَا جَزَآءُ مَن يَفْعَلُ ذَٰلِكَ مِنكُمْ إِلَّا خِزْىٌ فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا (the recompense of those among you who do that is disgrace in this worldly life), and وَيَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ يُرَدُّونَ إِلَىٰٓ أَشَدِّ ٱلْعَذَابِ (on the Day of Resurrection they will be returned to the worst of punishment).

That is what Allah ﷻ told them when they interfered with brotherhood, when they fought each other. How are we thinking we’re an exception? When we fight each other, when disagreements tear us apart, where did فَأَصْبَحْتُم بِنِعْمَتِهِۦٓ إِخْوَٰنًا go? When you’re filing lawsuits against each other, where did that part of the Quran go? Did it become irrelevant? Did you not care to look it up?

The Power of Shared Survival

Think about this: when people survive something together in this world - a rough plane landing, an attack, a train accident, a sinking ship - even if they didn’t know each other before, what happens? The survivors form a group. They get together every year. They have this natural love for each other just because they were together surviving something.

What did we survive? Hellfire. If a bus accident can bring people together and they meet every year because of that shared experience, what kind of togetherness should come between people who share the understanding that Allah ﷻ saved us from hellfire? How can you hate each other? He is the One who rescued you from it.

Self-Righteousness as an Obstacle to Unity

One of the biggest obstacles to unity is self-righteousness. You might have been living a life far from Islam, then you tasted Islam and became committed. Now your friends are still where you were, and you become judgmental of them. You look down on them, thinking they’re losers, forgetting very easily what you were just two years ago.

Allah ﷻ reminds you: you were at the edge of hellfire. It wasn’t you who saved yourself. It wasn’t your decision alone. Your decision is only half the story. It is Allah’s ﷻ blessing that He rescued you from it. You didn’t earn salvation - He gifted it to you. So how dare you act like you own it? How dare you judge people who haven’t been gifted it yet?

كَذَٰلِكَ يُبَيِّنُ ٱللَّهُ لَكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ لَعَلَّكُمْ تَهْتَدُونَ - this is how Allah ﷻ clarifies to you His miraculous signs, so that all of you may remain committed to guidance.

Ayah 104

وَلْتَكُن مِّنكُمْ أُمَّةٌ يَدْعُونَ إِلَى ٱلْخَيْرِ وَيَأْمُرُونَ بِٱلْمَعْرُوفِ وَيَنْهَوْنَ عَنِ ٱلْمُنكَرِ ۚ وَأُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ هُمُ ٱلْمُفْلِحُونَ

The Rise of a Nation Within the Nation
