The فَعُول form (as opposed to مَفْعُول) indicates he himself chose this - it’s his active decision to abstain, not that he was made incapable (which would be مَحْصُور). He voluntarily remains chaste and cut off from worldly desires. This is part of Allah honoring Zakariya’s (AS) du’a for “ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً” (pure offspring) - Allah gives him a son pure from worldly wants, exclusively dedicated to Allah’s mission.

نَبِيًّا (nabiyyan) [ن-ب-أ]

The root ن-ب-أ relates to news, prophecy, information from the unseen. نَبِيّ means a prophet - one who receives revelation from Allah.

الصَّالِحِينَ (as-salihin) [ص-ل-ح]

The root ص-ل-ح means to be righteous, upright, good, reformed. الصَّالِحِينَ are the righteous ones. “وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ” - a prophet from among the righteous. This doesn’t just mean from among good people generally, but from the lineage of righteous prophets who came before. Allah is telling Zakariya (AS): your son will continue the blessed prophetic lineage, upholding the goodness that was given to previous prophets in this line. You’re continuing the legacy of prophecy in your family.

Ayah 40

أَنَّىٰ (anna)

Again, “how” - but the interpretation here is debated. It could be shock: “How can this be possible?!” But a better reading, given that Zakariya (AS) just made du’a believing it’s possible, is that this is a “how” of joy and excitement: “How wonderful! I’m going to have a boy? Really?!” This is a happy, amazed “how,” not disbelief. A prophet wouldn’t make du’a while thinking it’s impossible - the essence of du’a is believing anything is possible with Allah. His response expresses joyful amazement at Allah’s immediate answer.

غُلَامٌ (ghulam) [غ-ل-م]

The root غ-ل-م means a boy, a youth (typically 9-10 years old and older). غُلَام is not an infant but a boy who has grown. Zakariya (AS) doesn’t say “طِفْل” (baby/child) but غُلَام - indicating not only will a baby be born, but he’ll grow to boyhood and develop these noble qualities even in his youth. In another passage, Allah says “يَا يَحْيَىٰ خُذِ الْكِتَابَ بِقُوَّةٍ” - even as a boy, Yahya (AS) will have strength and knowledge.

بَلَغَنِيَ (balaghaniya) [ب-ل-غ]

The root ب-ل-غ means to reach, to attain. “قَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ” - old age has reached me. Notice the phrasing: old age reached me, not “I reached old age” (which would be بَلَغْتُ الْكِبَرَ). This subtlety suggests: old age may have reached me, but that doesn’t mean my capabilities are gone. Allah can put old age on hold and restore capability.

الْكِبَرُ (al-kibar) [ك-ب-ر]

From the root ك-ب-ر meaning to be great, large, old. الْكِبَرُ specifically means old age, advanced years.

امْرَأَتِي (imra’ati) [م-ر-أ]

Means “my wife.” The root م-ر-أ relates to woman.

عَاقِرٌ (ʿaqir) [ع-ق-ر]

The root ع-ق-ر means barren, sterile, unable to conceive. عَاقِر describes a woman who cannot bear children. “وَامْرَأَتِي عَاقِرٌ” - and my wife is barren. She’s been barren from the beginning, even in her youth.

كَذَٰلِكَ (kadhalika)

Means “thus” or “like that” or “such is the case.” The angel responds: “كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” - That is how Allah does what He wills. This is Allah’s way - He does what He wants.

يَفْعَلُ (yafʿalu) [ف-ع-ل]

The root ف-ع-ل means to do, to act. يَفْعَلُ is the present tense: Allah does, He acts. The present tense indicates continuous action - Allah continuously does as He wills.

يَشَاءُ (yasha’u) [ش-ي-أ]

The root ش-ي-أ means to will, to want, to desire. يَشَاءُ means He wills, He wants. “اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” - Allah does whatever He wills. What is Allah doing here? Making an old man capable of fathering a child, making a barren woman conceive in old age when it’s naturally impossible. This is what Allah does - He overrides natural limitations by His will.

Ayah 41

آيَةً (ayatan) [أ-ي-ي]

The root أ-ي-ي relates to signs, miracles, verses. آيَة means a sign, a miracle, a proof. Zakariya (AS) asks for a sign as confirmation.

رَبِّ (rabbi) [ر-ب-ب]

From ر-ب-ب, meaning “My Lord.” He addresses Allah intimately.

اجْعَل (ijʿal) [ج-ع-ل]

The root ج-ع-ل means to make, to set, to appoint. اجْعَل is the command form: “Make for me” or “Grant me.”

آيَةً (ayatan)

A sign, as mentioned above. He wants a sign to confirm the good news.

آيَتُكَ (ayatuka)

Your sign - the sign Allah will give.

تُكَلِّمَ (tukallimu) [ك-ل-م]

The root ك-ل-م means to speak. تُكَلِّمَ is the second form, meaning to speak to someone. “أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ” - that you not speak to the people.

النَّاسَ (an-nas) [ن-و-س]

Means the people, mankind. From the root ن-و-س.

ثَلَاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ (thalathata ayyam)

Three days. ثَلَاثَة means three, أَيَّام (plural of يَوْم) means days.

رَمْزًا (ramzan) [ر-م-ز]

The root ر-م-ز means to gesture, to signal, to indicate without speech. رَمْز means a sign, a gesture, symbolic communication. “إِلَّا رَمْزًا” - except through gestures/signals. He will not be able to speak verbally but can communicate through signs.

اذْكُر (udhkur) [ذ-ك-ر]

The root ذ-ك-ر means to remember, to mention, to invoke. اذْكُر is the command: “Remember!” or “Invoke!” “وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا” - And remember your Lord abundantly. Interestingly, Allah says “اذْكُر” (remember Allah) to Zakariya, whose very name means “one who remembers Allah” - almost translating his name in the command.

كَثِيرًا (kathiran) [ك-ث-ر]

The root ك-ث-ر means to be many, abundant, much. كَثِيرًا means abundantly, frequently, much. Remember Allah much.

سَبِّحْ (sabbih) [س-ب-ح]

The root س-ب-ح means to glorify, to declare perfection, to swim (metaphorically swimming in Allah’s praise). سَبِّحْ is the command: “Glorify!” Declare Allah’s perfection.

بِالْعَشِيِّ (bil-ʿashiyy) [ع-ش-و]

From the root ع-ش-و meaning evening. بِالْعَشِيِّ means in the evening, at dusk.

الْإِبْكَارِ (al-ibkar) [ب-ك-ر]

The root ب-ك-ر means early morning, dawn. الْإِبْكَار refers to the early morning time. “وَالْإِبْكَارِ” - and in the early morning. So glorify Allah in the evening and early morning - the two ends of the day, continuously.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 37

The shift from “فَتَقَبَّلَ مِنْهَا” (expected: He accepted from her) to “فَتَقَبَّلَهَا” (He accepted her) is rhetorically powerful. The direct object pronoun ها refers to Maryam, but the flexibility of Arabic allows it to mean Allah accepted the mother entirely, accepted the child entirely, or accepted the prayer (دَعْوَة) itself. This ambiguity enriches meaning - Allah’s acceptance transcends the specific prayer to encompass totality.

The phrase “وَأَنبَتَهَا نَبَاتًا حَسَنًا” uses the cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق) construction - a verb followed by its own masdar for emphasis: “caused her to grow with beautiful growth.” This intensifies the meaning and emphasizes the excellence of her upbringing.

The switch from the mother’s prayer to Maryam’s raising shows Allah answering on multiple levels - accepting the dedication and then describing the fulfillment of that dedication through Maryam’s beautiful growth.

The word order “كَفَلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا” places the verb first, then the object (her), then the subject (Zakariya), which is marked stylistic emphasis in Arabic. Standard order would be subject-verb-object. This fronting draws attention to the act of guardianship itself, and to Maryam, before revealing who the guardian is.

The phrase “كُلَّمَا دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا زَكَرِيَّا الْمِحْرَابَ” uses كُلَّمَا (every time, whenever) to indicate repetition and consistency - this wasn’t a one-time occurrence but a regular pattern. The verb دَخَلَ عَلَيْهَا (he entered upon her) is intimate language, suggesting he went into her private quarters, her special space.

The use of عِنْدَهَا (with her, in her company) indicates the food was right there with her, in her presence, not stored elsewhere. The definiteness of الْمِحْرَابَ (THE sanctuary) suggests this was a known, specific place dedicated for her worship.

Maryam’s response “هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ” uses the pronoun هُوَ (it) at the beginning for emphasis - “IT is from Allah” (not from any human source). The preposition مِنْ عِندِ indicates source and company - it comes from Allah’s presence.

The addition of “إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ” is her theological statement generalizing from her experience. She doesn’t claim special status; rather, she frames it as Allah’s normal way of providing. The inna of emphasis (إِنَّ) adds certainty. The present tense يَرْزُقُ indicates continuous, ongoing action. The relative clause “مَن يَشَاءُ” (whoever He wills) is indefinite and general - anyone can be a recipient of this limitless provision.

Ayah 38

The adverb “هُنَالِكَ” (right then and there) is emphatic, indicating immediacy - at that very moment, in that very place, without delay, Zakariya (AS) was moved to make du’a. This shows cause and effect: seeing divine provision inspired immediate supplication.

The phrase “دَعَا زَكَرِيَّا رَبَّهُ” places the verb first (called), then the subject (Zakariya), then the object (his Lord) - creating emphasis on the act of calling/supplicating. The possessive “رَبَّهُ” (his Lord) personalizes the relationship.

“رَبِّ هَبْ لِي” uses the vocative “يَا” implicitly (O my Lord) followed by the imperative هَبْ (grant/give as a gift). The use of the imperative with Allah shows the intimacy and directness permitted in du’a - not disrespectful, but showing the believer’s confidence in approaching Allah.

“مِن لَّدُنكَ” employs the preposition مِن (from) with لَدُن (Your special closeness/presence) rather than the more common مِن عِندِكَ. This elevates the request - he’s asking for something from Allah’s intimate, exclusive treasure, not just general provision. The kaf suffix (كَ) makes it second person: “from YOUR special presence.”

The phrase “ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً” uses two indefinite nouns - “offspring” and “good/pure” - showing he’s asking for quality, not quantity. The adjective طَيِّبَة modifies and specifies the type of offspring desired. He doesn’t ask for children in general, but specifically for righteous, pure, wholesome children.

“إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ” employs multiple layers of emphasis: (1) إِنَّ (certainly), (2) the separated pronoun أَنتَ (You Yourself), (3) the definite article in السَّمِيعُ (THE All-Hearing One), and (4) the definite الدُّعَاءِ (THE supplication/all supplications). This piling of emphasis shows absolute certainty and trust. The word order - You are the All-Hearing of du’a - makes it a statement of Allah’s eternal attribute, not contingent on anything.

Ayah 39

The verb “نَادَتْهُ” (they called him) is third person feminine plural, which is used for non-human plurals or for grammatical agreement with الْمَلَائِكَةُ (the angels), which is treated as a plural. However, the plural form (rather than a singular or dual) suggests multiple angels called him, not just one. This indicates the magnitude and honor of the announcement.

“وَهُوَ قَائِمٌ يُصَلِّي فِي الْمِحْرَابِ” is a circumstantial clause (حال) - describing the state Zakariya (AS) was in when the angels called. He was standing (قَائِمٌ), praying (يُصَلِّي), in the sanctuary (فِي الْمِحْرَابِ). The participle قَائِمٌ emphasizes the standing posture specifically, among all prayer postures. The verb يُصَلِّي is present tense, indicating ongoing action - he was in the midst of prayer.

The phrase “أَنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكَ بِيَحْيَىٰ” has a complex structure. The أَنَّ clause functions as the content of what the angels announced. “Allah gives you glad tidings” uses the second person كَ (you) directly addressing Zakariya (AS). The preposition بِ introduces what the glad tidings are about - Yahya.

Going straight to the name “بِيَحْيَىٰ” (of Yahya) without first saying “you will have a boy” is striking. The name is given directly by Allah before birth, unlike the mother of Maryam who chose the name herself. This shows divine prerogative and honor.

“مُصَدِّقًا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ” uses a present participle (اسم فاعل) “مُصَدِّقًا” in the accusative as a حال (state/description) - describing Yahya’s role: he will be “one who confirms.” The baa preposition (بِ) indicates what he confirms: “a word from Allah.” The phrase “مِّنَ اللَّهِ” (from Allah) modifies “word” - a word that comes from Allah, which interpreters understand as ’Isa (AS).

“وَسَيِّدًا وَحَصُورًا وَنَبِيًّا مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ” lists four attributes in succession with coordinating وَ (and): a leader, chaste, a prophet, from among the righteous. These are all in the accusative case, parallel to “مُصَدِّقًا,” continuing the description of Yahya (AS). Each attribute is definite or specified, showing these are certain, established qualities he will have.

The final phrase “مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ” (from among the righteous) uses the preposition مِن (from) to indicate source or membership - he will be من (from/among) the category of righteous prophets, continuing a blessed lineage.

Ayah 40

“قَالَ رَبِّ أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ” begins with the vocative “رَبِّ” (My Lord) showing Zakariya (AS) is addressing Allah directly. The question “أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ” can be read two ways grammatically: (1) as shock/disbelief - “How can I have a boy?” or (2) as joyful amazement - “How wonderful that I will have a boy!” The context supports the second reading because he just made du’a believing it’s possible, and prophets don’t make du’a in disbelief.

The structure “يَكُونُ لِي” uses the verb كَانَ (to be) with the preposition لِ (for/to) - literally “there will be for me.” This construction indicates possession or occurrence. “غُلَامٌ” (a boy) is the subject of يَكُونُ - “a boy will be for me.”

“وَقَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ” uses وَقَدْ (and indeed/certainly) which adds emphasis. The verb “بَلَغَنِيَ” (it reached me) makes old age the subject - old age reached me, not I reached old age. This passive-like construction (though not technically passive) subtly suggests: yes, old age came to me, but Allah can override it. The definiteness of “الْكِبَرُ” (THE old age) indicates a known, recognized state - advanced old age.

“وَامْرَأَتِي عَاقِرٌ” is a nominal sentence (جملة اسمية) - my wife (subject) is barren (predicate). The lack of a verb makes it a timeless statement - she is and has always been barren. The indefiniteness of “عَاقِرٌ” (barren) emphasizes the quality itself.

The response “قَالَ كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” begins with “كَذَٰلِكَ” (thus/like that) - a demonstrative pointing back to what was just said: this situation you described (old age, barren wife) - THAT is exactly the context in which Allah does what He wills. “اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ” (Allah does) places Allah as the subject, followed by the verb, showing Allah is the active doer. “مَا يَشَاءُ” is a relative clause (whatever He wills) - the object of the verb. The present tense of both verbs (يَفْعَلُ and يَشَاءُ) indicates continuous, timeless action - Allah always does whatever He wills, in every situation.

Ayah 41

“قَالَ رَبِّ اجْعَل لِّي آيَةً” uses the imperative “اجْعَل” (make/grant) showing a direct request. “لِّي” (for me) specifies the beneficiary. “آيَةً” (a sign) is the object - he asks for a sign as confirmation of the glad tidings.

“قَالَ آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلَاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا” presents the sign: “Your sign is that you will not speak to people for three days except through gestures.” The structure “آيَتُكَ أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ” makes “أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ” (that you not speak) the predicate - your sign IS this: not speaking. The أَنْ with لَا creates a nominal clause. “تُكَلِّمَ” is in the subjunctive after أَلَّا. “النَّاسَ” (people) is the object of speaking - you won’t speak to people. “ثَلَاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ” (three days) is an adverbial of time (ظرف زمان) - for the duration of three days. “إِلَّا رَمْزًا” (except gestures) uses the exception particle إِلَّا - the only permitted communication will be رَمْزًا (through symbolic gestures/signs).

“وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا” employs the imperative “اذْكُر” (remember/invoke), followed by “رَّبَّكَ” (your Lord) as the direct object, then “كَثِيرًا” (much/abundantly) as an adverbial modifier (حال or مفعول مطلق نوعي) describing how to remember - abundantly, frequently.

“وَسَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ” uses another imperative “سَبِّحْ” (glorify), followed by time expressions: “بِالْعَشِيِّ” (in the evening) and “وَالْإِبْكَارِ” (and the early morning). The baa preposition (بِ) indicates the time frame. The two time periods mentioned represent the two ends of the day, creating a merism (mentioning two extremes to mean the whole) - glorify Allah throughout the day, continuously, at all times.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 37

The passage corrects and clarifies details found in non-canonical Christian texts, particularly the Infancy Gospel (Protevangelion of James). Those texts claim that when Maryam reached puberty (around 12-16 years old), the priests wanted to remove her from the temple and marry her off to a man named Joseph the carpenter, as she could no longer remain in the temple. They describe a selection process where staffs were gathered, and Joseph’s staff miraculously bloomed, indicating he should be her guardian.

The Qur’an removes all of this from the narrative. There is no Joseph figure. There is no marriage or betrothal. Maryam (AS) remained under Zakariya’s (AS) direct guardianship the entire time - “كَفَلَهَا زَكَرِيَّا” - he was responsible for her from beginning to end. The Qur’an clarifies that the miraculous food appeared while she was still in the mihrab, her dedicated place of worship, and soon after, the angels appeared to her in the same place announcing ’Isa (AS). She did not move out, did not go to someone else’s house, did not get married. This preserves her honor and corrects the confused narrative that had developed in Christian tradition.

The concept of dedicating a child to temple service has roots in Jewish tradition. Hannah’s dedication of Samuel (1 Samuel 1-2) is a parallel - she vowed to dedicate her child to the Lord’s service if granted a son, and Samuel grew up in the temple under the priest Eli. Similarly, the mother of Maryam dedicated her child, but in this case, the child was female, which was unexpected. The Qur’an honors this dedication while showing that Allah accepted it in a way that transcended social expectations.

Zakariya (AS) was the imam of the community, the leading religious authority - essentially the high priest or chief rabbi. He was also a prophet, though his prophecy was not widely known or accepted by the Jewish religious establishment of his time. His taking guardianship of Maryam signifies that she was raised by the most righteous, knowledgeable person available - under prophetic care.

The mihrab (sanctuary) where Maryam stayed was likely part of the Second Temple complex in Jerusalem or a dedicated space associated with it. Jewish tradition did have provisions for temple service by dedicated individuals, though typically male. The Qur’an doesn’t problematize her gender in this service - Allah simply accepted her and provided for her miraculously.

Ayah 38

Zakariya’s (AS) prayer echoes prayers found in the Hebrew Bible, particularly Hannah’s prayer (1 Samuel 1:11) and Abraham’s supplications for offspring. The theme of barrenness overcome by divine intervention is prominent in biblical narratives: Sarah and Abraham, Rebekah and Isaac, Rachel and Jacob, Hannah and Elkanah. In each case, the child born from such miraculous circumstances became significant in salvation history - Isaac, Jacob, Joseph, Samuel. Here, Yahya (AS) will be born miraculously and will play a crucial role in confirming and supporting ’Isa (AS).

The request for “ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً” (good, pure offspring) reflects a deep awareness that righteousness is not inherited automatically. Throughout prophetic history, there are examples of righteous fathers whose sons did not follow their path - Prophet Nuh (AS) had a disbelieving son (11:42-46), Prophet Lut (AS) had a disbelieving wife, Prophet Ibrahim (AS) had a son (Isma’il AS) who was righteous and another context involved Azar who was not on guidance. Thus, Zakariya (AS) asks not merely for biological offspring but for a child who will carry forward his spiritual legacy.

The immediacy of Zakariya’s (AS) prayer after witnessing Maryam’s provision establishes a principle seen throughout scripture: witnessing Allah’s miracles should inspire increased faith and bold supplication. This parallels instances where prophets, after seeing Allah’s power, made even greater requests - Musa (AS) asking to see Allah after witnessing miracles (7:143), Ibrahim (AS) asking for a demonstration of how Allah gives life to the dead after already believing (2:260).

Ayah 39

The angelic announcement to Zakariya (AS) parallels the announcement to Maryam (AS) later in the surah, and both find echoes in the Gospel of Luke (1:5-25 for Zakariya/Zechariah, 1:26-38 for Maryam/Mary). However, the Qur’anic account differs in key details and emphasizes different aspects.

In Luke 1:11-20, the angel Gabriel appears to Zechariah while he is serving in the temple, burning incense. The angel announces the birth of John (Yahya), describing him as one who “will be great in the sight of the Lord” and “will be filled with the Holy Spirit even before he is born.” The Gospel mentions he will “never take wine or other fermented drink” and will “bring back many of the people of Israel to the Lord their God.” When Zechariah expresses doubt (“How can I be sure of this? I am an old man and my wife is well along in years”), he is struck mute as a sign until the child’s birth.

The Qur’anic account adds dimensions not found in the biblical text: (1) The name Yahya is stated to be unprecedented - “لَمْ نَجْعَل لَّهُ مِن قَبْلُ سَمِيًّا” - no one before was given this name. This is confirmed even in the Gospel of Luke 1:61: “None of your relatives has this name.” (2) Yahya’s role as “مُصَدِّقًا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ” - confirmer of ’Isa (AS) - is explicit. The Gospels present John the Baptist as the forerunner who prepares the way, but the Qur’an emphasizes his role as a confirmer and supporter. (3) The qualities of سَيِّد (leader), حَصُور (chaste), and نَبِيّ (prophet) are clearly stated, whereas the Gospel focuses on his role as preparer.

Historically, Yahya (AS) / John the Baptist lived during a time of intense messianic expectation among Jews under Roman occupation. Multiple movements and claimants arose. Yahya’s (AS) message of repentance and baptism in the Jordan River attracted large followings (Matthew 3, Mark 1, Luke 3, John 1). The Qur’an situates him as a prophet in his own right, not merely a supporting character, while also confirming his role in relation to ’Isa (AS).

The corrupt religious leadership mentioned (“الْمَلَأَ مِن بَعْدِهِ” - the elites after him, from 19:5) refers to the Pharisees, Sadducees, and temple authorities whom both Yahya (AS) and ’Isa (AS) confronted. Yahya famously called them a “brood of vipers” (Matthew 3:7, Luke 3:7) and criticized Herod Antipas for marrying his brother’s wife, which ultimately led to his execution (Matthew 14:1-12, Mark 6:14-29).

Ayah 40

Zakariya’s (AS) response “أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ” echoes several biblical precedents of questioning divine announcements of miraculous births. Abraham laughed when told Sarah would bear a son (Genesis 17:17), and Sarah laughed as well (Genesis 18:12). In both cases, they questioned how this could be possible given their advanced age. Zakariya’s (AS) question here can be read as wonder and joy rather than doubt, unlike the biblical Zechariah whose doubt is more explicit and results in being struck mute as a mild punishment (Luke 1:18-20).

The emphasis on old age (“بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ”) and the wife’s barrenness (“امْرَأَتِي عَاقِرٌ”) highlights the impossibility from a natural standpoint, making the miracle more pronounced. Throughout biblical history, such impossibilities were overcome: Abraham and Sarah (Genesis 21), Isaac and Rebekah (Genesis 25:21), Jacob and Rachel (Genesis 30:22-24), Elkanah and Hannah (1 Samuel 1). Each time, the birth served a greater purpose in Allah’s plan.

The angel’s response - “كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” (Thus Allah does what He wills) - affirms divine sovereignty over natural laws. This principle is repeatedly demonstrated in the Qur’an and previous scriptures: Allah creates ’Isa (AS) without a father (3:59), creates Adam (AS) without parents (3:59), creates Hawwa from Adam (various narrations), and creates the rest of humanity from male and female. Allah’s creative power is not bound by biological norms.

Ayah 41

Zakariya’s (AS) request for a sign (“اجْعَل لِّي آيَةً”) parallels other prophetic requests for signs: Musa (AS) asked for a sign when commissioned (Exodus 4), Hizqeel (Ezekiel) was given various signs, and ’Isa (AS) himself provided signs to demonstrate his prophethood. Signs serve to strengthen faith and provide tangible confirmation of divine promises.

The specific sign - not speaking to people for three days except through gestures (“أَلَّا تُكَلِّمَ النَّاسَ ثَلَاثَةَ أَيَّامٍ إِلَّا رَمْزًا”) - has a parallel in Luke 1:20-22, where Zechariah is struck mute until the birth of John. The Gospel presents this as a consequence of doubt, whereas the Qur’an presents it as a requested sign that Zakariya (AS) will bear with patience. Some narrations suggest he could still recite Qur’an (or in his time, perhaps Torah and prayers), but couldn’t engage in ordinary speech with people.

The command to remember and glorify Allah abundantly (“وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا وَسَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ”) during this time of silence emphasizes that the silence from worldly speech should be filled with spiritual speech - dhikr and tasbih. This transforms the sign from a mere restriction into a spiritual retreat, an opportunity for increased devotion.

The mention of evening (عَشِيّ) and early morning (إِبْكَار) as times for glorification connects to Jewish prayer times (Shacharit in the morning, Mincha in the afternoon, Ma’ariv in the evening) and Islamic prayer times that would later be established. Throughout prophetic tradition, these transitional times of day held special spiritual significance - dawn and dusk, when day meets night, symbolizing the meeting of the physical and spiritual realms.

Coherence

Ayah 37

This ayah continues directly from the preceding verses where the mother of Maryam made her dedication and du’a. Ayah 36 ended with her saying “I have named her Maryam, and I seek refuge for her and her offspring from Shaytan the rejected.” Ayah 37 immediately responds with “فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا” (So her Lord accepted her) - Allah’s direct answer to the mother’s prayer. This immediate response pattern is consistent throughout the surah: sincere du’a is followed by divine acceptance and provision.

The theme of miraculous provision begins here and will continue throughout the surah. Just as Maryam receives out-of-season fruit miraculously, later Zakariya (AS) will receive a child out-of-season (in old age), and then Maryam herself will receive a child without the normal means (’Isa AS). This establishes a thematic pattern: Allah provides beyond natural means when He wills, for those who dedicate themselves to Him.

The mihrab (المحراب) mentioned here will be mentioned again when the angels appear to Maryam (3:42) in the same place, creating location continuity. The mihrab represents a space of devotion, separation from worldly concerns, and direct connection to Allah - it becomes the site of multiple divine interventions in this narrative.

Zakariya’s (AS) repeated visits to Maryam and finding miraculous provision sets up his own supplication in the next ayah. The coherence is: witnessing Allah’s provision for Maryam inspires Zakariya (AS) to ask for provision (a child) for himself, knowing that the same Allah who provides fruit out of season can provide a child out of season.

Ayah 38

“هُنَالِكَ” (right then and there) creates immediate temporal and spatial connection to the previous ayah. At that very moment of seeing the provision, in that very place (the mihrab), Zakariya (AS) made du’a. This shows how Allah’s signs in one person’s life should inspire others to make du’a - a principle repeated throughout the Qur’an.

Zakariya’s (AS) du’a “هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنكَ ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً” echoes Maryam’s statement “هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ” but elevates it with “لَدُن” (special closeness) instead of just “عِنْد” (from). He’s asking for the same category of blessing - something that can only come directly from Allah’s special treasures, just as Maryam’s food came.

This ayah also connects back to the opening du’as of the surah. In ayah 8, believers make du’a: “رَبَّنَا لَا تُزِغْ قُلُوبَنَا بَعْدَ إِذْ هَدَيْتَنَا وَهَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ رَحْمَةً” (Our Lord, do not let our hearts deviate after You have guided us, and grant us from Your special mercy). The word “هَبْ لَنَا مِن لَّدُنكَ” is nearly identical to Zakariya’s “هَبْ لِي مِن لَّدُنكَ.” This creates thematic unity - the surah teaches us HOW to make du’a by giving us models: first the believers’ collective du’a, then Maryam’s mother’s du’a, now Zakariya’s du’a, and later Maryam’s acceptance of Allah’s decree. Each builds on the previous, teaching different aspects of supplication and submission.

The attribute “السَّمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ” (the All-Hearing of du’a) connects to the surah’s emphasis on Allah responding to sincere calls. Earlier, the mother’s prayer was accepted; now Zakariya’s will be answered. The surah is demonstrating that Allah truly is السَّمِيع - He hears and responds.

Ayah 39

The immediate response - “فَنَادَتْهُ الْمَلَائِكَةُ” (then the angels called him) - creates a parallel to ayah 37’s “فَتَقَبَّلَهَا رَبُّهَا” (so her Lord accepted her). Both use the conjunction فَ to indicate immediate divine response. This parallel teaches: when you make sincere du’a while in a state of devotion, Allah’s response can be immediate.

The detail “وَهُوَ قَائِمٌ يُصَلِّي فِي الْمِحْرَابِ” (while he was standing, praying in the mihrab) connects back to where the previous angelic/miraculous intervention occurred - in Maryam’s mihrab. The mihrab is becoming established as the place where heaven and earth meet, where divine messages are delivered. This will be reinforced when angels appear to Maryam in her mihrab (3:42-43).

The announcement of Yahya’s name and qualities prepares for the later announcement to Maryam about ’Isa (AS) in ayah 45. Both announcements follow similar structures: angels giving glad tidings, naming the child, listing noble qualities. However, Yahya is “مُصَدِّقًا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ” (confirming a word from Allah), which creates forward linkage - this “word” is ’Isa (AS), who will be described as “كَلِمَةٌ مِّنْهُ” (a word from Him) in ayah 45. The relationship between Yahya and ’Isa is thus established before ’Isa’s birth is even announced.

Yahya being described as “سَيِّدًا” (a leader) despite being in a supporting role to ’Isa (AS) teaches an important lesson for the surah’s audience: supporting the truth doesn’t diminish one’s status. In fact, being the first to confirm the truth (’Isa AS) is itself a position of leadership. This is relevant to the believers addressed at the beginning of the surah, who are called to be witnesses (3:18) and uphold truth.

The quality “حَصُورًا” (chaste, abstinent) will contrast with accusations later in the surah against those who indulge in desires while claiming spiritual authority. Yahya’s chastity makes him purely devoted to his mission, just as Maryam’s chastity (later emphasized) makes her purely devoted to worship.

Ayah 40

Zakariya’s (AS) question “أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي غُلَامٌ” doesn’t express doubt (as some biblical accounts suggest) but rather wonder and joy, because he JUST asked for this in ayah 38. The coherence is perfect: you ask, Allah announces He’s giving it, you express amazed gratitude at how this will happen. This teaches the proper etiquette of receiving Allah’s answer - not suspicion, but joyful amazement.

The mention of his old age and his wife’s barrenness serves multiple purposes in the surah’s coherence: (1) It emphasizes that Allah provides “بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ” (without limits) as Maryam said in ayah 37 - He’s not limited by biology. (2) It prepares for the even greater miracle coming: Maryam conceiving without ANY husband, not just an old/barren couple conceiving. The miracles escalate in impossibility: out-of-season fruit → old couple’s baby → fatherless baby. (3) It demonstrates that even prophets experience natural human limitations and turn to Allah to overcome them.

The response “كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” (Thus Allah does what He wills) will be echoed later when Maryam asks “أَنَّىٰ يَكُونُ لِي وَلَدٌ” (How can I have a child? 3:47), and the response will be “كَذَٰلِكِ اللَّهُ يَخْلُقُ مَا يَشَاءُ” (Thus Allah creates what He wills). The parallel is intentional: both Zakariya and Maryam question the “how,” and both receive the same essential answer - Allah’s will transcends natural means. The slight variation (يَفْعَلُ vs. يَخْلُقُ) is significant: for Zakariya, Allah “does” (uses existing means in extraordinary ways), but for Maryam, Allah “creates” (brings into existence without normal means at all).

Ayah 41

Zakariya’s (AS) request for a sign follows naturally from the announcement - he wants confirmation and something tangible to hold onto during the waiting period. This mirrors earlier prophets: Musa (AS) asked for signs (7:106), Ibrahim (AS) asked for a demonstration (2:260). The surah teaches that asking for signs when Allah has promised something is not doubt, but a means of strengthening one’s faith and patience.

The sign given - temporary inability to speak to people - serves multiple purposes within the surah’s themes: (1) It removes him from worldly speech so he can focus on dhikr (remembrance of Allah), which fits the surah’s emphasis on devotion and remembrance. (2) It’s a symbolic death and rebirth - his speech dies temporarily and will be restored, just as his ability to father children was “dead” and is being restored. (3) It creates anticipation and builds toward the moment when he will speak again at the birth, which is recounted in Surah Maryam (19:10-11).

The command “وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا” (remember your Lord much) ties back to his very name - Zakariya, meaning one who remembers Allah. Allah is essentially saying: live up to your name during this period. This also connects to the surah’s opening, which mentions those with dhikr (3:7, those of understanding remember).

The times mentioned - “بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ” (evening and early morning) - will be echoed later when Maryam is commanded: “وَاسْجُدِي وَارْكَعِي مَعَ الرَّاكِعِينَ” (prostrate and bow with those who bow, 3:43). Both Zakariya and Maryam are given devotional instructions during their waiting periods, emphasizing that times of anticipation for Allah’s promises should be filled with increased worship.

This ayah closes the Zakariya-Yahya narrative for now (it will resume in Surah Maryam), and the immediate next passage returns to Maryam and the announcement of ’Isa (AS), maintaining the parallel between these two miraculous births throughout the surah.

Psychological, Political & Social

Ayah 37

The ayah reveals profound psychological insights about human inspiration and spiritual aspiration. Zakariya (AS), a prophet, is inspired by witnessing Allah’s provision for Maryam. This teaches that even the most spiritually elevated individuals learn from observing Allah’s blessings upon others. The healthy psychological response to seeing someone else blessed is not jealousy or comparison, but inspiration to ask Allah for one’s own needs. This counters toxic social dynamics where people resent others’ success or blessings.

Maryam’s calm response - “هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ” (It is from Allah) - demonstrates remarkable psychological maturity and spiritual certainty. She doesn’t boast, doesn’t claim special status, doesn’t create mystery around the provision. She simply attributes it to Allah and generalizes the principle: Allah provides for everyone without limits. This is the psychology of a person completely secure in their relationship with Allah - no need for attention-seeking, no need to appear special. She deflects attention from herself to Allah’s attribute of providing.

The social dimension is significant: a young girl is being cared for by the leading religious authority (Zakariya AS), and he’s repeatedly finding inexplicable provision with her. In a gossipy, suspicious society, this could lead to terrible rumors - “Where is she getting this food? Who’s visiting her? Is the guardian showing favoritism?” But the narrative presents this as a pure, miracle-witnessed-by-a-prophet scenario. Allah protected her reputation even in these circumstances, answering her mother’s du’a: “I seek refuge for her… from Shaytan.” Social protection is part of Allah’s acceptance.

From a political angle, this establishes that religious leadership (represented by Zakariya AS) recognizes and supports genuine spiritual devotion, even when it manifests in unexpected ways (a young woman in temple service, receiving miracles). This would become relevant later when corrupt religious authorities would reject both Yahya (AS) and ’Isa (AS) despite clear signs.

Ayah 38

Psychologically, this ayah teaches the importance of seizing moments of spiritual inspiration. Zakariya (AS) doesn’t delay - “هُنَالِكَ” (right then and there) - he makes du’a immediately upon witnessing the miracle. This captures a profound human truth: moments of witnessing Allah’s power create windows of heightened faith and boldness in supplication. If he had waited, rationalization might have crept in (“Maybe I imagined it,” “Maybe it’s not appropriate to ask,” “I’m too old anyway”). Acting on spiritual inspiration immediately is psychologically sound.

The content of his du’a reveals a mature understanding of parenthood. He doesn’t just ask for a child (biological success), but “ذُرِّيَّةً طَيِّبَةً” (good offspring) - emphasizing character and righteousness. This reflects a psychological truth: parents’ deepest desire, when spiritually aware, is not just for children but for righteous children. Worldly success without righteousness leaves a void.

Socially, Zakariya (AS) recognizes that his prophetic status doesn’t guarantee righteous offspring. This humility is crucial - positions, lineages, family names don’t automatically transfer spiritual success. Each person must be guided individually. This counters aristocratic or dynastic thinking prevalent in many societies (“Our family is religious, so our kids will automatically be good”).

Politically, his du’a for offspring has implications: prophets need successors to carry the message forward. In the context of corrupt religious leadership (which he fears, as mentioned in Surah Maryam 19:5), having a righteous son means having someone who will uphold truth when he’s gone. Leadership succession in religious communities is critical - without righteous successors, corruption sets in.

The phrase “إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ” (Certainly You are the All-Hearing of du’a) despite years of unanswered prayer teaches patience and trust. Psychologically, this is profound: you can pray for years without apparent answer and still maintain certainty that Allah hears. This defeats despair and cynicism that often accompany delayed responses. It models healthy spiritual psychology - trust in Allah’s hearing even when you don’t see immediate results.

Ayah 39

The arrival of angels during salah teaches that devotional states attract divine communication. Psychologically, when humans are most focused on Allah (in prayer), they’re most receptive to guidance and inspiration. This is universally true: moments of prayer, meditation, and connection with the Divine are when insights, solutions, and guidance often come.

The announcement itself - Allah congratulates you of Yahya - demonstrates how Allah honors His servants. Being congratulated by Allah (not just informed, but congratulated) elevates the psychological state of the believer. This teaches that Allah doesn’t merely give; He gives with honor, with joy, with celebration of His servants.

The qualities listed for Yahya paint a psychological profile of an ideal believer: (1) “مُصَدِّقًا” (confirmer) - he supports truth even when it’s unpopular, (2) “سَيِّدًا” (leader) - he takes responsibility and doesn’t follow crowds, (3) “حَصُورًا” (chaste) - he controls desires rather than being controlled by them, (4) “نَبِيًّا مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ” (prophet among the righteous) - he’s part of a tradition, not innovating a new path. These qualities together create a personality of principled leadership.

Socially, Yahya’s role as “مُصَدِّقًا بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنَ اللَّهِ” (confirmer of ’Isa AS) establishes that supporting the truth is a noble role, even if you’re not the central figure. In societies obsessed with being number one, being the lead, this teaches that the first supporter, the first believer, has an honored position. Abu Bakr’s (RA) greatness was in being “الصِّدِّيق” - the greatest confirmer of the Prophet ﷺ.

Politically, the description “حَصُورًا” (chaste, unmarried) is radical. In societies where political power is consolidated through marriage alliances, having a leader who doesn’t marry is free from those entanglements. He can’t be bought, influenced, or compromised through family connections. His chastity is political independence.

The fact that Allah named him “يَحْيَىٰ” - a name never given before - speaks to individual identity and uniqueness. Psychologically, each person has a unique role and identity given by Allah. You’re not merely repeating someone else’s path; your name, your mission, your life has unique dimensions that have never existed before.

Ayah 40

Zakariya’s (AS) response reveals the psychology of receiving good news that seems impossible. The natural human reaction is “How can this be?” This isn’t necessarily doubt - it’s the mind processing information that contradicts known reality. The ayah validates this human response. Prophets aren’t robots; they experience natural human reactions to extraordinary news.

His mention of old age and his wife’s barrenness shows vulnerability and honesty before Allah. Psychologically, authentic prayer includes acknowledging your reality, your limitations, your circumstances. He doesn’t pretend he’s not old; he names the obstacles. This teaches that being real with Allah, naming your struggles, is part of healthy spirituality.

Socially, barrenness in ancient (and many modern) societies carried stigma, especially for women. His wife “عَاقِرٌ” (barren) would have faced social judgment, perhaps blame, questioning of her worth. The fact that Allah chooses to bless her specifically, in old age, is social vindication. Allah honors those whom society has written off.

The response “كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” (Thus Allah does what He wills) is both theologically profound and psychologically liberating. It means: your circumstances, your age, your biology, your past - none of these limit Allah. Psychologically, this defeats fatalism and despair. People in impossible situations (barren, elderly, sick, poor, oppressed) are taught: Allah’s will is not bound by your circumstances.

Politically and socially, this principle challenges structures of power and oppression. When tyrants or systems seem immovable, when injustice seems permanent, “Allah does what He wills” means change is always possible. No status quo is permanent when Allah decides otherwise.

Ayah 41

Asking for a sign reveals the psychology of faith-seeking-understanding. Zakariya (AS) believes the promise but asks for something tangible to hold onto during the waiting period. This is healthy spiritual psychology - not doubt, but seeking means to strengthen patience and certainty. Humans need tangible reminders and confirmations; this is part of our psychological makeup.

The sign given - temporary speechlessness - is deeply symbolic psychologically. Speech is power, communication, social engagement. Being prevented from speaking to people forces internal focus. It’s a temporary retreat from social life into intensive spiritual life. Psychologically, this models the importance of periodic withdrawal from worldly speech to focus on divine remembrance.

The exception “إِلَّا رَمْزًا” (except through gestures) maintains necessary communication (he can still function) while limiting unnecessary speech. This teaches a principle applicable to all believers: reduce unnecessary speech, maximize necessary communication. Psychological research confirms that excessive talking depletes mental energy; focused, purposeful communication preserves it.

The command “وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا” (remember your Lord much) during this silent period teaches that external silence should be filled with internal dhikr. Psychologically, the human mind abhors vacuum - if you don’t fill silence with remembrance of Allah, it fills with anxiety, random thoughts, or worse. Dhikr is the psychological tool for maintaining peace during difficult or unusual circumstances.

“وَسَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ” (glorify in the evening and early morning) establishes routine and structure during the waiting period. Psychologically, humans need structure, especially during uncertain times. Having set times for worship creates rhythm and stability. The transitional times (dawn and dusk) are psychologically significant - moments of change, reflection, when humans naturally contemplate.

Socially, Zakariya’s (AS) silence would attract attention and questions. People would notice the leading imam can’t speak normally. This creates communal anticipation and makes the eventual birth an even more public miracle. Socially, this teaches that sometimes our struggles and unusual circumstances serve a greater purpose - they become testimonies that inspire others later.

Politically, a silent leader is a powerful image. In contexts where religious leaders use eloquent speech for manipulation, a leader who can’t speak but communicates through gesture and whose primary speech is dhikr represents purity of mission over rhetorical manipulation. True leadership doesn’t always require eloquence; sometimes it requires silence and letting actions speak.

Quranic Wisdom

Ayah 37

The spiritual lesson here is profound: complete dedication to Allah brings provision from unexpected sources, in unexpected ways. Maryam was dedicated to worship, set apart in the mihrab, and Allah provided for her miraculously. This teaches us that when we prioritize our relationship with Allah above worldly concerns, Allah takes responsibility for our worldly needs. “Whoever fears Allah, He will make a way out for him and provide for him from where he does not expect” (65:2-3).

Maryam’s response “هُوَ مِنْ عِندِ اللَّهِ” teaches us to attribute all blessings to Allah immediately, without hesitation. She doesn’t take credit, doesn’t create mystery or ambiguity. This is the wisdom of a heart that sees Allah’s hand in everything. For us: when we receive blessings, our first response should be to recognize Allah as the source, and to share that recognition with others.

Her addition “إِنَّ اللَّهَ يَرْزُقُ مَن يَشَاءُ بِغَيْرِ حِسَابٍ” teaches humility - she doesn’t claim to be special or uniquely blessed. Rather, she presents it as Allah’s general way. This is wisdom: when Allah blesses you, don’t see yourself as superior to others; recognize that Allah can bless anyone He wills, without limits.

The practical wisdom: when you see someone blessed, let it inspire you to ask Allah for your own needs, not to compare or despair. Zakariya (AS) saw Maryam’s provision and was moved to make his own du’a. This is the healthy spiritual response to witnessing others’ blessings.

Ayah 38

The wisdom of timing is evident: “هُنَالِكَ” (right then and there). When spiritual inspiration strikes, act on it immediately. Don’t delay making du’a when your heart is moved, when you’ve witnessed Allah’s power, when faith is high. These are windows of opportunity for answered prayer.

The wisdom of specificity in du’a: Zakariya (AS) didn’t make a vague du’a. He asked for specific offspring (ذُرِّيَّة), with a specific quality (طَيِّبَة - good/pure), from a specific source (مِن لَّدُنكَ - from Your special closeness). We learn to be specific in our requests to Allah. It’s not disrespectful; it’s showing you’ve thought about what you need and you’re bringing it to Allah with clarity.

The wisdom of combining du’a with affirmation of Allah’s attributes: “إِنَّكَ أَنتَ السَّمِيعُ الدُّعَاءِ” - you affirm that Allah hears. This strengthens your own conviction and demonstrates that you’re not asking out of doubt but out of trust. Ending du’a by mentioning Allah’s relevant attributes (The Hearing, The Provider, The Most Merciful, etc.) aligns your request with who Allah is.

The wisdom for parents: when you want children, want good children. Make du’a not just for biological offspring but for righteous offspring. Your legacy is not in numbers but in the quality of character and faith you leave behind.

The timeless wisdom: Allah hears all your prayers, even those that seem unanswered for years. Zakariya (AS) had been praying for a child for decades - likely from the time he was young and his wife was young. Now he’s old, she’s old, still hasn’t happened. But he didn’t lose faith that Allah heard. The hearing isn’t contingent on immediate answering. This teaches patience, persistence, and unwavering trust.

Ayah 39

The wisdom of prayer (salah) as the gateway to divine communication: angels spoke to him while he was standing in prayer. When you stand before Allah in salah, you’re opening a channel of communication. Allah responds to those who prioritize connection with Him. Practically: make your prayers meaningful, focus in them, because these are moments when heaven and earth connect.

The wisdom of confirmation and support (تَصْدِيق): Yahya’s primary role is to confirm ’Isa (AS). This teaches that supporting the truth, being the first to believe, being the one who backs up the message-bearer - this is a noble, honored position. You don’t have to be the main character to be significant. Abu Bakr (RA) earned the title “الصِّدِّيق” (the greatest confirmer) and that title elevated him. In our lives: be the one who confirms truth, who supports those carrying authentic messages, even if you’re not the spotlight.

The wisdom of chastity (حَصُور): Yahya’s abstinence from marriage wasn’t because marriage is bad - marriage is sunnah and blessed. Rather, for him specifically, staying unmarried was part of his unique mission. The wisdom: not everyone’s path is identical. Some people are called to unique forms of devotion that don’t fit standard templates. Respect individual paths that are sincerely dedicated to Allah, even if they differ from your own.

The wisdom of leadership through service (سَيِّد): being a سَيِّد (leader/master) is about taking responsibility, bearing burdens for others, serving your community. It’s not about status or authority over people. True leadership is servanthood. In our relationships, workplaces, communities: lead by serving, not by dominating.

The wisdom of lineage and legacy: “نَبِيًّا مِّنَ الصَّالِحِينَ” - a prophet from among the righteous. He continues a tradition. Your Islam should connect you to the righteous who came before - the Prophets (AS), the Sahaba (RA), the pious predecessors. You’re part of a lineage of faith. This gives you roots, identity, and models to emulate.

Ayah 40

The wisdom of honest questioning: Zakariya’s (AS) “أَنَّىٰ” (how?) teaches us that questioning how Allah’s promise will be fulfilled isn’t disbelief. It’s natural, human, acceptable. You can say “Ya Allah, I believe You can do this, but I don’t understand HOW, and I’m amazed.” This combines faith with wonder, trust with curiosity. Allah doesn’t condemn this; He answers it.

The wisdom of naming your obstacles: “قَدْ بَلَغَنِيَ الْكِبَرُ وَامْرَأَتِي عَاقِرٌ” - I’m old, my wife is barren. In du’a, it’s okay to tell Allah your problems explicitly. He knows them, but verbalizing them is part of processing and demonstrating your need. It’s therapeutic and spiritually necessary.

The ultimate wisdom: “كَذَٰلِكَ اللَّهُ يَفْعَلُ مَا يَشَاءُ” - This is how Allah does what He wills. Your impossible is not Allah’s impossible. No situation is beyond Allah’s power to change. For people facing infertility, chronic illness, financial despair, broken relationships, oppression - the wisdom is: Allah can do what He wills, regardless of your circumstances. Never lose hope.

This statement is also a call to surrender: sometimes you ask “how?” and Allah’s answer is simply “because I will it.” That answer should be enough. You may not understand the mechanism, the timeline, the wisdom behind it - but Allah’s will alone is sufficient reason. Spiritual maturity is accepting “Allah wills it” as complete explanation.

Ayah 41

The wisdom of asking for signs: it’s okay to ask Allah for tangible confirmation, for something to strengthen your faith during the waiting period. The believer seeks means to increase yaqeen (certainty). Allah gave Zakariya (AS) a sign; He gave Musa (AS) signs; He gives us signs constantly (in nature, in Qur’an, in our lives). Be attentive to signs, and don’t be shy to ask for them.

The wisdom of silence: the sign was temporary speechlessness toward people. This teaches the spiritual value of periodic silence. Too much speech scatters the heart, depletes spiritual energy, leads to mistakes (“Whoever believes in Allah and the Last Day should speak good or remain silent”). Intentional silence - not speaking unless necessary - is a form of worship and self-discipline.

The wisdom of gesture (رَمْز): you can communicate necessary things through minimal means. This teaches simplicity in communication. We often over-explain, over-talk, over-justify. Sometimes a gesture, a nod, a brief indication is enough. Minimize unnecessary words.

The profound wisdom of dhikr during difficulty: “وَاذْكُر رَّبَّكَ كَثِيرًا” - when you’re going through something unusual or difficult, increase remembrance of Allah. Dhikr is the soul’s nourishment. When worldly speech is cut off, spiritual speech should increase. Practically: in moments of stress, anxiety, waiting, uncertainty - engage in abundant dhikr. It stabilizes the heart.

“وَسَبِّحْ بِالْعَشِيِّ وَالْإِبْكَارِ” teaches consistency: glorify Allah in the evening and early morning. These transitional times of day are spiritually potent - moments between light and darkness, wakefulness and sleep. Establishing a routine of worship at these times anchors your day in remembrance of Allah. Practically: have a morning routine of dhikr/Qur’an, and an evening routine. This frames your day with Allah’s remembrance.

The ultimate wisdom across these ayahs: Allah responds to sincere devotion, accepts dedicated servants, provides miraculously when needed, and honors those who ask Him with trust and persistence. The human condition is one of need; the Divine response is one of generous giving. Our role is to ask, to worship, to remain patient, and to trust that Allah hears and will respond in the best way, at the best time.

Ayah 44–45 · linked video: 20. ’Ali ’Imran Ayah 44-45 Ramadan 2018

Root Word Analysis

Ayah 44

ذَٰلِكَ (dhalika) [ذ-ل-ك]

The demonstrative pronoun ذَٰلِكَ indicates something distant or significant, pointing to what is about to be revealed. It serves as a pause marker, drawing attention to the gravity of the information that follows.

أَنبَاءِ (anba’) [ن-ب-أ]

The root ن-ب-أ means to inform or bring news, particularly significant or weighty news. The word أَنبَاءِ (anba’) refers to news, events, or narratives of great importance. In this context, it emphasizes that what is being revealed is not ordinary information but carries prophetic weight. The singular form نَبَأ (naba’) is related to نُبُوَّة (nubuwwah) – prophethood – indicating that this is news that only comes through prophetic revelation.

الْغَيْبِ (al-ghayb) [غ-ي-ب]

The root غ-ي-ب means to be hidden, absent, or concealed. الْغَيْبِ refers to the unseen realm – matters beyond human perception and historical record. This includes not just events that happened in the past without witnesses, but also the inner states, private conversations, and intimate moments with Allah that no historian could ever document. The غَيْب also refers to information that has been deliberately hidden or kept away from people.

نُوحِيهَ (nuhihi) [و-ح-ي]

The root و-ح-ي means to inspire, reveal, or hint subtly. The word وَحْي (wahy) carries the meaning of subtle, quick communication – like a gesture or sign that conveys meaning without extensive explanation. نُوحِيهَ (we inspire it to you) suggests that Allah is not giving exhaustive historical accounts but rather dropping subtle hints and essential elements of the story. This is different from simply “sending down” (نُنَزِّل) or “informing” (نُخْبِر) – it emphasizes the intimate, subtle nature of prophetic revelation.

لَدَيْهِمْ (ladayhim) [ل-د-ي]

The root ل-د-ي indicates closeness, proximity, or presence. لَدَى means “at” or “with” in the sense of being in someone’s close company. لَدَيْهِمْ (with them) emphasizes that the Prophet ﷺ was not in their exclusive, intimate gatherings where private decisions were made. The word suggests not just physical presence but being part of an inner circle.

يُلْقُونَ (yulquna) [ل-ق-ي]

The root ل-ق-ي means to throw, cast, or encounter. يُلْقُونَ means “they were throwing” or “casting.” In this context, it refers to the physical act of throwing their pens as part of a decision-making ritual.

أَقْلَامَهُمْ (aqlamahum) [ق-ل-م]

The root ق-ل-م means to cut or trim, and قَلَم (qalam) is a pen or writing instrument. The plural أَقْلَامَهُمْ (their pens) refers to the sacred pens used by the religious scholars to write the Torah. These pens held special significance – they were not ordinary writing tools but instruments used to transcribe divine scripture, making them blessed objects. The scholars would write their names or options on these pens and cast them as a form of seeking divine guidance (similar to drawing lots).

يَكْفُلُ (yakfulu) [ك-ف-ل]

The root ك-ف-ل means to guarantee, sponsor, or take responsibility for someone. The word كَفَالَة (kafalah) means guardianship, sponsorship, or taking complete care of someone’s needs. يَكْفُلُ means “will take guardianship” or “will sponsor.” This implies full responsibility for Maryam’s upbringing, protection, and provision.

يَخْتَصِمُونَ (yakhtasimun) [خ-ص-م]

The root خ-ص-م means to dispute, argue, or engage in conflict. The word اختصام (ikhtisam) indicates intense disagreement or debate. يَخْتَصِمُونَ means “they were disputing” or “engaging in heated argument.” The form يَفْتَعِلُونَ (yafta’ilun) suggests reciprocal, intense action – they were arguing with each other, back and forth. This indicates the gravity and emotional intensity of the debate over who would take guardianship of Maryam.

Ayah 45

الْمَلَائِكَةُ (al-mala’ikah) [م-ل-ك]

The root م-ل-ك relates to kingship, dominion, and authority. مَلَك (malak – angel) literally means “one who possesses power” or “messenger.” Angels are created beings of light who carry out Allah’s commands without deviation. الْمَلَائِكَةُ refers to the angels in plural, though in this context it likely refers to the angel Jibreel (AS) speaking on behalf of others or using the royal plural.

يُبَشِّرُكِ (yubashshiruki) [ب-ش-ر]

The root ب-ش-ر means to bring good news, to announce joyful tidings. The word بُشْرَىٰ (bushra) means good news or glad tidings. يُبَشِّرُكِ uses the Form II pattern (تَفْعِيل) which indicates intensity and repetition – “He is giving you glad tidings,” “He is congratulating you,” “He is filling you with good news.” This is not merely informing Maryam of a fact; it is Allah Himself expressing joy and celebration about this miraculous birth. The intensive form emphasizes how blessed and honored this event is in Allah’s sight.

بِكَلِمَةٍ (bi-kalimatin) [ك-ل-م]

The root ك-ل-م means to speak, wound, or have an effect. كَلِمَة (kalimah) means a word, statement, or divine command. In the Quranic context, كَلِمَة refers to Allah’s creative command “كُن” (Be!). When Allah wills something into existence, He says “كُن فَيَكُونُ” (Be, and it is). Describing Isa (AS) as كَلِمَة from Allah emphasizes the miraculous, non-ordinary nature of his birth – he came into existence through Allah’s direct word without the normal biological process. This simultaneously affirms his miraculous origin while denying his divinity – he is the result of Allah’s word, not the Word itself made flesh.

الْمَسِيحُ (al-Masih) [م-س-ح]

The root م-س-ح means to wipe, anoint, or pass something over a surface. مَسَحَ means to anoint with oil. In Hebrew tradition, المَسِيحُ (al-Masih – the Messiah) referred to one who was anointed with sacred oil, originally applied to priests and later to kings appointed by Allah. The term carried connotations of being specially chosen, blessed, and appointed by Allah for a sacred mission. By the time of Isa (AS), it could mean “the anointed king,” “the chosen one,” or “the awaited deliverer.” Ironically, the Jews also used this term sarcastically to mock Isa (AS), suggesting he falsely claimed authority and kingship. The Quran reclaims this title and affirms it as his legitimate, Allah-given designation. The word المَسِيحُ connects to the original anointing ritual established through Musa (AS) and Harun (AS), linking Isa (AS) back to the family of Imran.

عِيسَى (Isa) [ع-ي-س]

The name عِيسَى is the Arabic form of the Hebrew Yehoshua/Yeshua (Jesus), which means “the one whom Allah saves” (from the root ي-ش-ع meaning salvation). The original Hebrew name was هوشع (Hoshea) meaning “salvation” itself, but Musa (AS) changed it to يهوشع (Yehoshua) meaning “the one whom Allah saves,” emphasizing that salvation belongs to Allah alone, not to any created being. The Quran uses عِيسَى instead of the Christian Arab form ياسوع (Yasu’), which sounds similar to the Arabic verb يَسُوع meaning “he perishes” or “is lost” (from the root س-و-ع). By reversing the letters (ع-ي-س instead of س-و-ع), Allah transforms the meaning from “one who perishes” to “one whom Allah saves,” affirming the prophetic meaning of the name. This is particularly significant given Allah’s later statement in the Quran: “وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ” (they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him).

ابْنُ (ibnu) [ب-ن-ي]

The root ب-ن-ي means to build or construct. ابْن (ibn) means “son” – literally “the one built from” someone, indicating biological descent. The placement of ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ at the end of the verse creates a dramatic build-up: first the glad tidings, then “a word from Allah,” then “the Messiah, Isa,” and finally – the shocking revelation – “son of Maryam.” Maryam is hearing that SHE will have a son, making the reality suddenly personal and immediate. The emphasis on “son of Maryam” (rather than “son of a father”) also subtly indicates the miraculous nature of his birth.

وَجِيهًا (wajihan) [و-ج-ه]

The root و-ج-ه means face, direction, or purpose. وَجِيه (wajih) means “one of high standing,” “honored,” “dignified,” “one who can show his face,” or “one who faces others without shame.” It describes someone whose head is held high, who is respected and noble, who has dignity before people and before Allah. The word وَجْه (face) is central here – someone who is وَجِيه is someone whose face shines with honor, not covered in shame or humiliation. This attribute takes on profound significance in light of the Christian narrative of Isa’s (AS) crucifixion – a death of public humiliation, mockery, torture, and shame. Allah is declaring that Isa (AS) will be وَجِيهًا – honored and dignified – directly contradicting the narrative of humiliating death. The Quran later confirms this: “وَمَا قَتَلُوهُ وَمَا صَلَبُوهُ وَلَٰكِن شُبِّهَ لَهُمْ” (they did not kill him, nor did they crucify him, but it was made to appear so to them). There is a powerful parallel with Musa (AS), who is also described as وَجِيهًا in the Quran: “وَكَانَ عِندَ اللَّهِ وَجِيهًا” (and he was honored in the sight of Allah) – after the Banu Israel caused him pain with false accusations, Allah declared his innocence and honor. Similarly, the Banu Israel accused Maryam and Isa (AS), but Allah declares his وَجِيه status.

الدُّنْيَا (al-dunya) [د-ن-و]

The root د-ن-و means to be near, low, or close. الدُّنْيَا means “the nearest” or “the lower” – referring to this worldly life, the temporal existence that is nearest to us now but lower in value compared to the Hereafter. This world is called الدُّنْيَا because it is close at hand and immediate, but also because it is the lower, lesser realm compared to the eternal Hereafter.

الْآخِرَةِ (al-akhirah) [أ-خ-ر]

The root أ-خ-ر means to delay, come after, or be last. الْآخِرَةِ means “the Hereafter,” “the Last Life,” or “the Final Abode” – the eternal life after death and resurrection. It is called الْآخِرَةِ because it comes after this life and is the final, ultimate reality.

الْمُقَرَّبِينَ (al-muqarrabin) [ق-ر-ب]

The root ق-ر-ب means to be near or close. The word قُرْب (qurb) means nearness or proximity. الْمُقَرَّبِينَ uses the Form II passive participle pattern (مُفَعَّل), meaning “those who have been brought near,” “those who have been given closeness,” or “those drawn close.” This term is used in the Quran to describe both angels and the highest rank of believers in Paradise. In Surah Al-Waqi’ah, Allah describes three categories: the foremost (السَّابِقُونَ السَّابِقُونَ), who are also called الْمُقَرَّبُونَ (those brought near). These are brought near to Allah physically in Jannah – they have the highest stations, the closest proximity to Allah’s presence. The use of this word for Isa (AS) indicates his exalted rank with Allah, his physical ascension and elevation to Allah’s presence (“بَل رَّفَعَهُ اللَّهُ إِلَيْهِ” – rather, Allah raised him to Himself), and his honored status among both the angels and the righteous. Angels are described as مُقَرَّبُونَ, and now Isa (AS) is described with the same attribute, suggesting his unique position among creation.

Grammatical & Rhetorical Nuances

Ayah 44

Opening with ذَٰلِكَ: Rhetorical Pause

The ayah opens with the demonstrative pronoun ذَٰلِكَ (that), which serves as an interjection – Allah pauses the narrative to make a meta-comment about what is being revealed. This is similar to a teacher stopping mid-lesson to say, “Pay attention, what’s coming next is especially important.” The use of ذَٰلِكَ creates distance and gravity, indicating that this information is weighty, significant, and removed from ordinary knowledge. This rhetorical device prepares the listener’s heart and mind for the momentous revelation about Isa (AS) that follows.

Construction of مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ

The phrase مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ (from the news of the unseen) uses the partitive مِن, indicating that what is being revealed is a portion or selection from the vast realm of the unseen. Allah is not giving an exhaustive historical account but rather selected elements, essential episodes, and key moments. The construct state (إضافة) أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ (news of the unseen) joins the concept of “news” with “the unseen,” emphasizing that this is not ordinary historical reporting but revelation of hidden realities.

نُوحِيهِ إِلَيْكَ: The Subtlety of Wahy

The verb نُوحِيهِ (We inspire it to you) is particularly significant. Allah could have used نُنَزِّلُهُ (We send it down) or نُخْبِرُكَ (We inform you), but instead He uses نُوحِي, which emphasizes subtle, intimate, quick communication. Wahy is not verbose or exhaustive – it is precise, essential, hinting at deeper realities. The attached pronoun ـهِ (it) refers back to أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ, and the preposition إِلَيْكَ (to you) emphasizes that this is directed specifically to the Prophet ﷺ, but also, by extension, it addresses those who claim to have knowledge of these events.

وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ: Emphatic Negation

The phrase وَمَا كُنتَ (and you were not) uses the emphatic negation مَا with the past tense verb كُنتَ, creating a strong denial: “You certainly were NOT.” The repetition of this structure (وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ… وَمَا كُنتَ لَدَيْهِمْ) emphasizes that the Prophet ﷺ had absolutely no access to this information through ordinary means. The use of لَدَيْهِمْ (in their close presence) rather than عِندَهُمْ (with them) emphasizes intimate proximity – these were exclusive, private gatherings, closed-door meetings where sacred decisions were made. The Prophet ﷺ was not just absent from the general vicinity; he was not in their inner circle, their private councils.

إِذْ يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَامَهُمْ: Present-Tense Vividness

The use of إِذْ (when) introduces a specific moment in time, and the present-tense verb يُلْقُونَ (they were throwing) brings the scene to life. In Arabic, the present tense can be used for past events to create vividness and immediacy – the reader is transported to that moment, seeing the scholars gathering, holding their sacred pens, marking them with names, and casting them to determine who would take guardianship of Maryam. This is not abstract history; it is a lived, visual scene.

أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ: Indirect Question

The phrase أَيُّهُمْ يَكْفُلُ مَرْيَمَ (which of them will take guardianship of Maryam) is an indirect question embedded in the narrative. It conveys the subject of their deliberation without directly quoting their speech. The interrogative أَيُّ (which) combined with the attached pronoun ـهُمْ (of them) creates the meaning “which one of them.” The present-tense verb يَكْفُلُ maintains the vividness of the scene.

إِذْ يَخْتَصِمُونَ: Second Temporal Clause

The second إِذْ introduces another moment: “when they were disputing.” The verb يَخْتَصِمُونَ uses the reciprocal form (يَفْتَعِلُونَ), indicating mutual, back-and-forth argumentation. This was not a calm discussion but an intense debate with strong opinions on both sides. Some scholars suggest this refers to the initial guardianship debate; others suggest it refers to later arguments about Maryam’s character when she returned with a child. The grammatical structure allows for both interpretations, and the Quran’s eloquence lies in this deliberate ambiguity – both moments of dispute are relevant to understanding Maryam’s story.

Ayah 45

إِذْ قَالَتِ الْمَلَائِكَةُ: Continuation Without وَإِذْ

Ayah 45 begins with إِذْ قَالَتِ الْمَلَائِكَةُ (when the angels said) without the coordinating conjunction وَ (and). This grammatical marker indicates that this is not a new, separate scene but a continuation of the same narrative begun in verse 42. The absence of وَ signals seamless narrative flow – we are still in the same moment, the same divine encounter with Maryam. This contrasts with verse 42, which began with وَإِذْ, marking a distinct narrative section. The grammar itself tells us this is the climax of that ongoing scene.

Vocative: يَا مَرْيَمُ

The angels address Maryam directly with the vocative particle يَا followed by her name: “O Maryam!” This creates intimacy and directness. The angels are not speaking about her but to her, engaging her personally in this momentous announcement.

إِنَّ اللَّهَ يُبَشِّرُكِ: Emphasis and Subject Fronting

The particle إِنَّ introduces emphasis: “Indeed, verily, certainly.” This is not a casual statement but a emphatic declaration. The subject اللَّهَ (Allah) is fronted for emphasis – it is ALLAH who is giving you this good news, not just the angels. The verb يُبَشِّرُكِ (He congratulates you) is in the present tense, suggesting ongoing, continuous action. Allah is not merely informing Maryam; He is expressing joy, celebration, and honor toward her. The attached pronoun ـكِ (you – feminine singular) makes this intensely personal.

بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ: Indefinite Noun with Attached Pronoun

The phrase بِكَلِمَةٍ مِّنْهُ (with a word from Him) uses an indefinite noun كَلِمَةٍ (a word) followed by the partitive مِّنْ and the attached pronoun ـهُ (Him). This structure creates a profound theological statement: the “word” is simultaneously indefinite (a word, one word among Allah’s infinite words) yet defined by its divine origin (from Him). The child to be born is the result of Allah’s creative word, not the Word itself incarnate. This subtly refutes Christian theology while affirming the miraculous nature of Isa’s (AS) birth.

اسْمُهُ الْمَسِيحُ عِيسَى ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ: Dramatic Build-Up

The structure of this phrase creates a powerful dramatic progression: 1. اسْمُهُ (his name) – we’re about to learn who this is 2. الْمَسِيحُ (the Messiah) – a title of honor and authority 3. عِيسَى (Isa) – his personal name 4. ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ (son of Maryam) – the shocking personal revelation

By placing ابْنُ مَرْيَمَ at the end, the Quran creates maximum impact. Maryam has been listening to all these descriptions – a word from Allah, the Messiah, Isa – and then suddenly, the reality hits: “son of MARYAM.” This is about HER. She will bear this child. The delayed revelation creates psychological realism – the information unfolds gradually, building to the moment of personal recognition.

وَجِيهًا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ: Balanced Structure

The phrase وَجِيهًا فِي الدُّنْيَا وَالْآخِرَةِ uses a balanced, symmetrical structure to describe Isa’s (AS) honor in both realms. The noun وَجِيهًا (honored) is placed first for emphasis, followed by the prepositional phrase فِي الدُّنْيَا (in this world) and then وَالْآخِرَةِ (and the Hereafter). The conjunction وَ links the two realms, indicating that his honor is comprehensive, spanning both temporal and eternal existence. This structure refutes the narrative of humiliation and death – if he were crucified and killed, how could he be وَجِيهًا in this world? The Quran declares his dignity and honor in both realms, necessitating a different understanding of his apparent fate.

وَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ: Partitive Construction

The phrase وَمِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ (and from those brought near) uses the partitive مِنَ, indicating membership in a category or group. Isa (AS) is not just مُقَرَّب (brought near) but مِنَ الْمُقَرَّبِينَ (from among those brought near) – he belongs to that exalted rank that includes angels and the foremost believers. The use of the plural الْمُقَرَّبِينَ suggests a community of the nearest ones to Allah, and Isa (AS) is among them. The definite article الْ indicates a known, established category in Islamic theology – the مُقَرَّبُونَ are the highest rank in Paradise, mentioned explicitly in Surah Al-Waqi’ah.

Historical & Biblical Context

Ayah 44

The Practice of Casting Lots in Jewish Tradition

The reference to يُلْقُونَ أَقْلَامَهُمْ (casting their pens) reflects an ancient practice among the Jewish religious leaders of seeking divine guidance through lots. According to classical mufassirun like Imam Razi, the scholars would take the pens with which they wrote the Torah – considered sacred instruments – and mark them with names or options. They would then cast these pens, and the one drawn would be considered Allah’s choice. This practice (similar to استخارة in concept, though different in form) was used when the scholars faced difficult decisions and couldn’t determine which option was closer to truth and righteousness.

Historically, this reflects earlier Biblical practices of casting lots (the Urim and Thummim used by Hebrew priests, or the lots cast for Jonah’s ship). However, by the time of Maryam, this practice had become formalized among the rabbinic class as a way of making sacred decisions about temple matters, custodianship, and religious duties.

Two Possible Historical Scenarios

There are two main scholarly interpretations of when this casting of lots occurred:

First Interpretation: The lots were cast when Maryam was first dedicated to the temple as an infant. Her mother had dedicated her for temple service, and the scholars had to decide who would take responsibility for raising and caring for this child. Multiple priests wanted the honor of raising this blessed child, so they cast lots, and Zakariya (AS) was chosen. This is the traditional Islamic interpretation and appears in most classical tafsir works.

Second Interpretation (Biblical Reconciliation): According to the Infancy Gospel of James and other Christian apocrypha, when Maryam reached a certain age (around 12-16 years old), she could no longer remain in the temple. The priests needed to find a guardian for her – someone who would take responsibility for her care and potentially marriage. They gathered and cast lots, and the lot fell to an older man named Joseph (Yusuf), who was already married with children. Joseph objected strenuously, saying he was too old to marry a young girl and the Israelites would mock him. This argument (يَخْتَصِمُونَ – they were disputing) is what the Quran may be referring to. Eventually, they agreed that Joseph would take guardianship without necessarily marrying her.

Scholar Gabriel Reynolds suggests the Quran may be referring to this second event rather than the first, which would explain the emphasis on يَخْتَصِمُونَ (intense argument). The first event (infant guardianship) would be a simpler matter with less controversy, while the second event (guardianship of a teenage girl) would involve significant debate and objection.

The Absence of the Prophet ﷺ: Addressing Christian and Jewish Claims

The emphasis that the Prophet ﷺ was not present during these events serves multiple purposes:

  1. Proving Prophethood: The Prophet ﷺ had no access to these private, closed-door deliberations of Jewish scholars. The details he reveals could only come through divine revelation, proving his prophethood.

  2. Addressing the People of the Book: The statement “you were not with them” extends beyond the Prophet ﷺ to address Jews and Christians who claim authority over these narratives. Allah is saying: none of you were there either. Your accounts have been mixed with error and concealment. Only I, Allah, was there, and only I can speak authoritatively about what truly happened.

  3. The Hidden Nature of Truth: The phrase مِنْ أَنبَاءِ الْغَيْبِ suggests that the true accounts of Maryam and Isa (AS) have become غَيْب (hidden/unseen) even though they are historical events. This is because:

    • Private moments (like Zakariya’s dua) were never witnessed
    • True accounts have been deliberately concealed by religious authorities
    • Corrupted versions have replaced the original narratives
    • Sacred texts and records are kept locked away in archives (like the Vatican) inaccessible to common people

Jewish historian Josephus (1st century CE) mentions that religious leaders kept certain scrolls and teachings restricted to the educated elite, hidden from the masses. Similarly, early Christian history shows that numerous gospels and accounts were suppressed or lost. Allah is revealing what has been hidden.

Connection to the Family of Imran

The very fact that lots were cast using pens that wrote the Torah connects this story back to the opening of the surah: آلُ عِمْرَانَ (the family of Imran).
